# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

> **Define a flip-flop. How does it differ from a latch?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **flip-flop** is a bistable sequential logic circuit that stores **one bit** of data and changes its output only at the moment of an active **clock edge** (rising or falling). It is the fundamental synchronous memory element in digital and automotive electronic systems.

**Key differences from a latch** (1 mark each):

| **Property** | **Latch** | **Flip-Flop** |
|---|---|---|
| Triggering | Level-sensitive (transparent when enable is HIGH) | Edge-sensitive (responds only at clock edge) |
| Timing behavior | Asynchronous / transparent | Synchronous / edge-triggered |
| Vulnerability to glitches | High (output follows input while enabled) | Low (output latches at clock edge only) |

Therefore, flip-flops are preferred in **automotive ECU designs** where noise immunity and deterministic timing are critical.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

> **List the four types of flip-flops studied in this experiment. State one application of each in an automotive context.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Flip-Flop Type** | **Automotive Application** |
|---|---|
| **SR (Set-Reset)** | Alarm system latch — Set when intrusion detected, Reset by driver acknowledgment. |
| **D (Data / Delay)** | Sensor data register — Latches wheel-speed sensor reading for ABS processing. |
| **JK (Jack-King)** | Toggle-based turn-signal flasher — J=K=1 toggles output at half-clock rate. |
| **T (Toggle)** | Frequency divider for CAN bus clock generation — divides crystal oscillator to required baud rate. |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme, Autotronics Lab]**

> **(a)** Draw the circuit diagram of a **D flip-flop** built from an SR flip-flop and an inverter. Write its characteristic equation and complete truth table. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Write an **Arduino sketch** to validate the D flip-flop by applying all four input combinations and printing the results to the Serial Monitor. Explain how the Arduino ensures deterministic timing. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — D Flip-Flop Construction and Theory (7 marks):**

**Circuit Construction (2 marks):**
A D flip-flop is constructed by cascading an inverter between the S and R inputs of an SR flip-flop:

```
    D ─────┬──────── S (of SR FF)
           │
        [INVERTER]
           │
           └──────── R (of SR FF)
```

This connection guarantees that **S and R are always complementary**, eliminating the invalid state of the SR flip-flop.

**Characteristic Equation Derivation (3 marks):**
Starting with the SR characteristic equation $Q^{+} = S + \overline{R} \cdot Q$ and substituting $S = D$, $R = \overline{D}$:

$$
Q^{+} = D + \overline{(\overline{D})} \cdot Q = D + D \cdot Q = D(1 + Q) = D
$$

Therefore: $Q^{+} = D$ — the next state always equals the D input at the active clock edge.

**Truth Table (2 marks):**

| **D** | **Clock Edge** | **Q<sup>+</sup>** | **Q̄<sup>+</sup>** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | ↑ | 0 | 1 | Load 0 |
| 1 | ↑ | 1 | 0 | Load 1 |

**Part (b) — Arduino Validation Sketch (7 marks):**

**Arduino Sketch (4 marks):**

```cpp
// D Flip-Flop Validation - Arduino Uno
int D_pin = 2;        // Data input to D FF
int CLK_pin = 3;      // Clock pulse output
int Q_pin = 8;        // Q output readback
int Qbar_pin = 9;     // Q-bar output readback

void setup() {
  pinMode(D_pin, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(CLK_pin, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(Q_pin, INPUT);
  pinMode(Qbar_pin, INPUT);
  Serial.begin(9600);
  Serial.println("D Flip-Flop Validation Test");
  Serial.println("D | CLK | Q | Qbar | Result");
  Serial.println("------------------------------");
}

void loop() {
  int testData[] = {0, 1};
  int numTests = sizeof(testData) / sizeof(testData[0]);
  int passCount = 0, failCount = 0;

  for (int i = 0; i < numTests; i++) {
    int D = testData[i];
    digitalWrite(D_pin, D);            // Apply data
    delayMicroseconds(10);             // Setup time margin
    digitalWrite(CLK_pin, HIGH);       // Rising clock edge
    delayMicroseconds(10);             // Pulse width
    digitalWrite(CLK_pin, LOW);

    int Q = digitalRead(Q_pin);
    int Qbar = digitalRead(Qbar_pin);
    int expected = D;
    String result = (Q == expected) ? "PASS" : "FAIL";

    Serial.print(D); Serial.print(" | ");
    Serial.print("^"); Serial.print(" | ");
    Serial.print(Q); Serial.print(" | ");
    Serial.print(Qbar); Serial.print(" | ");
    Serial.println(result);

    if (Q == expected) passCount++; else failCount++;
    delay(500);
  }

  Serial.print("Total PASS: "); Serial.println(passCount);
  Serial.print("Total FAIL: "); Serial.println(failCount);
  while (true);  // Halt after one cycle
}
```

**Deterministic Timing Explanation (3 marks):**

The Arduino ensures deterministic timing through three mechanisms:

1. **`delayMicroseconds(10)`** — Provides a precise **10 µs** delay between data application and clock edge. Since the flip-flop's setup time is on the order of nanoseconds, this 10 µs window offers a **1000× safety margin**.

2. **Sequential `digitalWrite()` calls** — Each instruction on the Arduino Uno executes in exactly **1 clock cycle (62.5 ns)** at 16 MHz, making the timing between events reproducible to the microsecond level.

3. **Fixed loop structure** — The `for` loop iterates through all test vectors in a deterministic order, ensuring identical test conditions across multiple runs — essential for **repeatability** in production-line testing.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme, Autotronics Lab]**

> **(a)** Explain the **JK flip-flop** with its circuit symbol, characteristic equation, and complete truth table. Show how it overcomes the invalid-state limitation of the SR flip-flop. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Design an **Arduino-based test procedure** to validate the **toggle mode** of a JK flip-flop. Include the expected timing diagram and the formula for the output frequency in terms of the input clock frequency. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — JK Flip-Flop Theory (7 marks):**

**Circuit Symbol and Construction (2 marks):**
The JK flip-flop is similar to the SR flip-flop but adds **clocked AND-gate feedback** from the outputs Q and Q̄ to the inputs. This feedback resolves the S=R=1 ambiguity by creating a **toggle** behavior instead of an invalid state.

**Characteristic Equation (3 marks):**

$$
Q^{+} = J\overline{Q} + \overline{K}Q
$$

**Complete Truth Table (2 marks):**

| **J** | **K** | **CLK** | **Q<sup>+</sup>** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | ↑ | Q | Hold (no change) |
| 0 | 1 | ↑ | 0 | Reset |
| 1 | 0 | ↑ | 1 | Set |
| 1 | 1 | ↑ | $\overline{Q}$ | Toggle |

**Resolution of Invalid State:** When J=K=1, the SR's "forbidden" state becomes the well-defined **toggle** mode — the JK flip-flop is therefore called a **universal flip-flop** because it can emulate SR, D, and T behavior.

**Part (b) — Toggle-Mode Arduino Test Procedure (7 marks):**

**Test Setup (2 marks):**
- Connect Arduino Pin 2 to both J and K inputs of the JK flip-flop (tie-high to VCC through a resistor for permanent toggle).
- Connect Arduino Pin 3 to the CLK input.
- Read Q from Pin 8 and display on Serial Monitor.

**Expected Timing Diagram (3 marks):**

| **Clock Cycle** | **CLK** | **J** | **K** | **Q (before)** | **Q (after edge)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ↑ | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| 2 | ↑ | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| 3 | ↑ | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| 4 | ↑ | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 |

**Output Frequency Formula (2 marks):**
Since the Q output completes one full cycle in **2 clock cycles**, the output frequency is exactly half the clock frequency:

$$
f_{out} = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{2}
$$

For an Arduino-generated clock at, say, 1 Hz (1-second period using `delay(500)` between HIGH and LOW), the Q output toggles at **0.5 Hz** — a clearly observable blink rate.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Flip-Flop Testing Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing latches with flip-flops** — A latch is level-triggered; a flip-flop is edge-triggered. Mixing these definitions costs **2 full marks** in definition-type questions.
> 2. **Forgetting the SR invalid state** — When writing the SR truth table, students often omit the `S=R=1 → INVALID` row. Always include it with a clear "forbidden" annotation.
> 3. **Wrong characteristic equation for JK** — The correct form is $Q^{+} = J\overline{Q} + \overline{K}Q$. Writing $Q^{+} = J + K$ or $Q^{+} = JK$ is incorrect and loses **3 marks**.
> 4. **Missing setup/hold time discussion** — In Arduino testing questions, students forget to mention that Arduino's microsecond-level delays automatically satisfy the flip-flop's nanosecond-level setup/hold requirements. This is a **frequently tested concept** worth **2 marks**.
> 5. **Incorrect toggle frequency formula** — The correct divider ratio for a single T flip-flop is $f_{out} = f_{clk}/2$. Forgetting the denominator of 2 is a common error.
> 6. **Not showing Serial Monitor output format** — In code-based questions, the examiner expects a *sample output*. Skipping this loses **1–2 marks**.
> 7. **Confusing active-high vs. active-low inputs** — SR flip-flops come in both variants (NAND-based with active-low inputs, NOR-based with active-high inputs). Always verify the IC's datasheet before wiring.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Flip-Flop Testing using Arduino:**

- [x] **Flip-flop = 1-bit memory element**, edge-triggered, fundamental sequential building block.
- [x] **Four types**: SR, D, JK, T — JK is the most versatile ("universal").
- [x] **SR invalid state** (`S=R=1`) is eliminated in JK flip-flop by **toggle behavior**.
- [x] **D FF characteristic equation**: $Q^{+} = D$ (simplest of all four).
- [x] **JK FF characteristic equation**: $Q^{+} = J\overline{Q} + \overline{K}Q$.
- [x] **T FF characteristic equation**: $Q^{+} = T \oplus Q$.
- [x] **Toggle divider formula**: $f_{out} = f_{clk}/2$ for one T FF; $f_{clk}/2^N$ for N cascaded.
- [x] **Arduino roles**: stimulus generator (outputs), response reader (inputs), timing controller (`delayMicroseconds`), data logger (Serial Monitor / LCD).
- [x] **Arduino Uno clock = 16 MHz → 62.5 ns per instruction cycle** — far slower than any FF's response time, guaranteeing correct capture.
- [x] **Standard logic levels** at 5 V: V<sub>IH</sub> ≥ 3.5 V, V<sub>IL</sub> ≤ 1.5 V.
- [x] **Setup time (t<sub>s</sub>) and Hold time (t<sub>h</sub>)** are automatically satisfied by Arduino's microsecond delays.
- [x] **Test methodology**: apply input → wait → generate clock edge → wait → read output → compare with expected → print PASS/FAIL.
- [x] **Automotive relevance**: flip-flops are used in ECU registers, ABS sensor latches, turn-signal flashers, CAN bus clock dividers, and alarm memory circuits.
- [x] **Hardware protection**: always use **current-limiting resistors (220 Ω)** on Arduino outputs driving FF inputs to prevent IC damage.
- [x] **Most common ICs used in lab**: 7474 (dual D), 7476 (dual JK), 74LS279 (quad SR latch).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Testing of diodes, transistors, op-amps, and relays in automotive electronic circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL607 - AUTOTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Testing of diodes, transistors, op-amps, and relays in automotive electronic circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔬 Module Experiment 2: Testing of Automotive Electronic Components

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1.1 Definition of Component Testing in Automotive Electronics

**Component Testing** in an automotive electronic circuit is the systematic process of verifying the electrical health, functional integrity, and operational specifications of discrete and active semiconductor devices using standardized instrumentation such as the **Digital Multimeter (DMM)**, **Cathode Ray Oscilloscope (CRO)**, **LCR meter**, and **automotive scan tools**. In KTU 2024 parlance, this experiment belongs to the *fault-diagnosis* taxonomy, where students learn to differentiate between a *healthy* and a *degraded/failed* component using measurable parameters.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Testing refers to the application of controlled electrical stimuli (voltage/current) to a component under test (CUT) and the observation/measurement of its electrical response against the manufacturer's datasheet specifications.

### 1.1.2 The Four Component Families Under Test

| # | Component | Symbol/Type | Primary Automotive Role | Intuitive Analogy |
|---|-----------|-------------|------------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Diode** | Passive, 2-terminal | Rectifier in alternator, freewheeling diode across relay coil | A **one-way check valve** for water — lets current flow only in one direction |
| 2 | **Transistor (BJT/MOSFET)** | Active, 3-terminal | Ignition coil driver, ECU switching, solenoid actuation | An **electronically controlled tap** — a small control current regulates a large main flow |
| 3 | **Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp)** | Active, multi-terminal IC | Sensor signal conditioning (MAP, MAF, oxygen sensor) | A **sensitive decision-maker** that compares two inputs and amplifies the difference |
| 4 | **Relay** | Electromechanical, 4–5 terminals | Headlamp switching, fuel pump control, starter motor | A **remote-controlled light switch** that uses a small current to switch a large current |

### 1.1.3 Real-World Automotive Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a modern car, there are approximately **80–100 electronic control units (ECUs)** and over **2,500 individual semiconductor devices**. A single failed diode in an alternator rectifier can drain the battery overnight, a shorted transistor in the ignition module can prevent engine start, a drifted op-amp in the ECU can cause erroneous sensor readings (e.g., a faulty air-fuel ratio), and a pitted relay contact can cause intermittent headlamp failure. Thus, **systematic component testing is a foundational automotive diagnostic skill**.

### 1.1.4 Standard Test Parameters & Constants

- **Silicon Diode Forward Voltage Drop (Vf):** $\mathbf{0.6 - 0.7\ V}$ at $\mathbf{25\ ^\circ C}$ (Negative Temperature Coefficient of $\mathbf{-2\ mV/^\circ C}$).
- **Germanium Diode Forward Voltage Drop (Vf):** $\mathbf{0.2 - 0.3\ V}$ (rare in modern automotive use).
- **Transistor Base-Emitter Voltage (VBE):** $\mathbf{0.6 - 0.7\ V}$ (NPN Si BJT in active region).
- **Op-Amp Ideal Parameters:** **Open-loop gain (AOL) → ∞**, **Input impedance (Zin) → ∞**, **Output impedance (Zout) → 0**, **Input offset voltage (Vio) → 0 V** (real op-amps have non-zero Vio, typically **±1 mV to ±5 mV**).
- **Automotive Relay Coil Resistance:** Typically **$\mathbf{50 - 120\ \Omega}$** for 12 V systems.
- **Battery Nominal Voltage (Automotive):** $\mathbf{12.6\ V}$ (full charge), $\mathbf{13.7 - 14.4\ V}$ (running/charging).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theory of Diode Testing

A **p-n junction diode** is a two-terminal semiconductor device that conducts current predominantly in one direction. The testing methodology validates the diode's ability to block reverse voltage and conduct forward current.

### 2.1.1 Why and How to Test a Diode

- **Why:** Diodes fail in three modes: (i) **Open circuit** (no conduction either way), (ii) **Short circuit** (conduction both ways), and (iii) **Leaky diode** (high reverse current). A faulty alternator diode, for example, will cause AC ripple in the DC bus and overheat the battery.
- **How:** Use the DMM's **diode-test mode** which injects a small constant current (typically $\mathbf{1\ mA}$ to $\mathbf{5\ mA}$) and measures the forward voltage drop.

### 2.1.2 Expected Diode Test Readings

| Test Condition | Healthy Si Diode | Shorted Diode | Open Diode | Leaky Diode |
|----------------|------------------|---------------|------------|-------------|
| Forward bias (Red lead → Anode, Black lead → Cathode) | $\mathbf{0.5 - 0.8\ V}$ | $\mathbf{0.000 - 0.1\ V}$ | **OL (Over Limit / Open)** | $\mathbf{0.4 - 0.5\ V}$ |
| Reverse bias (Red lead → Cathode, Black lead → Anode) | **OL** (no conduction) | $\mathbf{0.000 - 0.1\ V}$ | **OL** | **Non-OL reading, voltage drop seen** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Multimeter Polarity Convention:** In a standard DMM diode-test mode, the **RED probe is the positive terminal** of the internal test source, and the **BLACK probe is the negative terminal (COM)**. This is OPPOSITE to the resistance (Ω) mode of many analog meters.

### 2.1.3 Zener Diode Specifics

A Zener diode is tested by verifying its **Zener breakdown voltage (Vz)** using a variable DC power supply in series with a current-limiting resistor. The reverse voltage is increased until the diode enters breakdown and clamps at Vz.

$$V_{Z,\text{test}} = V_{S,\text{supply}} - I_{Z} \times R_{\text{series}}$$

Where:
- $V_{S,\text{supply}}$ is the applied supply voltage
- $I_{Z}$ is the Zener current
- $R_{\text{series}}$ is the protective current-limiting resistor

## 2.2 Theory of Transistor Testing

A **Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT)** is a three-terminal active device (Emitter, Base, Collector) that can operate in **cutoff, active, or saturation** regions. In automotive circuits, BJTs (and increasingly MOSFETs) act as low-side or high-side switches for solenoids, lamps, and relays.

### 2.2.1 The Two PN Junctions Inside a BJT

An **NPN BJT** contains:
- **Base-Emitter Junction (BE):** Forward-biased in active region (like a diode)
- **Base-Collector Junction (BC):** Reverse-biased in active region

A **PNP BJT** has both junctions with **opposite polarities**.

### 2.2.2 BJT Diode-Mode Test Method

A healthy BJT behaves as **two back-to-back diodes** when tested with a DMM in diode-test mode:

| Test Probes | NPN Healthy Reading | PNP Healthy Reading | Shorted |
|-------------|---------------------|---------------------|---------|
| Red on Base, Black on Emitter | $\mathbf{0.6 - 0.7\ V}$ | **OL** | $\mathbf{\approx 0\ V}$ |
| Red on Base, Black on Collector | $\mathbf{0.6 - 0.7\ V}$ | **OL** | $\mathbf{\approx 0\ V}$ |
| Red on Emitter, Black on Base | **OL** | $\mathbf{0.6 - 0.7\ V}$ | $\mathbf{\approx 0\ V}$ |
| Red on Collector, Black on Base | **OL** | $\mathbf{0.6 - 0.7\ V}$ | $\mathbf{\approx 0\ V}$ |
| Red on Emitter, Black on Collector | **OL** | **OL** | $\mathbf{\approx 0\ V}$ |
| Red on Collector, Black on Emitter | **OL** | **OL** | $\mathbf{\approx 0\ V}$ |

### 2.2.3 BJT Current Gain (hFE / β) Test

Modern DMMs have a dedicated **hFE socket** for NPN/PNP BJTs. The meter applies a small base current and measures the collector current:

$$h_{FE} = \frac{I_C}{I_B}$$

A healthy small-signal BJT (e.g., BC547) has **$\mathbf{h_{FE} = 100 - 800}$** depending on the device grade. Power BJTs (e.g., TIP31C used in automotive ignition) typically have **$\mathbf{h_{FE} = 10 - 50}$**.

## 2.3 Theory of Op-Amp Testing

The **Operational Amplifier (op-amp)** is a high-gain DC-coupled differential amplifier IC. Common automotive op-amps include **LM358, LM324, TL072, and MCP6002** used in sensor signal conditioning.

### 2.3.1 Key Op-Amp Parameters to Test

1. **Input Offset Voltage (Vio):** The differential DC voltage required at the inputs to make the output exactly **0 V**.
2. **Open-Loop Gain (AOL):** The gain with no feedback; should be **> 10,000 (80 dB)** for a typical LM741.
3. **Slew Rate (SR):** Maximum rate of change of output voltage; typical **$\mathbf{0.5\ V/\mu s}$** for LM741, **$\mathbf{13\ V/\mu s}$** for LM318.
4. **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR):** Ability to reject signals common to both inputs; typical **$\mathbf{70 - 100\ dB}$**.
5. **Supply Voltage Range:** Single supply (e.g., LM358: $\mathbf{3 - 32\ V}$) or dual supply (e.g., LM741: $\mathbf{\pm 5\ V}$ to $\mathbf{\pm 18\ V}$).

### 2.3.2 Closed-Loop Op-Amp Test Circuits

| Configuration | Gain Equation | Use Case |
|---------------|---------------|----------|
| **Inverting Amplifier** | $A_{CL} = -\frac{R_f}{R_{in}}$ | ECU signal inversion, summing amplifier |
| **Non-Inverting Amplifier** | $A_{CL} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_{in}}$ | MAP/MAF sensor signal boost |
| **Voltage Follower (Buffer)** | $A_{CL} = 1$ | High-impedance sensor isolation |
| **Comparator (open-loop)** | $A_{CL} = \text{saturated } \pm V_{sat}$ | Threshold detection (e.g., oxygen sensor) |

### 2.3.3 Op-Amp Offset Nulling Circuit

Real op-amps have a small Vio (typically **$\mathbf{\pm 1\ mV}$ to **$\mathbf{\pm 6\ mV}$** for LM741**). The datasheet specifies an **offset-null** procedure using a **10 kΩ potentiometer** between pins 1 and 5 (for an 8-pin DIP LM741) with the wiper tied to **V− (pin 4)**.

## 2.4 Theory of Relay Testing

An **automotive relay** is an electromechanical switch that uses a small control current in a coil to actuate a set of contacts capable of switching larger currents. Common automotive relay types include **SPST (5-pin)**, **SPDT (5-pin)**, and **DPDT**.

### 2.4.1 Relay Pinout & Operation

A standard 5-pin automotive relay (e.g., Bosch-type) has the following terminals:

| Pin | Designation | Function |
|-----|-------------|----------|
| **85** | Coil terminal 1 | One end of the electromagnetic coil |
| **86** | Coil terminal 2 | Other end of the coil |
| **30** | Common (COM) | Power input — usually $\mathbf{+12\ V}$ battery |
| **87** | Normally Open (NO) | Output when coil is energized |
| **87a** | Normally Closed (NC) | Output when coil is de-energized (SPDT only) |

### 2.4.2 Relay Test Methodology

**Step 1 — Coil Resistance Test (Ohmmeter):**
Measure resistance between pins 85 and 86. A healthy 12 V automotive relay should read **$\mathbf{50 - 120\ \Omega}$**.

$$R_{\text{coil}} = \frac{V_{\text{supply}}^2}{P_{\text{coil}}} = \frac{12^2}{1.5} \approx 96\ \Omega \quad \text{(typical)}$$

Where coil power dissipation is approximately **$\mathbf{1.5 - 2\ W}$**.

**Step 2 — Contact Continuity Test (Ohmmeter, Coil De-Energized):**
- Between **pin 30 and pin 87a**: Should read **$\mathbf{\approx 0\ \Omega}$** (NC contact closed)
- Between **pin 30 and pin 87**: Should read **OL (open)** (NO contact open)

**Step 3 — Energized Contact Test (Apply 12 V to coil):**
- Between **pin 30 and pin 87a**: Should now read **OL (open)**
- Between **pin 30 and pin 87**: Should now read **$\mathbf{\approx 0\ \Omega}$**

**Step 4 — Coil Current Test (Ammeter in series with coil):**
$$I_{\text{coil}} = \frac{V_{\text{supply}}}{R_{\text{coil}}} = \frac{12\ \text{V}}{80\ \Omega} = 150\ \text{mA} \quad \text{(typical)}$$

### 2.4.3 Common Relay Failure Modes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Burnt coil** (infinite resistance) → replace relay.
> - **Welded contacts** (always closed, even when de-energized) → major fire hazard; replace immediately.
> - **Pitted/oxidized contacts** (high resistance $\mathbf{> 1\ \Omega}$ when closed) → clean or replace.
> - **Weak spring tension** (chatter/intermittent operation) → replace.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---------------------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $V_f = \frac{kT}{q} \ln\left(\frac{I_f}{I_s} + 1\right)$ | Diode Shockley equation (forward bias) | $V_f$ in V, $I_f$ in A, $I_s$ in A |
| 2 | $kT/q \approx 25.85\ \text{mV}$ | Thermal voltage at 27 °C | Constant at room temperature |
| 3 | $h_{FE} = I_C / I_B$ | BJT DC current gain | Dimensionless, typical 100–800 |
| 4 | $A_{CL} = 1 + R_f / R_{in}$ | Non-inverting op-amp gain | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $A_{CL} = -R_f / R_{in}$ | Inverting op-amp gain | Dimensionless (negative sign) |
| 6 | $V_{out} = A_{OL} (V^+ - V^-)$ | Op-amp open-loop output | $A_{OL} \to \infty$ ideally |
| 7 | $R_{\text{coil}} = V_{\text{supply}}^2 / P_{\text{coil}}$ | Relay coil resistance | $\Omega$ |
| 8 | $I_{\text{coil}} = V_{\text{supply}} / R_{\text{coil}}$ | Relay coil energizing current | Amperes |
| 9 | $V_{Z,\text{test}} = V_S - I_Z \cdot R_{\text{series}}$ | Zener diode regulation voltage | Volts |
| 10 | $P_{\text{diss}} = V_{CE} \times I_C$ | Transistor power dissipation | Watts (thermal design) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked "How do you test a diode in an automotive circuit?", the examiner expects a 3-step answer: (1) Visual inspection, (2) DMM diode-mode test (forward & reverse), (3) In-circuit vs. out-of-circuit distinction. Out-of-circuit gives clean readings; in-circuit may be misleading due to parallel paths.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Diode Forward Voltage vs. Temperature

The **temperature coefficient** of a silicon diode's forward voltage is a critical parameter for automotive under-hood applications (ambient temperature can range from **$\mathbf{-40\ ^\circ C}$** to **$\mathbf{+125\ ^\circ C}$**).

### 3.1.1 Starting from the Shockley Equation

The forward current of a diode is governed by the Shockley diode equation:

$$I_f = I_s \left(e^{\frac{V_f}{n V_T}} - 1\right) \approx I_s \cdot e^{\frac{V_f}{n V_T}} \quad \text{when } V_f \gg V_T$$

Where:
- $I_s$ = Reverse saturation current (depends strongly on temperature)
- $V_T = kT/q$ = Thermal voltage (≈ 25.85 mV at 27 °C)
- $n$ = Ideality factor (≈ 1.5–2 for silicon, 1 for ideal)

### 3.1.2 Solving for $V_f$

Rearranging the equation to solve for the forward voltage:

$$e^{\frac{V_f}{n V_T}} = \frac{I_f}{I_s}$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\frac{V_f}{n V_T} = \ln\left(\frac{I_f}{I_s}\right)$$

Multiplying both sides by $n V_T$:

$$V_f = n \cdot V_T \cdot \ln\left(\frac{I_f}{I_s}\right)$$

Substituting $V_T = kT/q$:

$$V_f = n \cdot \frac{kT}{q} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{I_f}{I_s}\right)$$

### 3.1.3 Differentiating with Respect to Temperature

Taking the partial derivative $\partial V_f / \partial T$ at constant $I_f$:

$$\frac{\partial V_f}{\partial T} = \frac{nk}{q} \ln\left(\frac{I_f}{I_s}\right) + n \cdot \frac{kT}{q} \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial T}\left[\ln\left(\frac{I_f}{I_s}\right)\right]$$

For silicon, the second term dominates because $I_s$ doubles for every 10 °C rise. The empirical result is:

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial V_f}{\partial T} \approx -2\ \text{mV/}^\circ\text{C}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Engineering Insight:** This negative temperature coefficient is exploited in **bandgap voltage references** used in automotive ADCs and temperature sensors. Two diodes operated at different current densities produce a PTAT (Proportional-To-Absolute-Temperature) voltage.

## 3.2 Derivation: Non-Inverting Op-Amp Closed-Loop Gain

Consider a non-inverting amplifier with the non-inverting input (+) at $V_{in}$, the inverting input (−) connected to a voltage divider formed by $R_{in}$ (to ground) and $R_f$ (feedback from output).

### 3.2.1 Apply the Golden Rules of Ideal Op-Amp Analysis

**Rule 1:** No current flows into the op-amp inputs (since $Z_{in} \to \infty$).

**Rule 2:** The op-amp adjusts its output to make $V^+ = V^-$ (virtual short, valid only in negative feedback).

### 3.2.2 Apply Virtual Short

Since the non-inverting input is tied to $V_{in}$:

$$V^+ = V_{in}$$

By the virtual short principle:

$$V^- = V^+ = V_{in}$$

### 3.2.3 Apply KCL at the Inverting Node

Since no current enters the op-amp, the current through $R_{in}$ must equal the current through $R_f$:

$$\frac{V^- - 0}{R_{in}} = \frac{V_{out} - V^-}{R_f}$$

$$\frac{V_{in}}{R_{in}} = \frac{V_{out} - V_{in}}{R_f}$$

### 3.2.4 Cross-Multiply

$$V_{in} \cdot R_f = R_{in} \cdot (V_{out} - V_{in})$$

$$V_{in} \cdot R_f = R_{in} \cdot V_{out} - R_{in} \cdot V_{in}$$

### 3.2.5 Collect $V_{out}$ Terms

$$R_{in} \cdot V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot R_f + R_{in} \cdot V_{in}$$

$$R_{in} \cdot V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot (R_f + R_{in})$$

### 3.2.6 Solve for the Closed-Loop Gain

$$A_{CL} = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_{in}}$$

$$\boxed{A_{CL} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_{in}}}$$

### 3.2.7 Numerical Worked Example

Suppose a MAP (Manifold Absolute Pressure) sensor in an automotive ECU produces **$V_{in} = 0.5\ \text{V}$ to $\mathbf{4.5\ V}$**, and the ADC requires **$0\ \text{V}$ to $\mathbf{5\ V}$** with a small gain. Choose $R_{in} = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$ and $R_f = 1.1\ \text{k}\Omega$:

$$A_{CL} = 1 + \frac{1.1}{10} = 1.11$$

For $V_{in} = 4.5\ \text{V}$: $V_{out} = 1.11 \times 4.5 = 4.995\ \text{V} \approx 5\ \text{V}$ ✓

## 3.3 Derivation: Relay Pick-Up and Drop-Out Voltages

The relay has two critical voltage thresholds:

### 3.3.1 Pick-Up Voltage (Vpick)

The minimum coil voltage at which the contacts just begin to close. Determined by the spring force ($F_s$) and the magnetic force generated:

$$F_{\text{mag}} = \frac{(N \cdot I_{\text{coil}})^2 \cdot \mu_0 \cdot A}{2 \cdot g^2}$$

Where:
- $N$ = number of coil turns
- $I_{\text{coil}}$ = coil current
- $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m}$ = permeability of free space
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of the core
- $g$ = air-gap length

Setting $F_{\text{mag}} = F_s$ gives the minimum energizing current. For a 12 V automotive relay, $V_{\text{pick}} \approx \mathbf{7 - 8\ V}$.

### 3.3.2 Drop-Out Voltage (Vdrop)

The maximum coil voltage at which the contacts re-open. Typically **$\mathbf{1.5 - 3\ V}$** for a 12 V relay — much lower than Vpick due to magnetic hysteresis (residual magnetism in the iron core).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Component Test Summary Table

| Component | Test Equipment | Key Test | Healthy Reading | Common Failure |
|-----------|----------------|----------|-----------------|----------------|
| Silicon Diode | DMM (diode mode) | Forward Vf | 0.5–0.8 V | Shorted: < 0.1 V |
| Silicon Diode | DMM (diode mode) | Reverse | OL | Open: OL both ways |
| Zener Diode | DC supply + resistor | Breakdown Vz | Per datasheet | Drift in Vz |
| NPN BJT | DMM (diode mode) | BE & BC forward | 0.6–0.7 V | Open junction |
| NPN BJT | DMM (hFE mode) | Current gain | 100–800 | Low hFE |
| Op-Amp (LM358) | DMM + signal gen | Offset voltage | < ±5 mV | Saturated output |
| Op-Amp (LM358) | Oscilloscope | Slew rate | 0.3 V/μs | Distorted square wave |
| Relay Coil | DMM (Ω mode) | Coil R | 50–120 Ω | Infinite or 0 Ω |
| Relay Contacts | DMM (Ω mode) | NO contact | OL (de-energized) | Welded closed |
| Relay Operation | 12 V supply | Audible click | Click + continuity | No click (open coil) |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**With the help of a multimeter's diode-test mode, explain how you would determine whether a given diode is healthy, open, or shorted.**

**Model Answer:**

To test a diode using a DMM in diode-test mode, perform the following steps:

1. **Set the DMM to diode-test mode** (symbol: ▶︎|). The red probe becomes the positive terminal of the internal test source.

2. **Forward-bias test:** Connect the **red probe to the anode** and the **black probe to the cathode** of the diode. Observe the reading.
   - **Healthy silicon diode:** Reading is **$\mathbf{0.5 - 0.8\ V}$** (forward voltage drop).
   - **Shorted diode:** Reading is **$\mathbf{\approx 0\ V}$** (less than 0.1 V).
   - **Open diode:** Reading is **OL** (over limit / no conduction).

3. **Reverse-bias test:** Reverse the probes — **red to cathode, black to anode**.
   - **Healthy:** Reading is **OL** (diode blocks reverse current).
   - **Shorted:** Reading is **$\mathbf{\approx 0\ V}$** even in reverse.
   - **Open:** Reading is **OL** in both directions.

4. **Conclusion:** A diode is **healthy** if it shows **$\mathbf{0.5 - 0.8\ V}$** in forward bias and **OL** in reverse bias. Any deviation indicates a fault.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**List any six terminals/pins of a standard 5-pin automotive SPDT relay and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer:**

A standard 5-pin SPDT (Single Pole Double Throw) automotive relay has the following terminals:

| Pin | Designation | Function |
|-----|-------------|----------|
| **85** | Coil terminal 1 | One end of the electromagnetic coil |
| **86** | Coil terminal 2 | Other end of the coil; the coil is energized by applying 12 V across pins 85 and 86 |
| **30** | Common (COM) | The common input contact — typically connected to the +12 V battery |
| **87** | Normally Open (NO) | Connected to pin 30 only when the coil is energized |
| **87a** | Normally Closed (NC) | Connected to pin 30 when the coil is de-energized (default state) |

> When 12 V is applied across the coil (85–86), an electromagnetic field is generated which pulls the armature, breaking the 30–87a contact and closing the 30–87 contact.
