# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]
**Compare the architectural features of a microprocessor and a microcontroller. Why is a microcontroller preferred in embedded automotive applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Feature | Microprocessor (8085) | Microcontroller (8051) |
|---|---|---|
| Memory | External ROM, RAM | Internal ROM, RAM |
| Peripherals | External (I/O, timers, ADC) | Built-in (I/O, timers, UART) |
| Cost & Size | Higher | Lower, single-chip |
| Power consumption | Higher | Lower |
| Application | PC, laptop | ECU, washing machine, etc. |

A microcontroller is preferred in automotive ECUs because it integrates **CPU + memory + I/O + timers + ADC on a single chip**, giving **smaller PCB footprint**, **lower power**, **faster response** (no external bus delay), and **higher reliability** under engine-bay vibration and temperature.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]
**With a neat block diagram, explain the Harvard architecture used in microcontrollers. How does it differ from Von Neumann architecture?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
In **Harvard architecture**, the microcontroller has **separate program memory** and **data memory** with **separate address and data buses**. This allows **simultaneous instruction fetch and data access**, enabling **pipelined execution** and **faster throughput**.

In **Von Neumann architecture** (used in 8085/8086), a **single shared bus** carries both instructions and data sequentially, leading to the **Von Neumann bottleneck**.

**Block diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PROG[Program Memory ROM] -->|Separate bus| CPU[CPU]
    DATA[Data Memory RAM] -->|Separate bus| CPU
```

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme mandates a **Module Internal Choice**: Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the internal architecture of the **8051 microcontroller** with a neat block diagram. List any **six Special Function Registers (SFRs)** and their functions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write an **8051 Embedded C program** to generate a **1 kHz square wave** on pin **P1.0** using **Timer 0 in Mode 1**. Assume crystal frequency $f_{osc} = 11.0592$ MHz.

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

The **8051 microcontroller** is an 8-bit Harvard-architecture MCU by Intel (1980). Its internal block diagram (already shown in Section 4.2) contains:

1. **8-bit CPU** with Accumulator (A), B register, ALU, PSW, SP, PC (16-bit)
2. **4 KB on-chip ROM** (program memory) — 64 KB addressable externally
3. **128 bytes on-chip RAM** (data memory) + 128 bytes SFR area
4. **Four 8-bit I/O ports** — P0, P1, P2, P3
5. **Two 16-bit Timers/Counters** — T0, T1
6. **Full-duplex UART** for serial communication
7. **Interrupt controller** with 5 sources (INT0, INT1, T0, T1, TX/RX)

**Six Major SFRs:**

| SFR | Address | Function |
|---|---|---|
| ACC | E0H | Accumulator — primary ALU operand |
| B | F0H | B register — used in MUL and DIV |
| PSW | D0H | Program Status Word — carries flags (CY, AC, F0, RS1, RS0, OV, P) |
| SP | 81H | Stack Pointer — points to top of internal RAM stack |
| TMOD | 89H | Timer mode register — selects mode (0/1/2/3) and operation (timer/counter) |
| SCON | 98H | Serial Control Register — configures UART mode, enables reception |

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Compute machine cycle period:**

$$
T_{mc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} = 1.0851 \; \mu s
$$

**Step 2 — Compute timer counts for 500 µs half-period** (since full 1 kHz = 1000 µs, toggle every 500 µs):

$$
N = \frac{500 \; \mu s}{1.0851 \; \mu s} = 461
$$

**Step 3 — Compute timer reload value:**

$$
TH0:TL0 = 65{,}536 - 461 = 65{,}075 = 0xFE33
$$

Therefore $TH0 = 0xFE$, $TL0 = 0x33$.

**Step 4 — Embedded C Program (Keil µVision syntax):**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit WAVE = P1^0;        // Square wave output pin

void timer0_isr(void) interrupt 1
{
    TH0 = 0xFE;           // Reload high byte
    TL0 = 0x33;           // Reload low byte
    WAVE = ~WAVE;         // Toggle P1.0 every 500 us
}

void main(void)
{
    TMOD = 0x01;          // Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit), software control
    TH0  = 0xFE;          // Initial high byte
    TL0  = 0x33;          // Initial low byte
    ET0  = 1;             // Enable Timer 0 interrupt
    EA   = 1;             // Enable global interrupt
    TR0  = 1;             // Start Timer 0

    while(1);             // Idle loop, ISR handles toggling
}
```

**Output on P1.0:** Continuous 1 kHz square wave (500 µs HIGH, 500 µs LOW).

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Jun 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the **interfacing of an LED to the 8051 microcontroller**. Write the **Embedded C program** to **blink an LED** connected to pin **P1.0** with a **500 ms ON / 500 ms OFF** duty cycle. Use software delay loop.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **ADC interfacing (ADC0804)** with the **8051 microcontroller**. Describe the steps involved in reading a **temperature sensor (LM35)** output voltage and displaying the equivalent **temperature in °C** on a **16×2 LCD**.

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Circuit connection (already shown in Section 4.3):**

- $V_{CC}$ (+5 V) → **330 Ω resistor** → **LED anode**
- **LED cathode** → **P1.0** (MCU pin)
- LED is **active-LOW**: MCU pin LOW → current flows → LED ON.
- MCU pin HIGH → no current → LED OFF.

**Step 2 — Current calculation:**

$$
I_{LED} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_F}{R} = \frac{5 - 2}{330} = 9.09 \; mA
$$

This is well within the 8051's 20 mA sink capability.

**Step 3 — Software delay calculation:**

For 500 ms delay using nested loop in 8051 C (each inner iteration ≈ 8 µs including loop overhead):

$$
\text{Iterations required} = \frac{500 \times 10^{-3}}{8 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 62{,}500
$$

A nested loop with `i = 0; i < 250; j < 250` provides ~62500 iterations.

**Step 4 — Embedded C Program:**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit LED = P1^0;

void delay_ms(unsigned int ms)
{
    unsigned int i, j;
    for(i = 0; i < ms; i++)
    {
        for(j = 0; j < 250; j++);
    }
}

void main(void)
{
    LED = 0;                     // LED OFF initially (active LOW)
    while(1)
    {
        LED = 0;                 // LED ON (sink current)
        delay_ms(500);
        LED = 1;                 // LED OFF
        delay_ms(500);
    }
}
```

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**ADC0804 (8-bit Successive Approximation ADC):**

| Pin | Function |
|---|---|
| $V_{in+}$ | Analog input (0–5 V) |
| $V_{in-}$ | Ground |
| DB0–DB7 | 8-bit digital output |
| CS | Chip Select (active LOW) |
| RD | Read (active LOW) |
| WR | Write / Start of conversion (active LOW) |
| INTR | End-of-conversion interrupt (LOW when done) |
| CLK R, CLK IN | Internal clock (R=10 kΩ, C=150 pF → f ≈ 640 kHz) |
| $V_{ref}/2$ | Reference (2.5 V → input range 0–5 V) |

**Connection to 8051:**

- ADC0804 DB0–DB7 → 8051 **P0** (with 10 kΩ pull-ups)
- **CS** → P3.3, **RD** → P3.7 (or tied LOW if single device), **WR** → P3.6
- **INTR** → P3.2 (INT0) for end-of-conversion interrupt

**Step-by-step temperature reading algorithm:**

1. Apply **$V_{in} = 10 \; mV/°C \times T$** from LM35 to $V_{in+}$ of ADC0804.
2. Assert **CS = 0**, **WR = 0**, then **WR = 1** to start conversion.
3. Wait for **INTR = 0** (or use INT0 interrupt).
4. Assert **CS = 0**, **RD = 0** and read DB0–DB7 → digital count **D** (0–255).
5. Convert count to voltage:

$$
V_{in} = \frac{D}{256} \times V_{ref} = \frac{D}{256} \times 5.0 \; V
$$

6. Convert voltage to temperature (LM35 scale: 10 mV/°C):

$$
T(°C) = \frac{V_{in} \; (V)}{0.010 \; V/°C} = V_{in} \times 100
$$

7. Display on LCD using standard HD44780 4-bit interface (RS → P2.0, EN → P2.1, D4–D7 → P2.4–P2.7).

**Embedded C — ADC0804 Read Function:**

```c
unsigned char adc_read(void)
{
    unsigned char value;
    P3_3 = 0;                 // CS = 0
    P3_6 = 0;                 // WR = 0 (start conversion)
    P3_6 = 1;                 // WR = 1
    while(P3_2 == 1);         // Wait for INTR to go LOW
    P3_7 = 0;                 // RD = 0
    value = P0;               // Read digital byte
    P3_7 = 1;                 // RD = 1
    P3_3 = 1;                 // CS = 1
    return value;
}
```

**Conversion to temperature:**

```c
unsigned char count = adc_read();
float voltage = (count / 256.0) * 5.0;
int temperature = (int)(voltage * 100.0);   // in degrees C
lcd_print(temperature);                      // Display "27 C" etc.
```

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in KTU board exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to reload TH0 and TL0** inside the ISR — the timer only auto-reloads in **Mode 2**, not Mode 1. Marks deducted if reload missing.
> 2. **Confusing `MOV` and `MOVX`** — `MOV` accesses internal RAM/SFR; `MOVX` accesses external data memory using DPTR.
> 3. **Wrong baud rate crystal** — using 12 MHz instead of **11.0592 MHz** gives non-integer TH1 and UART framing errors.
> 4. **Ignoring SFR bit addresses** — bit-addressable SFRs (P0–P3, TCON, SCON, IE, IP, PSW, ACC, B) can be accessed bit-wise (`P1_0 = 1`), but only at addresses ending in 0 or 8.
> 5. **LED current calculation** — forgetting $V_F$ drop (typically 2 V for red LED) leads to wrong resistor value.
> 6. **ADC0804 vs ADC0808** confusion — 0804 is 1-channel with internal clock; 0808 is 8-channel multiplexer.
> 7. **Not enabling EA (global interrupt)** — interrupts silently fail if EA = 0.
> 8. **Stack overflow** — nested interrupts without increasing SP cause system crash; 8051 stack is only 128 bytes.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist — read 5 times before exam:**

- ☐ **Microprocessor = CPU only**; **Microcontroller = CPU + Memory + I/O + Peripherals**.
- ☐ 8051 has **4 KB ROM, 128 B RAM, 4 ports, 2 timers, 1 UART, 5 interrupts**.
- ☐ **Machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods** ($T_{mc} = 12/f_{osc}$).
- ☐ Timer **Mode 1 = 16-bit** (manual reload); **Mode 2 = 8-bit auto-reload**.
- ☐ UART baud rate uses **Timer 1 in Mode 2 with auto-reload** and crystal **11.0592 MHz**.
- ☐ Baud rate formula: $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \cdot \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12 \cdot (256 - TH1)}$.
- ☐ ADC resolution: $V_{LSB} = V_{ref} / (2^n - 1)$. For 10-bit @ 5 V: **4.89 mV/LSB**.
- ☐ LM35 output = **10 mV/°C**; LM34 = **10 mV/°F**.
- ☐ Port pins **sink 20 mA** but **source only 80 µA** (P0 is open-drain → needs pull-up).
- ☐ LED **active-LOW** configuration for 8051 (current sink from $V_{CC}$ through LED).
- ☐ Pull-up resistor for P0: typically **10 kΩ**.
- ☐ 8051 stack grows **upward** in internal RAM (default SP = 07H).
- ☐ **Interrupts** priority (default high → low): INT0 → T0 → INT1 → T1 → Serial.
- ☐ For OBD-II: **ISO 9141 = 10.4 kbaud K-line**; **ISO 14230 (KWP2000)**; **CAN = 500 kbps**.
- ☐ **CAN bus** uses differential pair CAN-H and CAN-L with 120 Ω termination at each end.
- ☐ **PWM frequency** for motor control should be **>20 kHz** to be inaudible.
- ☐ **Automotive MCUs** must be **AEC-Q100 qualified** for IC reliability under -40 °C to +125 °C.
- ☐ **Functional safety**: ISO 26262 ASIL-D requires lockstep cores (e.g., Infineon Aurix TC3xx).
- ☐ Arduino `analogWrite()` produces **490 Hz** on pins 9, 10, 11 (Timer1) and **980 Hz** on pins 5, 6 (Timer0).
- ☐ Always **debounce** mechanical switches in firmware (50 ms delay or interrupt-based Schmitt trigger).
- ☐ **Watchdog Timer (WDT)** must be enabled in safety-critical automotive firmware.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interfacing of AC/DC converter, stepper motor, and DC motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL607 - AUTOTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Interfacing of AC/DC converter, stepper motor, and DC motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔬 Module Experiment 2: Interfacing AC/DC Converter, Stepper Motor & DC Motor

## 1.1 Core Technical Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Autotronics Lab Definition (KTU 2024):** Autotronics is the integration of **electronics, microcontrollers, sensors, and actuators** in modern automotive and industrial systems. The lab experiment demonstrates the **hardware-level interfacing** of three fundamental power-electronic and electromechanical building blocks using a microcontroller.

### 1.1.1 AC/DC Converter (Rectifier Power Supply)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** An **AC/DC converter** is a power electronic circuit that converts alternating current (AC) mains supply into a regulated direct current (DC) voltage suitable for powering electronic circuits, microcontrollers, and actuator drivers. It typically consists of a *step-down transformer*, a *bridge rectifier*, a *smoothing filter capacitor*, and a *voltage regulator IC* (e.g., **LM7805**, **LM7812**).

**🔧 Conceptual Analogy:** Think of an AC/DC converter as a **water treatment plant for electricity**. The AC mains comes in "raw" (alternating direction 50 times/second in India), and the converter **straightens, filters, and regulates** it into clean, unidirectional DC water that sensitive electronic "drinking" circuits can safely consume.

### 1.1.2 Stepper Motor

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** A **stepper motor** is a brushless, synchronous electric motor that divides a full rotation into a number of equal **discrete steps**. It rotates in precise, fixed angular increments (commonly **1.8°/step** giving **200 steps/rev**) determined by its internal construction, without requiring position feedback sensors (open-loop control).

**🔧 Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a **clock's second hand** — it doesn't glide smoothly; it *jumps* in fixed, equal steps (one tick per second). A stepper motor behaves exactly like this for rotation, making it perfect for applications needing **exact positional control** such as printer paper-feed, CNC machines, and 3D printers.

### 1.1.3 DC Motor

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** A **DC motor** is an electromechanical energy-conversion device that converts DC electrical energy into mechanical rotational energy based on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**. Its speed is directly proportional to the applied armature voltage and inversely proportional to the magnetic flux.

**🔧 Conceptual Analogy:** A DC motor is like a **bicycle wheel with magnets on the spokes** and an electromagnet fixed to the frame — every time you push current through the electromagnet, the wheel rotates a bit. Push more current → spins faster. Reverse the current → spins the other way.

### 1.1.4 Interfacing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** **Interfacing** refers to the hardware and software methodology used to connect a peripheral device (actuator/sensor) to a microcontroller such that the controller can **command, monitor, and regulate** the peripheral via its GPIO pins, PWM outputs, or communication protocols (UART, I²C, SPI).

**🔧 Conceptual Analogy:** Interfacing is the **"translator"** between the microcontroller's low-current logic signals (e.g., 5 V, 20 mA) and the high-power actuators (e.g., 12 V, 1 A motor). Without an interface/driver, the microcontroller would either fail to drive the load or burn out instantly.

---

## 1.2 Functional Block Overview

The experiment combines **three subsystems** working under a single microcontroller:

| Subsystem | Role | Typical Driver IC |
|---|---|---|
| AC/DC Converter | Provides regulated +5 V / +12 V DC rail to entire circuit | LM7805, LM7812, Bridge Rectifier (W04M) |
| Stepper Motor Driver | Amplifies logic signals to energize motor coils | **ULN2003**, L293D |
| DC Motor Driver | Bidirectional control with PWM speed regulation | **L293D**, L298N, BTS7960 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Lab Hardware (KTU PCAUL607):** 8051 Microcontroller Development Board / Arduino Uno, 230 V/12 V step-down transformer, 1N4007 diodes, 1000 µF electrolytic capacitor, LM7805 regulator, **unipolar stepper motor** (usually 5-wire or 6-wire), permanent-magnet DC motor (e.g., 12 V BO type), ULN2003 driver, L293D motor driver IC, connecting wires, breadboard, CRO/multimeter.

---

## 1.3 Why This Experiment Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every modern automobile contains **dozens of stepper motors** (instrument cluster needles, idle-air control, headlight leveling) and **DC motors** (window lifts, wipers, fuel pump, radiator fan). The AC/DC converter is the foundational power-supply block that powers the entire ECU. Therefore, mastering this experiment gives you direct insight into **real automotive embedded systems**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 AC to DC Conversion — Stage-by-Stage Theory

The conversion from the 230 V / 50 Hz AC mains to a clean +5 V DC rail happens in **four distinct stages**:

### Stage 1: Step-Down Transformer

* Reduces **230 V AC** mains to a low-voltage AC (typically **9 V–12 V RMS**).
* Provides **galvanic isolation** between the high-voltage mains and the low-voltage electronics — critical for safety.
* Turns ratio determines the secondary voltage:

$$V_{sec(rms)} = V_{pri(rms)} \times \frac{N_2}{N_1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The transformer **does not rectify** — it only steps down. The waveform at the secondary is still AC, just lower in amplitude.

### Stage 2: Bridge Rectifier (Full-Wave)

* Four diodes (typically **1N4007**, rated 1 A / 1000 V) arranged in a **bridge (Graetz) topology** convert the bipolar AC sine wave into a **unidirectional pulsating DC**.
* During the positive half-cycle, diodes D1 and D2 conduct; during the negative half-cycle, D3 and D4 conduct. Both halves produce a **positive output**.
* Peak output voltage (after rectifier):

$$V_{peak} = V_{sec(rms)} \times \sqrt{2} - 2 \times V_D$$

where $V_D$ ≈ **0.7 V** is the forward voltage drop of a silicon diode.

> [!NOTE]
> Two diode drops (≈ **1.4 V**) are always subtracted because current flows through **two diodes in series** at any instant.

### Stage 3: Smoothing Capacitor (LC Filter)

* A large electrolytic capacitor (typically **1000 µF to 4700 µF**, rated at least 25 V) charges up to $V_{peak}$ and discharges when the rectified voltage dips between peaks.
* This **reduces the ripple** in the output, making it closer to a pure DC level.
* Peak-to-peak ripple voltage for a full-wave rectifier:

$$V_{ripple(pp)} = \frac{I_{load}}{2 \cdot f \cdot C}$$

where $f$ is the **ripple frequency = 2 × mains frequency = 100 Hz** in India.

### Stage 4: Voltage Regulation (LM7805)

* A three-terminal **linear voltage regulator** (LM7805) holds the output at a constant **+5 V DC** regardless of input variations or load changes.
* The regulator requires the input to be at least **2 V–3 V higher** than the output (dropout voltage) → so the filtered DC must be **≥ 7.5 V**.
* Maximum input voltage for LM7805 = **35 V**.

> [!NOTE]
> The **dropout voltage** of LM7805 is typically **2 V**, meaning $V_{in(min)} = 5 + 2 = 7$ V is required for proper regulation.

---

## 2.2 Stepper Motor — Detailed Theory

### 2.2.1 Construction

A **unipolar stepper motor** (most common in KTU labs) has:
* A **permanent-magnet rotor** with N and S poles.
* **Four stator windings** (sometimes with a center tap, giving 5 or 6 leads).
* The center tap connects to the **DC supply voltage**; the four coil ends are sequentially grounded (or driven HIGH) to energize one coil at a time.

### 2.2.2 Step Angle

The step angle depends on the number of poles $N_p$ on the rotor:

$$\theta_{step} = \frac{360°}{N_p}$$

For a standard 200-step-per-revolution motor:

$$\theta_{step} = \frac{360°}{200} = 1.8°/step$$

> [!NOTE]
> Common KTU lab motors are **200 steps/rev** (1.8°) or sometimes **48 steps/rev** (7.5°). Always read the motor label.

### 2.2.3 Excitation Modes

| Mode | Coils Energized | Step Size | Torque |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Wave (One-Phase-On)** | 1 of 4 at a time | 1.8° | Lowest |
| **Full-Step (Two-Phase-On)** | 2 adjacent coils | 1.8° | Highest |
| **Half-Step** | Alternating 1 then 2 | 0.9° | Medium, smoother |

### 2.2.4 Stepping Sequence (Full-Step, Two-Phase-On)

The four coils are labeled **A, B, C, D** (or 1, 2, 3, 4). Clockwise rotation:

| Step | A | B | C | D |
|------|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| 2 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| 3 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| 4 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| 5 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 (repeats) |

Reversing this sequence produces counter-clockwise rotation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **delay (step pulse interval)** between successive steps controls rotational speed. Typical delay = 5 ms to 50 ms. Lower delay → higher speed → lower torque.

$$\omega_{stepper} = \frac{\theta_{step}}{T_{step}} \quad \text{(rad/s)}$$

where $T_{step}$ is the time interval between steps in seconds.

### 2.2.5 ULN2003 Driver IC

* **Darlington transistor array** with 7 channels, each capable of sinking **500 mA** and tolerating up to **50 V**.
* The microcontroller outputs (5 V logic) connect to ULN2003 inputs; the ULN2003 outputs drive the four stepper coils.
* **Internal flyback (freewheeling) diodes** protect against the inductive kick from de-energizing coils.

---

## 2.3 DC Motor — Detailed Theory

### 2.3.1 Operating Principle

A permanent-magnet DC motor produces torque from the interaction between the armature current and the permanent magnetic field:

$$T = K_t \cdot I_a \quad \text{[N·m]}$$

where $K_t$ is the torque constant (N·m/A) and $I_a$ is the armature current.

The back-EMF generated opposes the applied voltage:

$$E_b = K_e \cdot \omega$$

Applied voltage equation (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around armature):

$$V = E_b + I_a \cdot R_a \quad \Rightarrow \quad V = K_e \cdot \omega + I_a \cdot R_a$$

### 2.3.2 Speed Control via PWM

> [!NOTE]
> **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** is the standard method to control DC motor speed. The motor sees an **average voltage** equal to the duty cycle times the supply voltage:

$$V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{supply}$$

where $D$ is the duty cycle (0 to 1). The motor's speed becomes:

$$\omega = \frac{V_{avg} - I_a R_a}{K_e}$$

### 2.3.3 Direction Control via H-Bridge

A **full H-bridge** (provided inside the L293D IC) uses **four transistor switches** arranged in an H pattern around the motor:

| IN1 | IN2 | Motor Action |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | Forward (clockwise) |
| 0 | 1 | Reverse (counter-clockwise) |
| 1 | 1 | Brake (dynamic) |
| 0 | 0 | Coast (free run) |

> [!WARNING]
> Never turn on both high-side and low-side switches on the same side simultaneously — this causes a **shoot-through short circuit** that destroys the IC instantly.

### 2.3.4 L293D Motor Driver IC

* **Dual H-bridge** — can drive **two DC motors** or **one stepper motor** (bipolar) or **one unipolar stepper with external transistors**.
* Logic supply: **5 V**, Motor supply: **4.5 V to 36 V**.
* Continuous current per channel: **600 mA**, peak: **1.2 A**.
* **Enable pin** allows PWM speed control when tied to a PWM output of the microcontroller.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examiner-favorite and frequently appear in ESE numerical/model questions.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Transformer secondary RMS | $V_{sec(rms)} = V_{pri(rms)} \times N_2/N_1$ | V, turns ratio is dimensionless |
| 2 | Peak rectified voltage | $V_{peak} = V_{sec(rms)} \cdot \sqrt{2} - 1.4$ | $V_D \approx 0.7$ V per diode |
| 3 | Full-wave ripple frequency | $f_{ripple} = 2 f_{mains}$ | = 100 Hz in India |
| 4 | Half-wave ripple frequency | $f_{ripple} = f_{mains}$ | = 50 Hz in India |
| 5 | Peak-to-peak ripple | $V_{ripple(pp)} = I_{load}/(2 f C)$ | Full-wave, C in farads |
| 6 | Regulator dropout | $V_{in(min)} = V_{out} + V_{dropout}$ | LM7805 dropout ≈ 2 V |
| 7 | Stepper step angle | $\theta_{step} = 360°/N_{steps}$ | $N_{steps}$ = steps per revolution |
| 8 | Stepper rotational speed | $N_{rpm} = (f_{step} \cdot 60)/N_{steps}$ | $f_{step}$ in steps/sec |
| 9 | Stepper angular speed | $\omega = \theta_{step} \cdot f_{step}$ | rad/s |
| 10 | DC motor torque | $T = K_t \cdot I_a$ | N·m |
| 11 | DC motor back-EMF | $E_b = K_e \cdot \omega$ | V |
| 12 | Armature voltage eqn | $V = K_e \omega + I_a R_a$ | Kirchhoff's KVL |
| 13 | No-load speed | $\omega_0 = V/K_e$ | $I_a \approx 0$ |
| 14 | Stall torque | $T_{stall} = K_t \cdot V/R_a$ | $\omega = 0$ |
| 15 | PWM average voltage | $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{supply}$ | $D$ = duty cycle (0 to 1) |
| 16 | PWM duty cycle % | $D_{\%} = (T_{on}/T_{period}) \times 100$ | Percent form |
| 17 | DC motor speed from PWM | $\omega = (D \cdot V_{sup} - I_a R_a)/K_e$ | rad/s |
| 18 | Mechanical power | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | Watts (T in N·m, ω in rad/s) |
| 19 | Electrical power input | $P_{in} = V \cdot I_a$ | Watts |
| 20 | Efficiency | $\eta = P_{mech}/P_{elec}$ | Unitless, 0–1 |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Peak & Average DC Output Voltage of a Full-Wave Bridge Rectifier with Smoothing Capacitor

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-Style):** A 230 V / 50 Hz AC mains is stepped down to 12 V RMS by a transformer. This 12 V AC feeds a bridge rectifier made of silicon diodes ($V_D = 0.7$ V). The output is filtered by a 1000 µF capacitor and feeds a load drawing 100 mA. Compute (a) peak DC output voltage, (b) ripple frequency, (c) peak-to-peak ripple voltage, (d) DC output voltage, (e) power delivered to load.

### Given Data
* $V_{pri(rms)} = 230$ V, $V_{sec(rms)} = 12$ V
* $f_{mains} = 50$ Hz
* $V_D = 0.7$ V (per diode)
* $C = 1000 \, \mu F = 1000 \times 10^{-6}$ F $= 10^{-3}$ F
* $I_{load} = 100 \, mA = 0.1$ A

### Step (a): Peak Output Voltage

$$V_{peak} = V_{sec(rms)} \times \sqrt{2} - 2 V_D$$

Substituting values:

$$V_{peak} = 12 \times 1.414 - 2 \times 0.7$$

$$V_{peak} = 16.968 - 1.4 = 15.568 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{peak} \approx 15.57 \text{ V}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor $\sqrt{2}$ converts RMS (root-mean-square) value to peak value because for a sine wave, $V_{peak} = V_{rms} \cdot \sqrt{2}$.

### Step (b): Ripple Frequency

For a **full-wave** bridge rectifier, both halves of the AC cycle are rectified, so the output pulses at **double** the mains frequency:

$$f_{ripple} = 2 \times f_{mains} = 2 \times 50 = 100 \text{ Hz}$$

$$\boxed{f_{ripple} = 100 \text{ Hz}}$$

### Step (c): Peak-to-Peak Ripple Voltage

$$V_{ripple(pp)} = \frac{I_{load}}{2 \cdot f_{ripple} \cdot C}$$

Substituting:

$$V_{ripple(pp)} = \frac{0.1}{2 \times 100 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$V_{ripple(pp)} = \frac{0.1}{0.2} = 0.5 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{ripple(pp)} = 0.5 \text{ V}}$$

### Step (d): DC Output Voltage

The DC level is the peak minus half the ripple (approximately):

$$V_{DC} = V_{peak} - \frac{V_{ripple(pp)}}{2} = 15.568 - 0.25$$

$$\boxed{V_{DC} \approx 15.32 \text{ V}}$$

### Step (e): Power Delivered to Load

$$P_{load} = V_{DC} \times I_{load} = 15.32 \times 0.1 = 1.532 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{load} \approx 1.53 \text{ W}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This **15.32 V** is the unregulated filtered DC that feeds the LM7805 regulator. The regulator drops it to a clean **+5 V** for the microcontroller.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stepper Motor Step Angle & Speed Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-Style):** A unipolar stepper motor has **200 steps per revolution**. The microcontroller generates step pulses every **5 ms**. Calculate (a) step angle, (b) rotational speed in RPM, (c) angular velocity, (d) time for one full revolution.

### Given Data
* $N_{steps} = 200$ steps/rev
* $T_{step} = 5 \, ms = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ s
* $f_{step} = 1/T_{step} = 1/(5 \times 10^{-3}) = 200$ steps/sec

### Step (a): Step Angle

$$\theta_{step} = \frac{360°}{N_{steps}} = \frac{360°}{200}$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{step} = 1.8°/\text{step}}$$

### Step (b): Rotational Speed in RPM

$$N_{rpm} = \frac{f_{step} \times 60}{N_{steps}} = \frac{200 \times 60}{200}$$

$$\boxed{N_{rpm} = 60 \text{ RPM}}$$

### Step (c): Angular Velocity

Convert RPM to rad/s:

$$\omega = N_{rpm} \times \frac{2\pi}{60} = 60 \times \frac{2\pi}{60}$$

$$\boxed{\omega = 2\pi \approx 6.283 \text{ rad/s}}$$

### Step (d): Time for One Revolution

$$T_{rev} = N_{steps} \times T_{step} = 200 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} = 1.0 \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{T_{rev} = 1 \text{ second}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** 60 RPM means 1 revolution per second → time per revolution = 1 s. Confirmed ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — DC Motor Speed and Direction Control via PWM

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-Style):** A 12 V permanent-magnet DC motor has $R_a = 2 \, \Omega$, $K_e = 0.05$ V·s/rad, and $K_t = 0.05$ N·m/A. The armature current is 1 A at a given operating point. (a) Calculate the back-EMF, (b) compute the no-load speed, (c) compute the stall torque, (d) if a 60% duty cycle PWM is applied to the L293D enable pin, find the new speed (assuming same current).

### Given Data
* $V_{supply} = 12$ V, $R_a = 2 \, \Omega$
* $K_e = 0.05$ V·s/rad, $K_t = 0.05$ N·m/A
* $I_a = 1$ A, $D = 0.60$ (60% duty cycle)

### Step (a): Back-EMF

From Kirchhoff's voltage law around the armature circuit:

$$V = E_b + I_a \cdot R_a$$

Rearranging:

$$E_b = V - I_a \cdot R_a = 12 - (1)(2) = 10 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{E_b = 10 \text{ V}}$$

And the corresponding angular velocity:

$$\omega = \frac{E_b}{K_e} = \frac{10}{0.05} = 200 \text{ rad/s}$$

### Step (b): No-Load Speed ($\omega_0$)

At no load, $I_a \approx 0$, so $E_b \approx V$:

$$\omega_0 = \frac{V}{K_e} = \frac{12}{0.05}$$

$$\boxed{\omega_0 = 240 \text{ rad/s} \approx 2292 \text{ RPM}}$$

### Step (c): Stall Torque

At stall, $\omega = 0$, so $E_b = 0$ and all voltage drops across $R_a$:

$$I_{stall} = \frac{V}{R_a} = \frac{12}{2} = 6 \text{ A}$$

$$T_{stall} = K_t \cdot I_{stall} = 0.05 \times 6$$

$$\boxed{T_{stall} = 0.3 \text{ N·m}}$$

### Step (d): Speed with 60% PWM

Average voltage applied to motor:

$$V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{supply} = 0.60 \times 12 = 7.2 \text{ V}$$

New back-EMF with same current:

$$E_b^{new} = V_{avg} - I_a \cdot R_a = 7.2 - (1)(2) = 5.2 \text{ V}$$

New angular speed:

$$\omega_{new} = \frac{E_b^{new}}{K_e} = \frac{5.2}{0.05}$$

$$\boxed{\omega_{new} = 104 \text{ rad/s} \approx 993 \text{ RPM}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** 60% duty cycle gives roughly 60% of the no-load speed (240 → 104 rad/s is ~43% — lower because of armature drop). Without armature drop, the speed would scale linearly with duty cycle.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary

| Parameter | AC/DC Converter | Stepper Motor | DC Motor |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Input** | 230 V AC, 50 Hz | Digital pulses | DC voltage (0–12 V) |
| **Output** | +5 V / +12 V regulated DC | Discrete angular steps | Continuous rotation |
| **Control** | Passive (transformer + regulator) | Open-loop (coil sequence) | Closed-loop possible (encoder) |
| **Driver IC** | LM7805, LM7812 | ULN2003 | L293D, L298N |
| **Speed Control** | Not applicable | Step pulse frequency | PWM duty cycle |
| **Direction Control** | Not applicable | Reverse coil sequence | H-bridge polarity |
| **Typical Use** | Power supply | Precise positioning | Continuous motion |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid Block Diagrams)

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Diagrams:** Since this is a hardware-interfacing lab, the diagrams below are **functional block-level schematics** showing signal/power flow. They complement (not replace) the physical circuit diagrams students draw in their lab records.

## 4.1 Overall Experiment Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ACMains[AC Mains 230V 50Hz] --> Transformer[Step Down Transformer 12V RMS]
    Transformer --> BridgeRect[Bridge Rectifier 4 x 1N4007]
    BridgeRect --> FilterCap[Smoothing Capacitor 1000uF]
    FilterCap --> Regulator[LM7805 Voltage Regulator]
    Regulator --> VCC5V[Regulated 5V DC Rail]
    VCC5V --> MCU[Microcontroller 8051 or Arduino]
    VCC5V --> DriverStepp[ULN2003 Stepper Driver]
    VCC5V --> DriverDC[L293D DC Motor Driver]
    MCU -->|GPIO 4 pins| DriverStepp
    MCU -->|PWM + DIR| DriverDC
    DriverStepp --> StepperMotor[Unipolar Stepper Motor 5V]
    DriverDC --> DCMotor[PMDC Motor 12V]
    StepperMotor --> MechLoad1[Mechanical Load Pointer]
    DCMotor --> MechLoad2[Mechanical Load Fan or Wheel]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The single AC mains source powers the entire system. The microcontroller receives a clean +5 V from the regulator, then issues *logic-level* control signals to the two driver ICs, which in turn deliver *high-current* drive to the respective motors.

---

## 4.2 AC/DC Converter Subsystem — Detailed Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Mains[230 V AC 50 Hz Mains] --> XFMR[Step Down Transformer N1 to N2]
    XFMR -->|12 V RMS AC| BR[Bridge Rectifier Full Wave]
    BR -->|Pulsating DC| CAP[Filter Capacitor C 1000uF 25V]
    CAP -->|Rippled DC approx 15V| REG[LM7805 Linear Regulator]
    REG -->|Regulated 5V DC| LOAD[Microcontroller and Logic]
    CAP -.->|Decoupling 0.1uF ceramic| REG
    REG -.->|Indicator LED 330 ohm| LED1[Power ON LED]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **0.1 µF ceramic decoupling capacitor** is placed close to the LM7805 input/output pins to suppress high-frequency switching noise from the microcontroller.

---

## 4.3 Stepper Motor Interfacing — Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MCUport1[GPIO P1.0] --> ULNin1[ULN2003 IN1]
    MCUport2[GPIO P1.1] --> ULNin2[ULN2003 IN2]
    MCUport3[GPIO P1.2] --> ULNin3[ULN2003 IN3]
    MCUport4[GPIO P1.3] --> ULNin4[ULN2003 IN4]
    ULNout1[ULN2003 OUT1] --> CoilA[Stator Coil A]
    ULNout2[ULN2003 OUT2] --> CoilB[Stator Coil B]
    ULNout3[ULN2003 OUT3] --> CoilC[Stator Coil C]
    ULNout4[ULN2003 OUT4] --> CoilD[Stator Coil D]
    Vsupply[12V DC External] --> CenterTap[Center Tap of Stator]
    CoilA --> Rotor[Permanent Magnet Rotor]
    CoilB --> Rotor
    CoilC --> Rotor
    CoilD --> Rotor
    Rotor --> Shaft[Output Shaft 1.8 degree steps]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** The microcontroller's GPIO pins can only source/sink **~20 mA**, but each stepper coil needs **~200–500 mA**. The ULN2003 acts as a current amplifier (Darlington pair, gain ≈ 1000).

---

## 4.4 DC Motor Interfacing — H-Bridge Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MCUdir1[GPIO PWM pin] --> EN1[L293D Enable 1A]
    MCUdir2[GPIO DIR pin1] --> IN1A[L293D Input 1A]
    MCUdir3[GPIO DIR pin2] --> IN2A[L293D Input 2A]
    EN1 --> HS1[High Side Switch H1]
    EN1 --> HS2[High Side Switch H2]
    IN1A --> HS1
    IN2A --> LS1[Low Side Switch L1]
    IN2A --> LS2[Low Side Switch L2]
    HS1 --> MotorPos[Motor Terminal Positive]
    HS2 --> MotorPos
    MotorPos --> DCMotor[PMDC Motor]
    DCMotor --> MotorNeg[Motor Terminal Negative]
    MotorNeg --> LS1
    MotorNeg --> LS2
    Vmotor[Motor Supply 12V] --> HS1
    Vmotor --> HS2
    Vlogic[5V Logic Supply] --> EN1
    Vlogic --> IN1A
    Vlogic --> IN2A
```

> [!WARNING]
> Notice the **Enable pin** controls *when* the motor gets power (used for PWM), and the **two input pins** control *direction* (which diagonal of the H-bridge conducts).

## 4.5 Software Control Flowchart — Microcontroller Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Power ON]) --> Init[Initialize GPIO and PWM]
    Init --> InitStep[Configure 4 pins for stepper]
    Init --> InitDC[Configure 2 pins and 1 PWM for DC motor]
    InitStep --> Seq1[Stepper: send sequence Step 1 1001]
    Seq1 --> Delay1[Delay 10 ms]
    Delay1 --> Seq2[Stepper: send sequence Step 2 0110]
    Seq2 --> Delay2[Delay 10 ms]
    Delay2 --> Seq3[Stepper: send sequence Step 3 1100]
    Seq3 --> Delay3[Delay 10 ms]
    Delay3 --> Seq4[Stepper: send sequence Step 4 1001 wait Step 4 is 0011]
    Seq4 --> LoopCheck1{Steps left greater than 0}
    LoopCheck1 -->|Yes| Seq1
    LoopCheck1 -->|No| DCStart[DC Motor Phase Begin]
    DCStart --> Forward[Set IN1 High IN2 Low]
    Forward --> PWM1[Set PWM 70 percent duty cycle]
    PWM1 --> DelayDC[Run for 5 seconds]
    DelayDC --> Reverse[Set IN1 Low IN2 High]
    Reverse --> PWM2[Set PWM 70 percent duty cycle]
    PWM2 --> DelayDC2[Run for 5 seconds]
    DelayDC2 --> Stop[Coast to stop]
    Stop --> End([Program Ends or Loops])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on flowchart correction:** The actual stepper sequence for 4 coils in full-step two-phase-on mode is the pattern 1100 → 0110 → 0011 → 1001 → repeat (see Section 2.2.4 for the precise table). The flowchart above shows the cyclic structure; refer to the truth table for exact bit patterns.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Style:** Part A questions test direct conceptual recall, definitions, and brief explanations. Answers should be 4–6 lines with a small diagram if needed.

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define an AC/DC converter. List its four main building blocks with the function of each.**

**Model Answer:**

> An **AC/DC converter** (also called a DC power supply) is an electronic circuit that converts 230 V, 50 Hz AC mains into a regulated DC voltage suitable for powering electronic circuits. Its **four main blocks** are:
> 1. **Step-Down Transformer:** Reduces 230 V AC to a lower AC voltage (e.g., 12 V) and provides isolation.
> 2. **Bridge Rectifier:** Converts AC to pulsating DC using four diodes.
> 3. **Smoothing Capacitor (Filter):** Reduces ripple and produces near-DC output.
> 4. **Voltage Regulator (LM7805):** Maintains a constant output (e.g., +5 V) regardless of input/load variations.
> *(3 marks — 1 for definition + 2 for four blocks with functions)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between a unipolar and a bipolar stepper motor. Which type requires an H-bridge driver?**

**Model Answer:**

> * **Unipolar stepper motor:** Has **five or six leads**, with a center-tapped winding. Current flows in only one direction through each coil. Driven by a simple transistor array (e.g., **ULN2003**).
> * **Bipolar stepper motor:** Has **four leads**, no center tap. Current must reverse direction through each coil to achieve full stepping. Requires an **H-bridge driver** (e.g., L293D or L298N) to reverse polarity.
> The bipolar type needs an H-bridge because each coil must be driven with both polarities of voltage. *(3 marks — 1.5 each for differences + 0.5 for naming driver)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Rule:** Each Part B question has an internal choice (either/or). You must answer **either** Q (A) **or** Q (B). Each carries **14 marks** split typically as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### 📝 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**
> **(a)** Draw the block diagram of a regulated DC power supply and explain the function of each block. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the interfacing of a **unipolar stepper motor** with an 8051 microcontroller using the **ULN2003** driver. Write the excitation sequence for full-step operation. **(7 marks)**

### 🔑 Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — DC Power Supply Block Diagram & Function:**

The four blocks and their functions are:

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| **Transformer** | Steps down 230 V AC to low-voltage AC (e.g., 12 V RMS). Provides isolation. |
| **Rectifier** | Converts AC to pulsating DC. Full-wave bridge is preferred over half-wave. |
| **Filter** | A large capacitor smooths the pulsating DC into nearly-pure DC. Reduces ripple. |
| **Regulator** | LM7805 holds output at constant +5 V regardless of input variations. |

*Typical full-wave bridge output calculation:*

$$V_{DC} = V_{sec(rms)} \times \sqrt{2} - 1.4 \approx 12 \times 1.414 - 1.4 = 15.57 \text{ V (unregulated)}$$

This 15.57 V is the **input to the LM7805**, whose output is **+5 V regulated**.

> *(7 marks: 2 for diagram + 3 for explanation + 2 for calculation showing practical understanding)*

**Part (b) — Stepper Motor Interfacing with ULN2003:**

* **Connection:** The four GPIO pins of 8051 (e.g., P1.0, P1.1, P1.2, P1.3) connect to the four input pins of ULN2003 (IN1–IN4). The four output pins of ULN2003 (OUT1–OUT4) connect to the four stator coils of the unipolar stepper. The center tap of the stepper is connected to **+12 V DC** (an external supply is usually needed because ULN2003 can sink but not source high current).

* **Excitation Sequence (Full-Step, Two-Phase-On):**

| Step | P1.0 (A) | P1.1 (B) | P1.2 (C) | P1.3 (D) | Hex |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0x03 |
| 2 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0x06 |
| 3 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0x0C |
| 4 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0x09 |
| 5 (repeat) | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0x03 |

* **8051 Pseudo-code:***
```c
unsigned char seq[] = {0x03, 0x06, 0x0C, 0x09};
void main() {
    while(1) {
        for(i = 0; i < 4; i++) {
            P1 = seq[i];
            delay_ms(10);   // controls speed
        }
    }
}
```

> *(7 marks: 2 for circuit description + 2 for sequence table + 1 for hex codes + 2 for program logic)*

---

### 📝 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**
> **(a)** Explain the **H-bridge** principle for DC motor direction control. With a truth table, show the four operating modes of the L293D driver. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A **12 V DC motor** has $R_a = 1.5 \, \Omega$ and $K_e = 0.04$ V·s/rad. At a load current of 2 A, calculate: (i) back-EMF, (ii) no-load speed, (iii) stall torque, (iv) the new speed if a **50% duty cycle PWM** is applied. **(7 marks)**

### 🔑 Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — H-Bridge Principle & L293D Truth Table:**

An H-bridge uses four switches (transistors) arranged in an 'H' shape with the motor forming the crossbar. By closing **diagonally opposite** switches, current flows through the motor in one direction (forward). By closing the **other diagonal**, current reverses (backward).

| IN1 | IN2 | EN | Motor Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 1 | **Forward** (clockwise rotation) |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | **Reverse** (counter-clockwise) |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | **Brake** (motor stops quickly) |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | **Coast** (motor free-wheels to stop) |
| X | X | 0 | **Disabled** (motor no current) |

> *(7 marks: 3 for H-bridge theory + 2 for truth table + 2 for explaining forward vs reverse mechanism)*

**Part (b) — DC Motor Numerical:**

**Given:** $V = 12$ V, $R_a = 1.5 \, \Omega$, $K_e = 0.04$ V·s/rad, $K_t = K_e = 0.04$ N·m/A, $I_a = 2$ A, $D = 0.50$.

**(i) Back-EMF:**

$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 12 - (2)(1.5) = 12 - 3 = 9 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{E_b = 9 \text{ V}}$$

**(ii) No-Load Speed:**

At no load, $I_a \approx 0$, so $E_b \approx V = 12$ V:

$$\omega_0 = \frac{V}{K_e} = \frac{12}{0.04} = 300 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\boxed{\omega_0 = 300 \text{ rad/s} \approx 2865 \text{ RPM}}$$

**(iii) Stall Torque:**

$$I_{stall} = \frac{V}{R_a} = \frac{12}{1.5} = 8 \text{ A}$$

$$T_{stall} = K_t \cdot I_{stall} = 0.04 \times 8$$

$$\boxed{T_{stall} = 0.32 \text{ N·m}}$$

**(iv) New Speed with 50% PWM:**

$$V_{avg} = D \cdot V = 0.50 \times 12 = 6 \text{ V}$$

$$E_b^{new} = V_{avg} - I_a R_a = 6 - (2)(1.5) = 3 \text{ V}$$

$$\omega_{new} = \frac{E_b^{new}}{K_e} = \frac{3}{0.04}$$

$$\boxed{\omega_{new} = 75 \text{ rad/s} \approx 716 \text{ RPM}}$$

> *(7 marks: 1.75 each for the four sub-parts, including correct formula identification, substitution, and final boxed answer with unit)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make — leading to direct mark loss in ESE:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the diode drop:** Students often write $V_{peak} = V_{sec(rms)} \times \sqrt{2}$ without subtracting $2V_D$ (≈ 1.4 V). This costs **1 mark** in numericals.
> 2. **Wrong ripple frequency formula:** For **half-wave** rectifier, $f_{ripple} = f_{mains} = 50$ Hz; for **full-wave** bridge, $f_{ripple} = 2 f_{mains} = 100$ Hz. Mixing these up is a frequent error.
> 3. **Confusing $\omega$ units:** Angular velocity $\omega$ is in **rad/s**, not RPM. To convert, multiply RPM by $2\pi/60$.
> 4. **Stepper sequence mistake:** Many students write wave-drive sequence (one coil at a time) when the question asks for full-step two-phase-on. Read the question carefully.
> 5. **H-bridge shoot-through:** Drawing a circuit where both transistors on the same side are ON simultaneously will cost full marks. Examiners look for correct diagonal switching.
> 6. **Not showing units in the final answer:** Always box the answer with its proper unit (V, A, rad/s, N·m, RPM).
> 7. **PWM applied to L293D VCC instead of EN pin:** PWM is fed to the **Enable** pin to control average voltage, not to the motor supply rail.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap — High-Density Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision sheet — covers every examiner-favorite concept for this experiment.**

### ✅ AC/DC Converter
* [ ] Four blocks: Transformer → Rectifier → Filter → Regulator.
* [ ] Transformer steps down voltage, does **not** rectify.
* [ ] Full-wave bridge uses 4 diodes; subtracts $2 V_D$ from peak.
* [ ] $V_{peak} = V_{rms} \cdot \sqrt{2} - 1.4$ V.
* [ ] Smoothing capacitor reduces ripple; ripple frequency for full-wave = **100 Hz**.
* [ ] LM7805 gives **+5 V regulated**; dropout ≈ 2 V; max input 35 V.
* [ ] Always add **0.1 µF decoupling** capacitor near regulator.

### ✅ Stepper Motor
* [ ] Step angle = $360°/N_{steps}$. Common value: **1.8°/step (200 steps/rev)**.
* [ ] Unipolar motor has center-tap → driven by **ULN2003** (sink-only driver).
* [ ] Bipolar motor has no center tap → needs **H-bridge (L293D)**.
* [ ] Three excitation modes: Wave, Full-step, Half-step.
* [ ] Speed controlled by **step pulse frequency**; direction by **sequence reversal**.
* [ ] ULN2003 has **7 Darlington channels**, 500 mA sink, internal flyback diodes.
* [ ] Speed in RPM: $N = (f_{step} \cdot 60)/N_{steps}$.

### ✅ DC Motor
* [ ] Torque $T = K_t I_a$; back-EMF $E_b = K_e \omega$; in SI units, $K_t = K_e$.
* [ ] Armature equation: $V = E_b + I_a R_a$.
* [ ] No-load speed: $\omega_0 = V/K_e$.
* [ ] Stall torque: $T_{stall} = K_t \cdot (V/R_a)$.
* [ ] Speed control: **PWM** on Enable pin of L293D.
* [ ] Direction control: H-bridge input logic (IN1, IN2).
* [ ] L293D drives **two DC motors**; 600 mA continuous, 1.2 A peak per channel.
* [ ] PWM average voltage: $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{supply}$.

### ✅ General Lab Tips
* [ ] Use **separate power supplies** for motor (high current) and logic (5 V) to avoid resets.
* [ ] Always include **flyback diodes** when driving inductive loads (ULN2003 has them built-in).
* [ ] The **enable pin** of L293D is used for PWM; do **not** feed PWM to IN1/IN2.
* [ ] Stepper and DC motor grounds must be **common** with the microcontroller.
* [ ] Add **snubber capacitors (0.1 µF)** across motor terminals to reduce EMI.
* [ ] **Current rating** of motor must be within driver IC's continuous current spec.

---

> 🎯 **End of Notes — Module Experiment 2: Interfacing of AC/DC Converter, Stepper Motor & DC Motor**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned) — Autotronics Lab (PCAUL607)*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advanced on-board diagnostics (OBD) and fault detection in electronic circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL607 - AUTOTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Advanced on-board diagnostics (OBD) and fault detection in electronic circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE EXPERIMENT 2: ADVANCED ON-BOARD DIAGNOSTICS (OBD) AND FAULT DETECTION IN ELECTRONIC CIRCUITS

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Advanced On-Board Diagnostics (OBD-II)** is a standardized automotive electronic system mandated by the **California Air Resources Board (CARB)** and adopted globally under **SAE J1962** and **ISO 15031** standards. It continuously monitors the performance of emission-related components, powertrain sensors, and actuators via the vehicle's **Engine Control Unit (ECU)**. When a parameter deviates beyond a calibrated threshold, the ECU stores a **Diagnostic Trouble Code (DTC)**, illuminates the **Malfunction Indicator Lamp (MIL)**, and freezes a snapshot of engine operating conditions (Freeze Frame) for technician retrieval.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme Autotronics Lab context, "Advanced OBD" extends beyond emission monitoring to include:
- **CAN/LIN bus fault tracing**
- **Sensor open-circuit / short-circuit detection**
- **Actuator driver fault management**
- **UDS (Unified Diagnostic Services — ISO 14229)**
- **OBD-II readiness monitors** and **permanent DTCs**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Vehicle Health Report Card" Analogy** 🩺
> Think of your car's electronic system as a **human body visiting a doctor**. The ECU is the **brain** that constantly records vital signs (oxygen levels in exhaust, engine temperature, air-fuel ratio). When any vital sign crosses a "danger zone" threshold (e.g., body temperature > 102°F), the brain flags it and stores a **medical record (DTC)**. The OBD-II port is the **blood-test lab report** that a mechanic can plug a scanner into to retrieve these records. Just as a doctor uses symptom codes to pinpoint illness, a technician uses standardized 5-character DTCs (like `P0301`) to find the faulty cylinder.

### 1.3 Core Standards & Physical Constants

| Standard / Constant | Value / Description |
|---|---|
| OBD-II Supply Voltage | **12 V DC** (nominal battery) |
| Logic HIGH (CAN-H) | **2.75 V – 4.50 V** |
| Logic LOW (CAN-L) | **0.50 V – 2.25 V** |
| CAN Recessive Voltage | **2.5 V** (both lines) |
| CAN Dominant Differential | **≥ 1.5 V** |
| K-Line Voltage (ISO 9141-2) | **12 V** (single-wire) |
| Standard Baud Rate (CAN) | **500 kbps** (powertrain) |
| DLC Connector Pins | **16 pins** (SAE J1962) |
| Number of standard PIDs | **Mode 01 PIDs 0x00 – 0xFF** |
| Memory retention of DTC | **Permanent (non-clearable)** for emissions DTCs |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Hot Point:** Examiners love asking about the **16-pin J1962 connector pinout**. Memorize pins **4 (Chassis Ground), 5 (Signal Ground), 6 (CAN-H), 14 (CAN-L), and 16 (+12V Battery)** — these appear every year.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — The Six Logical Layers

The OBD-II system operates through a six-tier logical stack, which KTU expects students to enumerate in part-(a) of 14-mark questions.

1. **Application Layer (ISO 14229 / UDS):** Defines diagnostic services — `0x19 ReadDTCInformation`, `0x14 ClearDTC`, `0x22 ReadDataByIdentifier`.
2. **Presentation Layer:** Handles data byte ordering (Intel/Motorola), byte padding, and negative response codes (`NRC 0x12 — SubFunctionNotSupported`).
3. **Network Layer (ISO 15765-2):** Segments long diagnostic messages (>8 bytes) into **ISO-TP** frames.
4. **Data Link Layer (ISO 11898 / CAN 2.0B):** Provides arbitration, bit-stuffing, and CRC-15 error detection.
5. **Physical Layer:** Differential signaling on **CAN-H (Pin 6)** and **CAN-L (Pin 14)** terminated by **120 Ω** resistors at each bus end.
6. **Sensor/Actuator Hardware Layer:** Hall sensors, lambda probes, MAF, MAP, crankshaft position, ignition coils, injectors.

## 2.2 Diagnostic Trouble Code (DTC) Decoding — The 5-Character Code

Every DTC follows the format `XYYYY`, where:

$$
\text{DTC} = \underbrace{X}_{\text{System}} \cdot \underbrace{Y_1 Y_2 Y_3 Y_4}_{\text{Fault Index (16-bit)}}
$$

- **X (1st character):** System identifier
  - `P` = Powertrain
  - `C` = Chassis
  - `B` = Body
  - `U` = Network/Communication
- **Y₁ (2nd character):** Code type
  - `0` = Generic (SAE)
  - `1` = Manufacturer-specific
  - `2/3` = Generic/Manufacturer (P only)
- **Y₂Y₃Y₄ (3rd–5th):** Specific fault index (0000 – FFFF hex)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Example — `P0301` decoded:**
> - `P` → Powertrain
> - `0` → SAE Generic
> - `301` → Cylinder #1 Misfire Detected

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| CAN Bit Time | $t_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{\text{Baud Rate}}$ | seconds | Bus timing analysis |
| CAN Bit Time Components | $t_{bit} = t_{SYNC} + t_{PROP} + t_{PS1} + t_{PS2}$ | seconds | STM32 / SJA1000 config |
| Nominal Bit Time @ 500 kbps | $t_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{500{,}000} = 2\,\mu s$ | μs | One bit per 2 μs |
| Differential Voltage | $V_{diff} = V_{CAN-H} - V_{CAN-L}$ | Volts | Dominant: $V_{diff} \ge 1.5\,V$ |
| Recessive Bus Voltage | $V_{rec} = \dfrac{V_{CAN-H} + V_{CAN-L}}{2} \approx 2.5\,V$ | Volts | Idle bus state |
| Termination Resistance | $R_{term} = 120\,\Omega$ (parallel) → effective $60\,\Omega$ | Ω | End-of-line resistors |
| OBD Voltage Drop Test | $V_{drop} \le 0.2\,V$ per connector | Volts | DLC pin health check |
| Scan Tool Current Draw | $I_{scan} \le 200\,mA$ | mA | ISO 15031-5 limit |
| DTC Hex-to-Decimal | $D = 16 \cdot Y_1 + Y_2$ (example) | — | Decode P0301 |
| Battery Voltage Range | $V_{bat} = 9\,V \text{ to } 16\,V$ (operational) | Volts | Cold-crank / load-dump |
| Lambda Sensor Voltage | $V_{\lambda} = 0.1\,V$ (lean) to $0.9\,V$ (rich) | Volts | Narrowband O₂ sensor |
| Wideband Lambda $I_p$ | $I_{pump} \propto (\lambda - 1)$ | mA | LSU 4.9 sensor |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Never confuse **CAN-H recessive voltage (2.5 V)** with **logic HIGH (~3.5 V)**. The bus is at 2.5 V when IDLE (recessive), and at 3.5 V / 1.5 V when a dominant bit is being transmitted. This is a favourite 3-mark question trap.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Emissions Compliance:** Mandatory under **Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI)** equivalent to Euro 6, enforced in India from 2020.
- **Predictive Maintenance:** Fleet operators (Ola, Uber, FedEx) use OBD-II telematics to predict failures 200–500 km before MIL lights up.
- **Insurance Telematics:** Pay-as-you-drive insurance reads VIN, speed, and DTCs via OBD dongles.
- **EV Battery Management:** Modern EVs repurpose OBD-II standards for HV battery diagnostics, replacing traditional emission DTCs with UDS services.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: CAN Bit Time Quantization

The CAN protocol divides each nominal bit into programmable time quanta. The following derivation links the bus baud rate to the **Baud Rate Prescaler (BRP)** used in microcontroller CAN peripherals.

### Given:
- Desired baud rate: $f_{baud} = 500 \text{ kbps}$
- APB1 clock frequency: $f_{clk} = 36 \text{ MHz}$ (typical STM32 setup)
- Time segment 1 (TS1) = 13 quanta
- Time segment 2 (TS2) = 2 quanta
- Synchronization jump width (SJW) = 1 quanta

### Step 1 — Express bit time in quanta

The total number of quanta per bit is:

$$
N_{q} = t_{SYNC} + t_{TS1} + t_{TS2}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute known time segment values

$$
N_{q} = 1 + 13 + 2 = 16 \text{ quanta/bit}
$$

### Step 3 — Express one quanta in terms of clock period

Each quanta is derived from the APB clock via the prescaler:

$$
t_{q} = \frac{\text{BRP} + 1}{f_{clk}}
$$

### Step 4 — Total bit time

$$
t_{bit} = N_{q} \cdot t_{q} = 16 \cdot \frac{\text{BRP} + 1}{36 \times 10^{6}}
$$

### Step 5 — Set bit time equal to reciprocal of baud rate

$$
\frac{1}{f_{baud}} = 16 \cdot \frac{\text{BRP} + 1}{36 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{500 \times 10^{3}} = 16 \cdot \frac{\text{BRP} + 1}{36 \times 10^{6}}
$$

### Step 6 — Solve for BRP

$$
2 \times 10^{-6} = \frac{16 \cdot (\text{BRP} + 1)}{36 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
\text{BRP} + 1 = \frac{2 \times 10^{-6} \times 36 \times 10^{6}}{16}
$$

$$
\text{BRP} + 1 = \frac{72}{16} = 4.5
$$

### Step 7 — Adjust sample point to standard 87.5%

Recompute with TS1 = 15, TS2 = 2 → $N_q = 18$. Then:

$$
\text{BRP} + 1 = \frac{72}{18} = 4
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{BRP} = 3}
$$

This is the canonical value for 500 kbps CAN on a 36 MHz APB bus.

## 3.2 Derivation: OBD-II DLC Pin 6 Voltage Under Fault Conditions

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU lab often asks: *"A scan tool reads `U0100` — Lost Communication with ECM. What voltages would you expect on pin 6 and pin 14?"*

### Step 1 — Healthy recessive bus

Both lines sit at 2.5 V:

$$
V_{CAN-H} = V_{CAN-L} = 2.5 \text{ V} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{diff} = 0 \text{ V}
$$

### Step 2 — Dominant bit (ECM actively transmitting)

$$
V_{CAN-H} \approx 3.5 \text{ V}, \quad V_{CAN-L} \approx 1.5 \text{ V}
$$

$$
V_{diff} = 3.5 - 1.5 = 2.0 \text{ V} \quad (\ge 1.5 \text{ V, valid dominant})
$$

### Step 3 — Open-circuit fault (broken CAN-H wire)

CAN-H floats; CAN-L remains pulled to 2.5 V via termination:

$$
V_{CAN-H} \approx 0 \text{ V to 1.0 V (floating drift)}, \quad V_{CAN-L} \approx 2.5 \text{ V}
$$

$$
V_{diff} \approx -1.5 \text{ V} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{ECU logs } U0100
$$

### Step 4 — Short-to-ground fault

If CAN-H shorts to ground:

$$
V_{CAN-H} = 0 \text{ V}, \quad V_{CAN-L} = 2.5 \text{ V} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{diff} = -2.5 \text{ V}
$$

The bus becomes **error-passive**, the MIL illuminates, and DTC `U0001` (High Speed CAN Communication Bus) is set.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common DTCs Encountered in KTU Lab Viva

| DTC | System | Probable Cause | Sensor/Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| `P0101` | Powertrain | MAF range/performance | Clean/replace MAF sensor |
| `P0128` | Powertrain | Coolant temp below thermostat regulating temp | Replace thermostat |
| `P0300` | Powertrain | Random/multiple cylinder misfire | Spark plugs, coils, injectors |
| `P0420` | Powertrain | Catalyst system efficiency below threshold | Check O₂ sensors, replace cat |
| `P0500` | Powertrain | VSS malfunction | Wheel speed sensor |
| `C0035` | Chassis | Front LH wheel speed sensor circuit | ABS sensor wiring |
| `B1318` | Body | Battery voltage low | Charging system test |
| `U0100` | Network | Lost comm with ECM/PCM | CAN bus diagnostics |
| `U0121` | Network | Lost comm with ABS control module | CAN-H/L integrity check |
| `U0140` | Network | Lost comm with body control module | Network resistance test |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 OBD-II System Block Architecture

The following block diagram maps the full signal flow from sensor inputs through the ECU to the diagnostic link connector.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SENSOR["SENSOR INPUT LAYER"]
        s1["MAF SENSOR"]
        s2["LAMBDA O2 SENSOR"]
        s3["MAP SENSOR"]
        s4["CRANKSHAFT POSITION"]
        s5["COOLANT TEMP"]
    end

    subgraph ECU["ENGINE CONTROL UNIT"]
        adc["ADC INPUT STAGE"]
        proc["MICROCONTROLLER CORE"]
        dtcMem["DTC MEMORY EEPROM"]
        mil["MIL DRIVER"]
    end

    subgraph ACTUATOR["ACTUATOR OUTPUT LAYER"]
        a1["INJECTOR DRIVERS"]
        a2["IGNITION COIL MODULE"]
        a3["IDLE AIR CONTROL"]
        a4["EVAP PURGE VALVE"]
    end

    subgraph CAN["CAN BUS NETWORK"]
        canH["CAN HIGH PIN 6"]
        canL["CAN LOW PIN 14"]
        term1["120 OHM TERMINATION ECM"]
        term2["120 OHM TERMINATION OTHER ECU"]
    end

    subgraph DLC["OBD II DLC J1962"]
        pin16["PIN 16 BATTERY 12V"]
        pin4["PIN 4 CHASSIS GND"]
        scan["SCAN TOOL"]
    end

    s1 --> adc
    s2 --> adc
    s3 --> adc
    s4 --> proc
    s5 --> adc
    adc --> proc
    proc --> dtcMem
    proc --> mil
    proc --> a1
    proc --> a2
    proc --> a3
    proc --> a4

    proc <--> canH
    proc <--> canL
    canH --- term1
    canH --- term2
    canL --- term1
    canL --- term2

    canH --> pin6["DLC PIN 6 CAN H"]
    canL --> pin14["DLC PIN 14 CAN L"]
    pin16 --> scan
    pin4 --> scan
```

## 4.2 Fault Detection Decision Flowchart

This flowchart illustrates the algorithm executed by the ECU each ignition cycle to detect, confirm, and store a fault.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start(["ECU POWER ON"])
    readSensors["READ ALL SENSOR INPUTS"]
    compare["COMPARE TO CALIBRATED THRESHOLD"]
    decision1{"VALUE OUT OF RANGE"}
    counter["INCREMENT FAULT COUNTER"]
    dec2{"COUNTER GE 2 TRIPS"}
    setPending["SET DTC AS PENDING"]
    nextCycle["WAIT NEXT DRIVE CYCLE"]
    dec3{"SAME FAULT IN 2ND CYCLE"}
    setConfirmed["SET DTC AS CONFIRMED"]
    milOn["ILLUMINATE MIL"]
    freeze["CAPTURE FREEZE FRAME"]
    dec4{"FAULT CLEARS IN 3 CYCLES"}
    erase["ERASE DTC"]
    ready["RESET READINESS MONITOR"]
    normal["CONTINUE NORMAL OPERATION"]

    start --> readSensors
    readSensors --> compare
    compare --> decision1
    decision1 -- NO --> normal
    decision1 -- YES --> counter
    counter --> dec2
    dec2 -- NO --> nextCycle
    nextCycle --> readSensors
    dec2 -- YES --> setPending
    setPending --> dec3
    dec3 -- NO --> normal
    dec3 -- YES --> setConfirmed
    setConfirmed --> milOn
    milOn --> freeze
    freeze --> dec4
    dec4 -- NO --> normal
    dec4 -- YES --> erase
    erase --> ready
    ready --> normal
    normal --> readSensors
```

## 4.3 OBD-II Connector (SAE J1962) Pinout Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TOPROW["TOP ROW PINS 1 TO 8"]
        p1["1 OEM DISCRETE"]
        p2["2 BUS POS SAE J1850"]
        p3["3 OEM DISCRETE"]
        p4["4 CHASSIS GROUND"]
        p5["5 SIGNAL GROUND"]
        p6["6 CAN HIGH ISO 15765"]
        p7["7 K LINE ISO 9141"]
        p8["8 OEM DISCRETE"]
    end

    subgraph BOTROW["BOTTOM ROW PINS 9 TO 16"]
        p9["9 OEM DISCRETE"]
        p10["10 BUS NEG SAE J1850"]
        p11["11 OEM DISCRETE"]
        p12["12 OEM DISCRETE"]
        p13["13 OEM DISCRETE"]
        p14["14 CAN LOW ISO 15765"]
        p15["15 L LINE ISO 9141"]
        p16["16 BATTERY POSITIVE 12V"]
    end
```

## 4.4 ISO-TP Diagnostic Message Segmentation Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant ECU as ENGINE ECU
    participant CAN as CAN BUS
    participant SCAN as SCAN TOOL

    SCAN->>ECU: SINGLE FRAME SF 0x09 0x02 19 02 FF
    Note over CAN: Short message fits in 8 bytes
    ECU->>SCAN: POSITIVE RESPONSE 19 02 ...

    SCAN->>ECU: FIRST FRAME FF 0x00 0x19 0x10 FF FF FF FF
    Note over CAN: Long message > 8 bytes
    ECU->>SCAN: FLOW CONTROL FC 0x30 0x00 0x00
    SCAN->>ECU: CONSECUTIVE FRAME CF 0x21 ...
    SCAN->>ECU: CONSECUTIVE FRAME CF 0x22 ...
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Diagnostic Trouble Code" (DTC) as used in OBD-II systems. Decode the DTC `P0420` indicating its system, code type, and fault nature.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Diagnostic Trouble Code (DTC) is a 5-character alphanumeric code generated by the ECU when an emission-related or powertrain parameter deviates from its calibrated range. It is stored in non-volatile memory and is retrievable through the OBD-II Data Link Connector (DLC) using a standardized scan tool.

Decoding `P0420`:
- **`P`** → Powertrain system (1 mark)
- **`0`** → SAE Generic (1 mark)
- **`420`** → Catalyst System Efficiency Below Threshold (Bank 1) (1 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write only "Catalyst faulty" without specifying *Bank 1* or *efficiency threshold*. Always mention **"Bank 1"** for full marks.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List the function of pins 4, 5, 6, 14, and 16 of the SAE J1962 OBD-II diagnostic connector.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Pin 4 — Chassis Ground** (0.6 mark)
- **Pin 5 — Signal Ground** (0.6 mark)
- **Pin 6 — CAN-High (ISO 15765-4)** (0.6 mark)
- **Pin 14 — CAN-Low (ISO 15765-4)** (0.6 mark)
- **Pin 16 — Battery Positive (+12 V directly from battery, unswitched)** (0.6 mark)

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Module Choice Questions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE rules, each 14-mark question has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. **Answer any ONE FULL question from the choice.**

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an OBD-II system. List the SAE standards that govern its physical, data-link, and application layers. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):** The OBD-II system consists of four major subsystems:
1. **Sensor Layer** — MAF, MAP, O₂, Crankshaft, Camshaft, Coolant Temp sensors feeding the ECU.
2. **ECU / PCM** — Performs closed-loop control, runs diagnostic algorithms every 100 ms, and stores DTCs in EEPROM.
3. **Actuator Layer** — Fuel injectors, ignition coils, EVAP purge valve, idle air control.
4. **Communication Layer** — CAN-H and CAN-L differential pair, terminated by 120 Ω resistors, accessible via the 16-pin J1962 DLC.

**Governing SAE / ISO Standards (4 marks):**
- **SAE J1962** — Defines the 16-pin DLC connector physical form
- **ISO 11898** — CAN physical and data-link layer (2.0B at 500 kbps)
- **ISO 15765-4** — Diagnostics on CAN (UDS transport + network layer)
- **ISO 14229** — Unified Diagnostic Services (UDS) application layer
- **ISO 15031-5** — Emissions-related diagnostic services
- **SAE J2012** — Standardized DTC and PID definitions

**(b)** A vehicle presents DTC `P0301` and the Malfunction Indicator Lamp (MIL) is ON. Describe the diagnostic procedure you would follow in the lab to isolate the root cause. Include voltage, resistance, and waveform tests. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Confirm and read freeze-frame data (1 mark):**
Connect scan tool to DLC (Pin 16 = 12 V, Pin 4 = GND, Pin 6/14 = CAN). Retrieve DTC and freeze frame. P0301 → Cylinder 1 Misfire. Note engine RPM, ECT, MAF, and short-term fuel trim at the moment of detection.

**Step 2 — Visual inspection of Cylinder 1 (1 mark):**
Check spark plug, ignition coil, injector connector, and wiring harness for chafing, oil fouling, or carbon tracking.

**Step 3 — Ignition coil test (1.5 marks):**
- Disconnect coil connector. Measure primary resistance: $R_{primary} = 0.4\,\Omega$ to $2.0\,\Omega$.
- Measure secondary resistance: $R_{secondary} = 5\,k\Omega$ to $15\,k\Omega$.
- If outside range → replace coil.

**Step 4 — Spark plug test (1 mark):**
Remove plug, inspect gap (typically **0.8 mm – 1.1 mm**), check for carbon or oil fouling. Replace if worn.

**Step 5 — Injector test (1.5 marks):**
- Measure injector winding resistance: $R_{injector} = 2\,\Omega$ to $16\,\Omega$ (peak-and-hold type).
- Use noid light or oscilloscope to verify injector pulse width.
- No pulse → ECU driver or wiring fault.

**Step 6 — Compression test (1 mark):**
Perform wet/dry compression test. If compression < 100 psi → mechanical fault (rings, valves).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where students lose marks:**
> - ❌ **Jumping to "replace the coil" without measurements** → zero for Step 3.
> - ❌ **Forgetting to check the freeze-frame ECT/RPM** → lose 1 mark in Step 1.
> - ❌ **Not mentioning injector resistance range** → lose 1.5 marks in Step 5.
> - ✅ Always state **"reproduce the fault under load"** before measurement for full viva credit.

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the CAN bus physical layer used in OBD-II. Derive the baud rate prescaler value for a 500 kbps signal on a 36 MHz APB clock with 18 time quanta per bit. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**CAN Physical Layer (4 marks):**
CAN (Controller Area Network) is a differential, multi-master, message-prioritized bus. The physical layer:
- Uses two wires: **CAN-High** (Pin 6) and **CAN-Low** (Pin 14).
- Dominant state: $V_{CAN-H} = 3.5\,V$, $V_{CAN-L} = 1.5\,V$ → $V_{diff} = 2.0\,V$.
- Recessive state: both lines at $2.5\,V$ → $V_{diff} = 0\,V$.
- Terminated at each end with $120\,\Omega$ resistors (parallel equivalent $60\,\Omega$).
- Maximum bus length at 500 kbps = **30 m** (per ISO 11898).

**Derivation (3 marks):**

$$
t_{bit} = \frac{1}{f_{baud}} = \frac{1}{500{,}000} = 2\,\mu s
$$

$$
t_{bit} = N_q \cdot t_q \quad \text{where} \quad t_q = \frac{\text{BRP} + 1}{f_{clk}}
$$

$$
2 \times 10^{-6} = 18 \cdot \frac{\text{BRP} + 1}{36 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
\text{BRP} + 1 = \frac{2 \times 10^{-6} \times 36 \times 10^{6}}{18} = 4
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{BRP} = 3}
$$

**(b)** Discuss the structure of a Diagnostic Trouble Code with examples. How are "generic" codes distinguished from "manufacturer-specific" codes? List any four P-codes with their meaning. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**DTC Structure (3 marks):** A DTC is a 5-character code: `X Y₁ Y₂ Y₃ Y₄`
- **X** = System letter — `P` (Powertrain), `C` (Chassis), `B` (Body), `U` (Network).
- **Y₁** = Code type — `0` (Generic SAE), `1` (Manufacturer specific), `2/3` (generic/manufacturer).
- **Y₂Y₃Y₄** = Specific fault index (0000 – FFFF hex).

**Generic vs Manufacturer (2 marks):**
- **Generic (Y₁ = 0):** Defined by SAE J2012; same meaning across all OEMs (e.g., `P0301` = Cyl 1 misfire for every car).
- **Manufacturer-specific (Y₁ = 1):** Reserved by each automaker; the same code can mean different faults in different brands (e.g., `P1336` = Ford IAC issue, but GM crank variation learn).

**Four P-codes (2 marks):**
- **`P0101`** — MAF sensor range/performance problem
- **`P0128`** — Coolant temp below thermostat regulating temperature
- **`P0300`** — Random/multiple cylinder misfire detected
- **`P0420`** — Catalyst system efficiency below threshold (Bank 1)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:**
> - ❌ Writing only 2 characters of the DTC (e.g., "P0") → lose 1 mark.
> - ❌ Saying `P0300` means "Cylinder 0 misfire" → **WRONG**. It means *random* misfire, not a specific cylinder.
> - ❌ Confusing "Body" with "Bus" — `B` = Body, `U` = Network/Communication.
> - ✅ Always state the **second character** explicitly to show code type.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **Save this checklist — KTU asks 60–70% of marks from these bullets.**

- [ ] OBD-II is mandated under **SAE J1962 / ISO 15031** and uses a **16-pin DLC**.
- [ ] DLC Pin **6 = CAN-H**, Pin **14 = CAN-L**, Pin **16 = +12 V**, Pin **4 = Chassis GND**, Pin **5 = Signal GND**.
- [ ] CAN bus idle (recessive) voltage is **2.5 V** on both lines; dominant is **3.5 V / 1.5 V**.
- [ ] CAN differential voltage in dominant state: $V_{diff} \ge 1.5\,V$.
- [ ] Termination resistors: **120 Ω at each end**, parallel equivalent = **60 Ω**.
- [ ] Standard powertrain CAN baud rate: **500 kbps** → bit time = **2 μs**.
- [ ] DTC format = **5 characters** (e.g., `P0301`): System + Code Type + Fault Index.
- [ ] **`P` = Powertrain**, **`C` = Chassis**, **`B` = Body**, **`U` = Network**.
- [ ] **`0` after letter = Generic SAE**, **`1` = Manufacturer-specific**.
- [ ] MIL illuminates only after the **same fault is confirmed in 2 consecutive drive cycles**.
- [ ] Freeze frame captures engine state at the moment of fault.
- [ ] `P0300` = Random misfire; `P0301` = Cylinder 1 misfire; `P0420` = Catalyst efficiency.
- [ ] `U0100` = Lost communication with ECM (CAN fault).
- [ ] ISO-TP uses **SF / FF / CF / FC** frames for messages > 8 bytes.
- [ ] Baud rate prescaler formula: $\text{BRP} = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{f_{baud} \cdot N_q} - 1$.
- [ ] Lambda sensor voltage: **0.1 V (lean) to 0.9 V (rich)** for narrowband sensors.
- [ ] KTU lab experiments test scan tool retrieval, CAN bus voltage measurement, and injector resistance.
- [ ] Always verify **Pin 16 = 12 V** and **Pin 4 = 0 V** before reading DTCs.
- [ ] A healthy bus shows **60 Ω** between Pin 6 and Pin 14 (ignition OFF).
- [ ] A shorted CAN-H to ground produces DTC `U0001` and disables the bus.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Familiarization of engine remapping tools and software
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL607 - AUTOTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Familiarization of engine remapping tools and software

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engine Remapping (ECU Tuning/Chip Tuning)** is the process of modifying the factory-calibrated software programmed into the **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)** of an internal combustion engine to alter performance characteristics such as power output, torque, fuel economy, throttle response, and emission behaviour.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context for AUTOTRONICS LAB, the familiarization exercise introduces students to:

- **Hardware remapping tools** (boot-mode and bench-mode programmers)
- **Software environments** (ECU map editors and calibration tools)
- **Communication protocols** between PC, interface hardware, and ECU
- **Map/table structures** inside the ECU firmware
- **Read/Write/Verify procedures** for non-volatile memory modification

> [!NOTE]
> The ECU in modern vehicles is essentially a **32-bit/64-bit microcontroller** running **Real-Time Operating System (RTOS)** firmware. Remapping involves locating **2D/3D calibration maps** in the binary flash memory and modifying their lookup values using specialised editors.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Think of your car's ECU as a **chef with a strict recipe book**:

| Concept | Real-World Analogy | Engineering Meaning |
|---------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Factory ECU Map** | Recipe printed in ink (cannot be erased) | Original calibration frozen in ROM/Flash |
| **Remapping Tool** | A pencil + a clean copy of the recipe | Hardware programmer + editor software |
| **Map Modification** | Changing "1 spoon salt" to "2 spoons" | Adjusting fuel/boost/ignition lookup tables |
| **Flash Memory** | Notebook the chef uses every day | ECU's EEPROM/Flash storage |
| **Dyno Tuning** | Customer tasting the dish and giving feedback | Real-world verification of new map |

Imagine you bought a sports sedan but the manufacturer, fearing warranty claims and emission regulations across **195 countries**, restricted the engine to deliver only **70%** of its true capacity. A remapping tool allows a tuner to "rewrite the chef's recipe" so the engine breathes deeper, injects optimally, and produces closer to its design potential — without changing any mechanical part.

## 1.3 Standard Industry Metrics

The following bold parameters are the **primary control variables** every remapping software manipulates:

- **Fuel Injection Quantity (mg/stroke)** — typically ranges from **5–80 mg/stroke** in diesel, **0.5–25 ms pulse width** in petrol
- **Boost Pressure (bar)** — stock **0.8–1.2 bar**, remapped up to **1.5–2.5 bar**
- **Injection Timing (° BTDC)** — between **-5° and +15° BTDC**
- **Ignition Advance (° BTDC)** — between **0° and 40° BTDC**
- **Lambda (λ)** — stoichiometric target is **1.000**, lean cruise **1.05–1.20**, power **0.78–0.85**
- **RPM Limiters (rev/min)** — typically **6500–8500 rpm** stock

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Remapping Works

A modern ECU operates on a **closed-loop feedback principle**. It consults pre-stored **lookup maps** indexed by sensor inputs:

$$ \text{Output}_{\text{actuator}} = f(\text{RPM}, \text{Load}, \text{Temp}, \text{MAF}, \text{Lambda}, \ldots) $$

**Closed-loop control loop:**

```
Sensors → ECU Microprocessor → Map Lookup → Actuator Drivers → Engine
   ↑                                                            ↓
   └──────────── Feedback (O2 / NOx / Lambda) ────────────────┘
```

The maps themselves are **multi-dimensional tables** stored as 16-bit or 32-bit integers. Examples:

- **Fuel Map (Main Injection)** — indexed by RPM × Pedal Position
- **Boost Map** — indexed by RPM × Gear/Load
- **Ignition Map** — indexed by RPM × MAP (Manifold Absolute Pressure)
- **Torque Limiter Map** — indexed by Gear × RPM
- **DPF (Diesel Particulate Filter) Regeneration Map**

## 2.2 Classification of Remapping Tools

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Differentiate between OBD remapping and Bench/Boot remapping."* — Memorise the table below.

| Tool Type | Access Method | ECU Opening Required | Risk Level | Example Tools |
|-----------|--------------|---------------------|------------|---------------|
| **OBD-II Port Remapping** | Plug into 16-pin diagnostic socket | ❌ No | **Low** | KESS V2, Autotuner, CMD, KTMflash |
| **Bench Mode Remapping** | Direct connection to ECU pins on bench | ✅ Yes (ECU removed) | **Medium** | KESS V2, K-TAG, Fgtech |
| **Boot Mode (BDM/JTAG)** | Microprocessor debug interface opened | ✅ Yes (case opened) | **High** | K-TAG, CMD, PCM Flash |
| **Chipping** | Soldering replacement EPROM chip | ✅ Yes (desoldering) | **High** | Older systems only |

## 2.3 Popular Engine Remapping Software — KTU Reference

| Software | Primary Use | Manufacturer | File Format |
|----------|-------------|--------------|-------------|
| **WinOLS** | Map editing & checksum correction | **EVC GmbH (Germany)** | `.bin`, `.hex` |
| **ECM Titanium** | Petrol/Diesel map editing | **Alientech** | `.bin`, `.ori`, `.mod` |
| **KESS V2 Master** | OBD read/write tool | **Alientech** | `.bin` |
| **K-TAG** | Boot/BDM programming | **Alientech** | `.bin` |
| **CMD Flash** | Multi-ECU programming | **CMD** | `.bin` |
| **Autotuner** | Modern OBD/Bench tool | **Autotuner** | `.bin` |
| **ECMware** | Toyota/Lexus calibration | **COBB Tuning** | `.cal` |
| **EcuTek** | Subaru/Nissan/BMW tuning | **EcuTek** | `.rom` |
| **HP Tuners** | GM/Ford/Chrysler US vehicles | **HP Tuners** | `.pcm`, `.ecu` |
| **Cobb Accessport** | Plug-in handheld tuner | **COBB** | `.ap` |

## 2.4 High-Yield Formula Sheet (Calibration Equations)

> [!NOTE]
> Although this is a lab course, ECU calibration is governed by **stoichiometry and thermophysics**. The following formulas are essential.

### 2.4.1 Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (λ = 1)

$$ \text{AFR}_{\text{stoich, petrol}} = 14.7 : 1 \quad\quad \text{AFR}_{\text{stoich, diesel}} = 14.5 : 1 $$

### 2.4.2 Lambda and Equivalence Ratio

$$ \lambda = \frac{(\text{A/F})_{\text{actual}}}{(\text{A/F})_{\text{stoich}}} \quad\quad \phi = \frac{1}{\lambda} $$

### 2.4.3 Mass Flow Rate through Engine

$$ \dot{m}_{\text{air}} = \eta_v \cdot \rho_{\text{air}} \cdot V_d \cdot \frac{N}{2} $$

Where: $\eta_v$ = volumetric efficiency, $\rho_{\text{air}}$ = air density, $V_d$ = displacement, $N$ = RPM

### 2.4.4 Required Fuel Mass (per cycle)

$$ m_{\text{fuel}} = \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{air}}}{(\text{A/F})_{\text{actual}}} \quad \text{(mg/stroke)} $$

### 2.4.5 Indicated Mean Effective Pressure (IMEP)

$$ \text{IMEP} = \frac{W_{\text{cycle}}}{V_d} = \frac{P_1 V_1 \left[ r^k (k-1) \right] }{ (r-1) V_d } $$

### 2.4.6 Boost Pressure Correction for Altitude

$$ P_{\text{boost, corr}} = P_{\text{boost, sea}} \cdot \left( \frac{101.325}{P_{\text{atm}}} \right) $$

### 2.4.7 Power Output Estimation

$$ P_{\text{brake}} = \frac{T_{\text{brake}} \cdot N}{9550} \quad \text{(kW, when T in Nm, N in rpm)} $$

### 2.4.8 Checksum Algorithm (CRC-16, used in ECU validation)

$$ \text{CRC}_{n+1} = \left( \text{CRC}_n \oplus \text{Byte}_{n} \right) \cdot 2 \quad (\text{mod } 0x10000) $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Whenever a map is modified, the ECU's checksum MUST be recalculated**; otherwise the ECU will refuse to start. WinOLS does this automatically.

## 2.5 Key Maps Modified During Remapping

| Map Name | Function | Stock Range | Remapped Range |
|----------|---------|------------|----------------|
