| **Injection Quantity (IQ)** | mg/stroke of fuel | Conservative | +10% to +30% |
| **Injection Timing** | Pilot/main timing | Lagging | Advanced by 2°–6° |
| **Boost Pressure Map** | Turbocharger target | Conservative | +0.2 to +0.6 bar |
| **Ignition Timing** | Spark advance | Conservative | +2° to +8° |
| **Torque Limiter** | Max torque cap | OEM cap | Raised by 15%–40% |
| **RPM Limiter** | Max engine speed | 6500–7500 rpm | 7000–8500 rpm |
| **Speed Limiter (Vmax)** | Max vehicle speed | 180–250 km/h | Removed/raised |
| **Throttle Response** | Pedal mapping | Linearised | Sharper curve |
| **DPF Regen Interval** | Soot burn-off cycle | 400 km | 600–800 km |
| **EGR Valve Map** | Exhaust gas recirculation | High recirculation | Reduced for power |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedure & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Standard Step-by-Step Procedure for Engine Remapping (KTU Lab Sequence)

> [!NOTE]
> The following procedure is the **canonical KTU lab exercise** expected for this experiment. Practise writing it in **passive voice, present tense** as required by KTU record-book conventions.

### Phase A — Pre-Remapping (Data Acquisition)

**Step 1: Vehicle/Ecu Identification**
$$\text{Identify} \rightarrow \{\text{VIN, ECU Part No., SW Version, HW Version, Engine Code}\}$$

**Step 2: Original ECU Read (Backup)**
$$\text{OBD/Bench Interface} \xrightarrow{\text{K-Line/CAN/UDS}} \text{ECU Flash Memory}$$

The tool requests the ECU for read access using **UDS protocol (Unified Diagnostic Services, ISO 14229)**:

$$\text{Tool} \rightarrow \text{RequestDownload (0x34)} \rightarrow \text{ECU} \rightarrow \text{Positive Response (0x74)}$$

**Step 3: Backup Verification**
$$\text{Calculate CRC}_{16} \text{ of original file} \rightarrow \text{Store as `.ori` backup}$$

### Phase B — Map Analysis & Modification

**Step 4: Open File in WinOLS / ECM Titanium**
- Load `.bin` file
- Auto-detect ECU type → reveals **map catalogue** (fuel, boost, ignition, torque limiters)

**Step 5: Locate Target Maps**
$$\text{2D Map Search} = f(\text{axis\_X (RPM)}, \text{axis\_Y (Load)})$$

**Step 6: Apply Modifications**

Mathematically, a **percentage increase** in fuel injection quantity:

$$IQ_{\text{new}}(i,j) = IQ_{\text{orig}}(i,j) \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\Delta\%}{100}\right)$$

For example, to increase injection quantity by **15%** across the entire map:

$$\begin{aligned}
IQ_{\text{new}}(i,j) &= IQ_{\text{orig}}(i,j) \times 1.15 \\
&= IQ_{\text{orig}}(i,j) \times \frac{115}{100} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7: Recalculate Checksum**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CRC}_{\text{calc}} &= \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \text{Byte}_k \cdot 2^{k} \pmod{2^{16}} \\
\text{CRC}_{\text{new}} &= \text{CRC}_{\text{calc}} \quad \text{(written back into checksum location)}
\end{aligned}$$

### Phase C — Writing & Verification

**Step 8: Write Modified File to ECU**

$$\text{Tool} \rightarrow \text{TransferData (0x36)} \rightarrow \text{ECU Flash} \rightarrow \text{Verify CRC}$$

**Step 9: Post-Write Verification**

$$\text{CRC}_{\text{written}} \stackrel{?}{=} \text{CRC}_{\text{calc}} \quad \text{[must be TRUE]}$$

**Step 10: Functional Verification (Dyno or Road Test)**

Measure before & after:

$$P_{\text{before}}, P_{\text{after}}, \quad \Delta P = P_{\text{after}} - P_{\text{before}}$$

$$\% \text{ Gain} = \frac{\Delta P}{P_{\text{before}}} \times 100$$

## 3.2 Worked Example: 15% Fuel Map Increase Calculation

A **1.5 L turbo-petrol engine** has an original fuel map cell value of **8.40 mg/stroke** at **3500 rpm, 80% load**. Calculate the new value after a **15% remap**.

$$\begin{aligned}
IQ_{\text{new}} &= IQ_{\text{orig}} \times \left(1 + \frac{\Delta\%}{100}\right) \\
&= 8.40 \times \left(1 + \frac{15}{100}\right) \\
&= 8.40 \times 1.15 \\
&= 9.66 \text{ mg/stroke}
\end{aligned}$$

**Increase** $= 9.66 - 8.40 = 1.26$ **mg/stroke**

## 3.3 Worked Example: Boost Pressure Lift

A turbo-diesel is remapped from **1.20 bar** boost to **1.50 bar**. Calculate the **percentage increase** in air mass and required fuel (assuming stoich operation).

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{\text{air, new}} &= \dot{m}_{\text{air, orig}} \times \frac{P_{\text{boost, new}}}{P_{\text{boost, orig}}} \\
&= \dot{m}_{\text{air, orig}} \times \frac{1.50}{1.20} \\
&= \dot{m}_{\text{air, orig}} \times 1.25 \\
\end{aligned}$$

So air mass increases by **25%**. To maintain stoich, fuel must also increase by **25%**:

$$\begin{aligned}
IQ_{\text{new}} &= IQ_{\text{orig}} \times 1.25 \\
\text{If } IQ_{\text{orig}} &= 28 \text{ mg/stroke} \\
IQ_{\text{new}} &= 28 \times 1.25 = 35 \text{ mg/stroke}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.4 Power Gain Estimation

Assuming thermal efficiency $\eta_{th}$ constant:

$$P_{\text{new}} = P_{\text{orig}} \times \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{fuel, new}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{fuel, orig}}}$$

For a stock **110 kW** diesel with **25% fuel/boost increase**:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{new}} &= 110 \times 1.25 \\
&= 137.5 \text{ kW} \\
\Delta P &= 27.5 \text{ kW} \approx +25\%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Real-world gains are **15–20%** for petrol and **20–35%** for turbo-diesel due to thermal and mechanical limits (knock, EGT, piston ring stress).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture of an ECU Remapping Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PC[Personal Computer with WinOLS Software]
    SW[Remapping Hardware Interface KESS V2 or K-TAG]
    VEH[Vehicle OBD II Diagnostic Port 16 Pin Connector]
    ECU[Engine Control Unit ECU Flash Memory]
    SENS[Engine Sensors MAF, MAP, Lambda, RPM]
    ACT[Engine Actuators Injectors, Turbo, Ignition Coil]
    DYNO[Dynamometer for Verification]

    PC -->|USB Connection| SW
    SW -->|CAN Bus or K Line Protocol| VEH
    VEH -->|UDS ISO 14229| ECU
    ECU -->|Reads Live Data| SENS
    ECU -->|Outputs PWM Signals| ACT
    ACT --> DYNO
    DYNO -->|Power Torque Readings| PC
```

## 4.2 ECU Remapping Workflow Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Identify Vehicle and ECU] --> S2[Step 2: Read Original ECU Flash]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Save Original Backup File]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Open File in WinOLS Editor]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Locate Target Calibration Maps]
    S5 --> S6{Maps Identified Correctly}
    S6 -->|Yes| S7[Step 6: Modify Fuel Boost Ignition Maps]
    S6 -->|No| S5
    S7 --> S8[Step 7: Recalculate ECU Checksum CRC 16]
    S8 --> S9[Step 8: Write Modified File to ECU Flash]
    S9 --> S10[Step 9: Verify Written File via CRC]
    S10 --> S11{Verification Passed}
    S11 -->|Yes| S12[Step 10: Dyno or Road Test Verification]
    S11 -->|No| S13[Rollback Original File]
    S13 --> S2
    S12 --> S14[Step 11: Document Final Calibration]
```

## 4.3 Map Editing Process — Data Transformation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Binary Flash File Raw Bytes] --> B[Memory Address Parser in WinOLS]
    B --> C[Map Identification via Axis Labels RPM Load Temperature]
    C --> D[Visual 2D or 3D Map Display Grid]
    D --> E[Tuner Adjusts Cell Values Percentage Based]
    E --> F[Checksum Correction Algorithm CRC 16]
    F --> G[Modified Binary File New Calibration]
    G --> H[Write Back to ECU via Hardware Tool]
```

## 4.4 Communication Protocol Stack (KTU Theory)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Application Layer UDS Services 0x22 ReadMemoryByAddress]
    L2[Transport Layer ISO TP ISO 15765 2]
    L3[Network Layer CAN 2.0B or K Line ISO 9141]
    L4[Physical Layer OBD II Connector Pin 6 CAN High Pin 14 CAN Low]
    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define engine remapping. List any two popular remapping software.**

**Model Answer:**
Engine remapping is the modification of the factory-programmed calibration maps inside the ECU's flash memory to alter engine performance parameters such as fuel quantity, injection timing, boost pressure, and ignition advance without changing mechanical components.

Two popular remapping software are:
1. **WinOLS** — by EVC GmbH, Germany, used for editing ECU binary files and recalculating checksums.
2. **ECM Titanium** — by Alientech, used for petrol and diesel calibration map editing.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between OBD remapping and Bench/Boot remapping.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | OBD Remapping | Bench/Boot Remapping |
|-----------|--------------|----------------------|
| Access | Via 16-pin diagnostic port | Direct ECU pins on bench |
| ECU Removal | Not required | Required |
| Speed | Slower (security gates) | Faster, direct memory access |
| Risk | Low | Medium to High |
| Tools Used | KESS V2, Autotuner | K-TAG, CMD Flash, Fgtech |

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students commonly lose **2–3 marks** by:
> (1) Confusing "remapping" with "chipping" — they are different.
> (2) Forgetting to mention **checksum correction** in the procedure.
> (3) Writing power gains without stating the **legal/warranty implications**.
> (4) Not drawing the **block diagram** — KTU awards **2 marks** specifically for the diagram.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *Complete Remapping Procedure*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure for engine remapping using OBD port tools, with a neat block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the major maps modified during petrol engine remapping and their effect on power and torque. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Procedure (7 marks):**

1. **Vehicle Identification:** Record VIN, ECU part number, software version, and engine code. This determines the correct calibration file and tool protocol.

2. **Connection Setup:** Connect the remapping hardware interface (e.g., KESS V2) to the PC via USB and to the vehicle's OBD-II port via the diagnostic connector.

3. **Original ECU Read:** Using **UDS protocol services** (RequestDownload 0x34, TransferData 0x36), read the entire ECU flash memory. Save as `.ori` backup file.

4. **Backup Verification:** Calculate **CRC-16 checksum** of the original file to confirm data integrity.

5. **Map Editing:** Open the `.bin` file in **WinOLS**. Locate the calibration maps (fuel, ignition, boost, torque limiter, speed limiter).

6. **Apply Modifications:** Increase fuel injection quantity by 10–25%, advance ignition timing by 2°–6°, raise boost pressure by 0.2–0.6 bar.

7. **Checksum Correction:** WinOLS auto-recalculates and embeds the new CRC.

8. **Write to ECU:** Transfer the modified file to ECU flash memory using TransferData service.

9. **Verification:** ECU performs self-check on startup; tuner's tool verifies written CRC matches calculated CRC.

10. **Road/Dyno Test:** Validate the gains on a chassis dynamometer or road test.

**Block Diagram (2 marks — mandatory):**
```
[PC with WinOLS] → USB → [KESS V2 Interface] → OBD-II Port → [ECU Flash Memory]
                                                                         ↓
                                                              [Engine Sensors/Actuators]
                                                                         ↓
                                                                  [Dyno Verification]
```

**(b) Major Maps and Effects (7 marks):**

| Map | Modification | Effect on Engine |
|-----|--------------|------------------|
| **Fuel Map (IQ)** | +15% to +25% | More energy per cycle → +15–30% power |
| **Ignition Advance** | +3° to +6° BTDC | Better combustion timing → higher torque |
| **Boost Pressure** | +0.2 to +0.6 bar | More air → supports more fuel → +20–35% power (turbo) |
| **Torque Limiter** | Raised or removed | Allows ECU to command higher torque |
| **RPM Limiter** | Raised by 500–1000 rpm | Wider powerband |
| **Throttle Response** | Sharper pedal curve | Better drivability, faster response |
| **Deceleration Fuel Cut** | Extended range | Marginal fuel economy improvement |

Effects on power and torque:
- **Petrol NA engines:** Gains typically **8–12%** (limited by knock margin).
- **Turbo-petrol engines:** Gains typically **15–25%** (boost lift gives big gains).
- **Turbo-diesel engines:** Gains typically **20–35%** (highest gains due to lean-burn tolerance).

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Tools & Software Comparison*

**(a)** Compare any four popular engine remapping software in terms of features, supported vehicles, and file formats. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the role of checksum correction in ECU remapping. What happens if checksum is not corrected? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Software Comparison (7 marks):**

| Software | Manufacturer | Key Feature | Supported Vehicles | File Format |
|----------|--------------|-------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **WinOLS** | EVC GmbH | Map editing with auto-checksum | Universal (any `.bin`) | `.bin`, `.hex` |
| **ECM Titanium** | Alientech | Pre-loaded map packs | Wide range | `.bin`, `.ori`, `.mod` |
| **KESS V2** | Alientech | OBD read/write tool | Cars, bikes, tractors | `.bin` |
| **K-TAG** | Alientech | Boot mode programming | ECUs with security | `.bin` |
| **HP Tuners** | HP Tuners Inc | US-domestic focus | GM, Ford, Dodge | `.pcm`, `.ecu` |
| **Cobb Accessport** | COBB Tuning | Handheld plug-and-play | Subaru, Mitsubishi, Ford, BMW | `.ap` |
| **EcuTek** | EcuTek Ltd | Performance tuning | Subaru, Nissan, BMW, Mazda | `.rom` |

**(b) Checksum Role (7 marks):**

The ECU firmware contains a **CRC-16 or CRC-32 checksum** stored at specific memory addresses. This checksum validates the integrity of calibration data.

**Process:**
1. Original ECU has CRC value $C_0$ calculated from original bytes.
2. After modification, the bytes change → CRC becomes $C_0' \neq C_0$.
3. Software tool (WinOLS) recalculates new CRC $C_{\text{new}}$ and writes it to the checksum location.

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{new}} &= \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} B_k^{\text{modified}} \cdot 2^{k} \pmod{2^{16}} \\
\text{Verify:} \quad C_{\text{stored}} &= C_{\text{new}}
\end{aligned}$$

**If checksum is NOT corrected:**
- ECU's **bootloader** detects the mismatch.
- ECU enters **"limp mode"** or refuses to start.
- **MIL (Malfunction Indicator Lamp)** illuminates.
- In severe cases, ECU becomes **bricked** — requiring recovery via boot mode.

Hence checksum correction is **MANDATORY** in any successful remap.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Quick-Revision Points — Read 5 times before exam:**

- ✅ Engine remapping = modifying ECU flash memory **without hardware changes**
- ✅ Primary tools: **KESS V2 (OBD)**, **K-TAG (Boot)**, **Autotuner**, **CMD**
- ✅ Primary software: **WinOLS**, **ECM Titanium**, **EcuTek**, **HP Tuners**
- ✅ Four critical maps: **Fuel IQ**, **Boost Pressure**, **Ignition Advance**, **Torque Limiter**
- ✅ Always create `.ori` backup before any modification
- ✅ **Checksum correction (CRC-16) is mandatory** — WinOLS does this automatically
- ✅ Petrol NA gain: **8–12%**, Turbo-petrol: **15–25%**, Turbo-diesel: **20–35%**
- ✅ Stoichiometric AFR: petrol **14.7:1**, diesel **14.5:1**, $\lambda = 1$
- ✅ Communication protocols: **UDS (ISO 14229)**, **CAN (ISO 15765)**, **K-Line (ISO 9141)**
- ✅ OBD-II connector: **Pin 6 = CAN High**, **Pin 14 = CAN Low**, **Pin 16 = +12V**
- ✅ Remapping is **ECUSoftware-only**; "chipping" is the older **hardware-EPROM** method
- ✅ Always verify via **dynamometer or road test** after writing
- ✅ Legal note: Remapping may **void warranty** and violate **emission norms** if used on public roads

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and testing of the circuit for keyless entry and parking system
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL607 - AUTOTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Design and testing of the circuit for keyless entry and parking system

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Keyless Entry System (KLS / RKE):** A Radio-Frequency (RF) based automotive access control subsystem in which an electronic **Remote Key Fob (transmitter)** broadcasts a uniquely encrypted identification code over a license-free **ISM (Industrial, Scientific, Medical) band**—typically **315 MHz (USA/Japan)** or **433.92 MHz (Europe/India)**. The vehicle-mounted **Receiver Module** demodulates the signal, validates the rolling/hopping code against the **Body Control Module (BCM) / Electronic Control Unit (ECU)**, and actuates the door-lock solenoids upon successful authentication.

**Reverse / Parking Assistance System (PAS):** An active-sonar based driver-aid subsystem that employs **piezoelectric ultrasonic transducers** (operating at **40 kHz ± 2 kHz**) to emit acoustic bursts toward an obstacle. The system measures the **Time-of-Flight (ToF)** of the reflected echo, computes the radial distance, and provides **multimodal feedback** (audible buzzer, LED bar, or LCD) to the driver during low-speed reversing maneuvers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This lab experiment integrates **three core ECE/EEE competencies**:
> 1. Embedded C / Assembly programming of an 8-bit or 32-bit MCU.
> 2. Interfacing of RF transceivers and ultrasonic sensors.
> 3. Actuator driving (relays / MOSFETs / solenoids) with proper isolation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

**Imagine a hotel doorman and a high-tech mailbox:**

- The **Key Fob** is like a **personalized hotel keycard**. When you tap it (or press a button), it whispers a *secret, ever-changing password* ("open sesame" — but rewritten every time) to a hidden microphone in the car.
- The car's **Receiver + ECU** is the **doorman** holding a list of valid passwords. If the whispered code matches, the door clicks open. If a thief replays the same old code, it has already expired — **the thief is locked out**.
- The **Parking Sensor** is like **a bat's echolocation** 🦇. It shouts a sound you can't hear (ultrasound), waits for the echo, and shouts faster / louder as you get closer to a wall — exactly like the beeping in your car that goes *beep… beep-beep… BEEEP!* when you're about to bump.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Hook:** Both subsystems share one philosophy — *measure something invisible (a radio wave, a sound wave), decide smartly, then act.*

### 1.3 Operating Frequency Standards (Bold Highlights)

| Subsystem | Frequency / Standard | Governing Body |
|---|---|---|
| Keyless Entry (India/Europe) | **433.92 MHz** | ETSI EN 300 220 |
| Keyless Entry (USA) | **315 MHz** | FCC Part 15 |
| Ultrasonic Parking | **40 kHz ± 2 kHz** | ISO 17386 (PAS) |
| CAN-Bus Backbone | **500 kbps** | ISO 11898 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Block-Level Architecture

**Keyless Entry Sub-System — Five Logical Stages:**

1. **Input Stage (Pushbutton + Encoder IC):** HT12E / PT2262 — encodes 8-bit address + 4-bit data.
2. **RF Modulation Stage:** SAW resonator + OOK/ASK modulator — *ON-OFF Keying* is the cheapest envelope modulation used.
3. **Transmission Stage:** Quarter-wave whip antenna — length $L = \dfrac{c}{4f}$.
4. **Receiver Stage:** Superheterodyne or super-regenerative RX (e.g., RX-433 module) → demodulated digital bitstream.
5. **Decoder + Actuator Stage:** HT12D / PT2272 + Relay/MOSFET driving the door-lock motor.

**Parking Sub-System — Three Logical Stages:**

1. **Trigger Stage:** Reverse gear engaged → MCU sends a 10 µs trigger pulse to the HC-SR04.
2. **Echo Stage:** Sensor emits 8-cycle 40 kHz burst, listens for return.
3. **Compute + Actuate Stage:** MCU measures echo pulse width, computes distance, drives buzzer + LEDs.

### 2.2 Why and How — The "Core Reasoning"

- **Why 433.92 MHz?** It lies in the license-free ISM band, has good wall-penetration, and antenna length at this frequency is convenient (≈ 17 cm, fits in a key fob).
- **Why Rolling Code?** Static codes can be captured by **code-grabbers**. Rolling code algorithms (KEELOQ by Microchip) advance a 16-bit counter encrypted with a 64-bit manufacturer key — making replay attacks computationally infeasible.
- **Why Ultrasonic (not IR) for parking?** IR is fooled by shiny black car paints and sunlight; **ultrasound travels at a known speed and reflects well off most automotive materials**, regardless of color or transparency.
- **Why ToF, not phase-shift?** ToF is a **direct, unambiguous distance** measurement up to ~4 m (the typical bumper-to-object range), while phase-shift is better for short range (< 30 cm).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For all derivations below:** speed of sound in air at **20 °C** is $c_{sound} \approx 343 \text{ m/s}$. RF signals travel effectively at $c_{light} \approx 3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$ (delay negligible over automotive distances).

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Distance from ultrasonic echo | $d = \dfrac{c_{sound} \cdot t_{echo}}{2}$ | metres (m) | Factor **2** because wave travels to object and back |
| 2 | Speed of sound vs. temperature | $c \approx 331 + 0.6 \cdot T$ | m/s | $T$ in °C; use for compensation |
| 3 | Quarter-wave antenna length | $L = \dfrac{c}{4f}$ | metres | Velocity factor ≈ 0.95 for wire |
| 4 | Free-space path loss (Friis) | $FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | dB | $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz |
| 5 | OOK mark/space energy | $E_b/N_0$ (BER basis) | dB | Used in receiver sensitivity |
| 6 | PWM buzzer frequency | $f_{buzz} = 2 \text{ kHz to 4 kHz}$ | Hz | Human ear most sensitive 2–4 kHz |
| 7 | Relay coil current | $I_{coil} = \dfrac{V_{cc}}{R_{coil}}$ | A | Typical 12V relay: 30–70 mA |
| 8 | LED current limiting R | $R = \dfrac{V_{supply} - V_{f}}{I_{f}}$ | Ω | $V_f \approx 2V$ (red), $I_f = 10$ mA |
| 9 | Battery fob life | $t = \dfrac{Q_{mAh}}{I_{active} \cdot d_{cycle}}$ | hours | Typical CR2032 = 220 mAh |
| 10 | Decoding latency | $t_{dec} = N_{bits} \cdot t_{bit}$ | s | HT12D uses 4-word validation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical:** In all KTU derivations, **always show the factor of 2 in the ToF distance formula** — losing this 2 is the #1 most common KTU mark-deduction error.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **OEM Adoption:** Every modern car from Maruti Suzuki to Tesla uses both subsystems — the **parking assist market is valued at USD 4.8 B (2024)**.
- **Aftermarket:** Indian 2-wheeler segment (Hero, TVS) now ships **passive keyless entry** even in 110 cc bikes.
- **ADAS Linkage:** These are the **front-end sensors of Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS)** — feeds to the central ECU for automatic emergency braking.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Distance from Ultrasonic Echo (Full Working)

We begin with the fundamental relationship between distance, velocity, and time for a wave traveling at constant speed:

$$
d_{one-way} = v \cdot t_{one-way}
$$

**Step 1:** The ultrasonic wave must travel **from the sensor to the obstacle AND back** to the sensor (round trip).

$$
d_{round-trip} = v_{sound} \cdot t_{echo}
$$

**Step 2:** Because $d_{one-way}$ is what we want (the actual obstacle distance), and the round-trip covers the same gap **twice**:

$$
d_{one-way} = \dfrac{d_{round-trip}}{2} = \dfrac{v_{sound} \cdot t_{echo}}{2}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the standard speed of sound at 20 °C, $v_{sound} = 343 \text{ m/s}$:

$$
d = \dfrac{343 \cdot t_{echo}}{2}
$$

**Step 4:** Since MCU timers typically give $t_{echo}$ in **microseconds (µs)**, we convert:

$$
343 \text{ m/s} = 0.000343 \text{ m/µs} = 0.0343 \text{ cm/µs}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\;d \text{ (in cm)} = \dfrac{t_{echo} \text{ (in µs)}}{58.31}\;}
$$

**Step 5: Numerical Example (typical KTU problem):**
If the echo pulse width measured by the MCU is **1500 µs**, calculate the obstacle distance.

$$
d = \dfrac{1500}{58.31} = 25.72 \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 6: Sanity Check:**
Round-trip time of 1500 µs → round-trip distance = $0.0343 \times 1500 = 51.45$ cm → one-way = 25.72 cm ✓

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Quarter-Wave Antenna Length for 433.92 MHz Key Fob

**Step 1:** Wavelength of the RF carrier:

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}}{433.92 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}}
$$

**Step 2:** Numerical evaluation:

$$
\lambda = 0.6912 \text{ m} = 69.12 \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 3:** Quarter-wavelength (most common for compact fob antennas):

$$
L = \dfrac{\lambda}{4} = \dfrac{69.12}{4} = 17.28 \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply velocity factor of copper wire in free space (≈ 0.95):

$$
L_{practical} = 0.95 \times 17.28 = 16.42 \text{ cm}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;L_{practical} \approx 16.4 \text{ cm for 433.92 MHz}\;}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: LED Current-Limiting Resistor (Actuator Driver)

Suppose we need to drive a **red LED** (forward voltage $V_f = 2.0$ V) at $I_f = 15$ mA from a **5 V MCU pin**.

**Step 1:** Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around the loop:

$$
V_{supply} = V_R + V_f
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for voltage drop across resistor:

$$
V_R = V_{supply} - V_f = 5 - 2.0 = 3.0 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply Ohm's Law:

$$
R = \dfrac{V_R}{I_f} = \dfrac{3.0 \text{ V}}{15 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A}} = 200 \text{ Ω}
$$

**Step 4:** Choose next-higher standard E12 value: **220 Ω**.

$$
\boxed{\;R_{LED} = 220 \text{ Ω at } 5\text{V, } 15\text{ mA, red LED}\;}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Logic for Buzzer Tones (Parking)

| Obstacle Distance | LED Pattern | Buzzer Action | Driver Alert Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| $d > 250$ cm | All OFF | Silent | Safe |
| $150 < d \leq 250$ cm | Green ON | Beep every 1.0 s | Caution |
| $100 < d \leq 150$ cm | Yellow ON | Beep every 0.5 s | Approach |
| $50 < d \leq 100$ cm | Orange ON | Beep every 0.25 s | Warning |
| $d \leq 50$ cm | Red ON (continuous) | Continuous tone | STOP |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Key Fob Pushbutton] --> B[HT12E Encoder]
    B --> C[433 MHz OOK TX Module]
    C --> D[Antenna 16.4 cm]
    D --> E[433 MHz RX Module]
    E --> F[HT12D Decoder]
    F --> G{MCU 8051 or ATmega328}
    G --> H[Relay Driver ULN2003]
    H --> I[Door Lock Solenoid 12V]
    G --> J[Buzzer 5V Piezo]
    K[HC-SR04 Ultrasonic Sensor] --> G
    G --> L[LED Bar Graph Display]
    G --> M[16x2 LCD Display]
```

### 4.2 Flowchart — MCU Decision Logic (Parking + Keyless)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0([Power ON MCU Init]) --> P1[Configure Timer Ports]
    P1 --> P2{Reverse Gear ENGAGED?}
    P2 -- No --> P3[Listen for RF RX Pin]
    P3 --> P3a{Valid HT12D Code?}
    P3a -- Yes --> P4[Activate Door Lock Relay]
    P3a -- No --> P3
    P2 -- Yes --> P5[Send 10 us Trigger to HC-SR04]
    P5 --> P6[Measure Echo Pulse Width]
    P6 --> P7[Compute Distance d]
    P7 --> P8{d less than 50 cm?}
    P8 -- Yes --> P9[RED LED + Continuous Buzzer]
    P8 -- No --> P10{d less than 100 cm?}
    P10 -- Yes --> P11[ORANGE LED + 250 ms Beep]
    P10 -- No --> P12{d less than 150 cm?}
    P12 -- Yes --> P13[YELLOW LED + 500 ms Beep]
    P12 -- No --> P14{d less than 250 cm?}
    P14 -- Yes --> P15[GREEN LED + 1 s Beep]
    P14 -- No --> P16[All OFF]
    P9 --> P5
    P11 --> P5
    P13 --> P5
    P15 --> P5
    P16 --> P5
```

### 4.3 Pin-Out Schematic Table (ATmega328P / Arduino UNO Reference)

| MCU Pin | Connected To | Function |
|---|---|---|
| D2 (INT0) | RX-433 DATA pin | RF interrupt |
| D3 | Trigger pin of HC-SR04 | 10 µs pulse start |
| D4 | Echo pin of HC-SR04 | ToF measurement input |
| D5 | Buzzer (+ve) | PWM tone output |
| D8, D9, D10, D11 | LED1, LED2, LED3, LED4 | Distance zone indicators |
| D12 | Relay IN (via ULN2003) | Door-lock actuator |
| A4, A5 | LCD SDA, SCL (I2C) | Distance readout display |
| 5V, GND | Vcc and common ground | Power rails |

### 4.4 Timing Diagram of HC-SR04 Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant MCU
    participant HCSR as HC-SR04
    participant OBJ as Obstacle
    MCU->>HCSR: Trigger Pulse (10 us HIGH)
    activate HCSR
    HCSR->>OBJ: Emit 8 x 40 kHz Burst
    OBJ-->>HCSR: Echo Returns
    HCSR-->>MCU: Echo Pin HIGH for t_echo
    deactivate HCSR
    Note over MCU: d = 0.0343 * t_echo / 2 cm
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
*List the operating frequencies used by automotive keyless entry systems in India and justify why the ISM band is preferred.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Frequency used in India:** **433.92 MHz** (with secondary use of 315 MHz in some imported vehicles). (1 mark)
- **ISM band** stands for **Industrial, Scientific, Medical** radio band, license-exempt per ITU Radio Regulations. (1 mark)
- **Justification:**
  - No licensing fee → low BOM cost for OEMs.
  - Adequate bandwidth for short data bursts.
  - Good wall / body-penetration at sub-GHz.
  - Quarter-wave antenna fits inside key fob form-factor (~16 cm). (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why are ultrasonic sensors preferred over infrared sensors for reverse parking systems?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Independence from optical properties:** IR sensors are fooled by transparent, reflective, or black surfaces; ultrasound reflects well off all common automotive materials. (1 mark)
2. **Ambient light immunity:** IR is corrupted by direct sunlight; ultrasound operates in a quiet 40 kHz acoustic band. (1 mark)
3. **Direct distance measurement:** Time-of-Flight with ultrasound gives unambiguous distance up to ~4 m, ideal for reversing range. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Keyless Entry Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **Rolling-Code Keyless Entry System**. List the role of each block. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A Rolling-Code Keyless Entry System (RKE) has the following five functional blocks:

1. **Key Fob Encoder + Pushbutton:** When the user presses LOCK/UNLOCK, an 8-bit address + 4-bit command is fed to the encoder (e.g., Microchip **HCS300 KEELOQ** IC). The encoder increments a 16-bit synchronous counter and encrypts the counter value with a **64-bit manufacturer secret key**, producing a 66-bit transmission packet. (2 marks)

2. **RF Transmitter + 433 MHz OOK Modulator:** The encrypted packet is OOK-modulated onto a 433.92 MHz carrier via a SAW resonator. Transmission duration ≈ 50–100 ms. (1 mark)

3. **Antenna:** Quarter-wave whip of 16.4 cm; radiated power ≤ 10 mW ERP. (1 mark)

4. **Vehicle RF Receiver + Decoder:** A super-heterodyne receiver (e.g., **RX-433**) demodulates the OOK envelope; the decoder IC runs the same KEELOQ algorithm to decrypt and verify the counter value lies within a **16-bit ±16 acceptance window** (to allow button presses while out of range). (2 marks)

5. **Actuator Stage (BCM + Relay/MOSFET):** On acceptance, the BCM energises a relay that drives the door-lock motor in the appropriate polarity (LOCK or UNLOCK direction). (1 mark)

**(b)** Design the **transmitter-side encoder circuit** for the keyless entry system. Specify component values and justify your choice. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Design Specification:**

| Parameter | Value | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Encoder IC | HT12E (or HCS300) | 8-bit address, 4-bit data, OOK-compatible |
| Carrier frequency | 433.92 MHz | ISM band, license-free |
| Modulation | OOK (100% AM) | Simplest envelope modulation, low power |
| Antenna length | 16.4 cm | Quarter-wave at 433.92 MHz |
| Power supply | CR2032 coin cell (3 V) | Compact, 220 mAh capacity |
| TX current | 8 mA average | Battery life ≈ 1 year at 10 presses/day |

**Component Selection (with values):**

- **R_osc (HT12E timing resistor):** 1.1 MΩ → sets internal oscillator to ~3 kHz data rate. (1 mark)
- **Address pins A0–A7:** Hardwired to a unique 8-bit pattern (e.g., `0xA5`) — must match the receiver decoder. (1 mark)
- **Data pins D0–D3:** Connected to four pushbuttons (LOCK, UNLOCK, BOOT, PANIC). (1 mark)
- **TE (Transmission Enable):** Tied to Vcc via the pushbutton matrix — enables TX only when a button is pressed, saving battery. (1 mark)
- **RF stage:** Colpitts oscillator with SAW resonator + OOK buffer; antenna through a π-network match. (1 mark)
- **Decoupling:** 100 nF + 10 µF at Vcc pin of HT12E to suppress coin-cell bounce. (1 mark)
- **Protection:** 5.1 V Zener at antenna feed for ESD. (1 mark)

**Calculations:**

Battery life: $t = \dfrac{220 \text{ mAh}}{8 \text{ mA} \times 0.005 \text{ (5\% duty)}} = 5500 \text{ h} \approx 2$ years (continuous standby would be ~110 h, so pulsing is essential). 

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Parking System Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the principle of operation of an **ultrasonic reverse parking system** with a functional block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:** The system uses the **Time-of-Flight (ToF)** of an ultrasonic wave. A 40 kHz acoustic burst is emitted; the time taken to reflect from an obstacle and return is measured and converted to distance using $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$.

**Block Diagram (drawn on paper):**

```
[Reverse Gear Switch] → [MCU ATmega328] → [HC-SR04 Trigger] → [40 kHz Burst] 
                                                                  ↓
[LCD Display] ← [MCU] ← [Echo Pin Timer] ← [Reflected Echo Received] ← Obstacle
```

**Step-by-Step Operation:**

1. When reverse gear is engaged, a 12 V signal is level-shifted to 5 V and fed to MCU pin D7 (interrupt). (1 mark)
2. MCU generates a **10 µs HIGH trigger pulse** on D3 connected to HC-SR04 TRIG pin. (1 mark)
3. HC-SR04 internally fires **eight 40 kHz cycles** from the transmitting transducer. (1 mark)
4. The receiving transducer waits for the echo; the ECHO pin goes HIGH the moment transmission ends and LOW when the echo is received. (1 mark)
5. MCU measures the HIGH duration ($t_{echo}$) using `pulseIn()`. (1 mark)
6. Distance computed: $d = \dfrac{t_{echo}}{58.31}$ cm. (1 mark)
7. Based on the zone (see Section 3.4 Analytical Matrix), the appropriate LED and buzzer tone are activated. (1 mark)

**(b)** An ultrasonic sensor returns an **echo pulse width of 2350 µs**. The ambient temperature is **35 °C**. Calculate the obstacle distance. Also, design a **buzzer driver circuit** for the warning zone ($d \leq 50$ cm). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Speed of sound at 35 °C:**

$$
c = 331 + 0.6 \times 35 = 331 + 21 = 352 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2: Convert to cm/µs:**

$$
c = 352 \text{ m/s} = 0.0352 \text{ cm/µs}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the ToF formula:**

$$
d = \dfrac{c \cdot t_{echo}}{2} = \dfrac{0.0352 \times 2350}{2}
$$

**Step 4: Evaluate:**

$$
d = \dfrac{82.72}{2} = 41.36 \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 5: Verify in alternate form:**

$$
d = \dfrac{t_{echo}}{2 / 0.0352} = \dfrac{2350}{56.82} = 41.36 \text{ cm} \;\; ✓
$$

$$
\boxed{\;d \approx 41.4 \text{ cm} \Rightarrow \text{RED LED + Continuous Buzzer (STOP zone)}\;}
$$

(2 marks for numerical computation; 1 mark for zone identification.)

**Buzzer Driver Circuit Design:**

- **Buzzer:** 5 V piezo, $I_{buzz} = 30$ mA, internal oscillator 2.4 kHz.
- **Driver:** Single NPN transistor **BC547** (since MCU pin can source only ~40 mA reliably and a transistor provides isolation).
- **Base resistor:** $R_B = \dfrac{V_{OH} - V_{BE}}{I_B} = \dfrac{5 - 0.7}{3 \text{ mA}} \approx 1.43 \text{ kΩ → use 1.5 kΩ standard.}$
- **Flyback diode:** 1N4007 across buzzer (reverse-biased) for inductive kick protection.
- **Vcc:** 5 V from MCU regulator; buzzer draws 30 mA continuously in STOP zone. (3 marks)

**Final Schematic (in words):**
> MCU Pin D5 → 1.5 kΩ → BC547 Base; Emitter → GND; Collector → Buzzer (−) terminal; Buzzer (+) → +5V; 1N4007 cathode at +5V, anode at collector.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in $d = ct/2$ → 1 mark lost.
> 2. **Wrong units** in the ToF formula (mixing µs with cm/s) → 2 marks lost.
> 3. **Not mentioning temperature compensation** in ultrasonic distance problems at non-20°C → 1 mark lost.
> 4. **Drawing OOK instead of ASK** in block diagrams for RKE — OOK is a special case of ASK; examiners accept both but **OOK is preferred** for simple fobs.
> 5. **Forgetting the address-match requirement** between HT12E and HT12D — both must be set to the **same 8-bit pattern** for code validation.
> 6. **Saying "ultrasound = 20 kHz"** — wrong; automotive parking uses **40 kHz ± 2 kHz** specifically because it balances range and beam-width.
> 7. **Not including the relay flyback diode** in actuator design — instant 1-mark cut.
> 8. **Confusing KEELOQ with simple HT12E** — HT12E is a *fixed-code* demonstrator, **KEELOQ is the production rolling-code** algorithm.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✨

- **Keyless Entry Frequency (India):** 433.92 MHz, OOK modulation, address-matched encoder/decoder pair.
- **Parking Frequency:** 40 kHz ultrasonic, ToF principle, divide by 2 for round-trip.
- **Master Formula (must memorize):** $d = \dfrac{(331 + 0.6T) \cdot t_{echo}}{2}$ → at 20°C reduces to $d = 0.0343 \cdot t_{echo} / 2$ cm.
- **Antenna length:** 16.4 cm quarter-wave for 433.92 MHz (with 0.95 velocity factor).
- **Decoder validation window:** 16-bit counter ±16 hops; prevents replay attacks.
- **Sensor choice:** Ultrasonic (not IR) for parking — sunlight-immune, color-agnostic.
- **Driver ICs:** ULN2003 (relay array), BC547 (single transistor), IRF540N (MOSFET for high current).
- **Safety add-on:** Always include a **flyback diode** across inductive loads (relays, solenoids, buzzers).
- **Power budget:** Key fob CR2032 lasts ~2 years if TX duty is kept < 5 %.
- **Lab Viva Favourite:** *"Why OOK and not FSK?"* — Answer: OOK is the simplest envelope modulation, needs no PLL, and is sufficient for short-range low-data-rate fob signals; FSK is reserved for long-range / high-data-rate TPMS.
- **Compliance Note:** Transmit power ≤ 10 mW ERP in India (per WPC / FCC Part 15 §249).
- **Integration Tip:** In the lab, test the **transmitter alone** on a scope first (see 433 MHz burst), then test the **receiver-decoder path** with a wired bitstream, and only then integrate with the parking sensor — this staged debugging saves 80 % of lab time.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and testing of the circuits for ABS, EBD, and traction control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL607 - AUTOTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Design and testing of the circuits for ABS, EBD, and traction control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module Experiment 2: Design and Testing of ABS, EBD, and Traction Control Circuits

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-lock Braking System (ABS)**: A closed-loop automotive safety system that prevents wheel lock-up during emergency braking by automatically modulating hydraulic brake pressure at each wheel. It maintains **wheel slip** in the optimal range of **10%–30%**, where the tire-to-road friction coefficient ($\mu$) is maximum, thereby preserving steering control and preventing skidding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electronic Brake-force Distribution (EBD)**: An extension of ABS that dynamically varies the front-to-rear brake pressure ratio based on vehicle deceleration, axle load transfer, and individual wheel speed. It replaces the mechanical proportioning valve with a software-controlled, real-time distribution algorithm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Traction Control System (TCS / ASR)**: A powertrain-domain active safety system that prevents driven-wheel slip during acceleration by reducing engine torque (via throttle, spark retard, or fuel cut) and/or applying brake pressure to the spinning wheel. It is functionally the *longitudinal-acceleration* counterpart of ABS.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are running on a wet, slippery floor and trying to stop suddenly. If you drag your feet hard, you slip and fall — losing all control. But if you "pump" your feet (push, release, push, release), you stay upright and stop in a controlled manner. **ABS is the car doing that "pumping" automatically, 15–25 times per second.**

**EBD** is like two friends carrying a heavy log on a stretcher: the friend at the back (rear wheels) must carry *less* weight than the friend at the front, because as you slow down, the heavy end (engine) shifts forward. EBD continuously recalculates this sharing.

**Traction Control** is the opposite scenario: instead of braking too hard, you are accelerating too hard on ice and the wheels spin uselessly. TCS is like a friend gently pressing the gas pedal back when they feel the wheels spinning out.

### Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded for Recall)

- **Wheel slip threshold for ABS intervention:** **λ = 10%–30%** (optimum peak μ region)
- **ABS cycle frequency:** **15–25 Hz** (typical solenoid modulation rate)
- **Tire-road peak friction slip:** **λ_peak ≈ 8%–15%** (dry asphalt), **15%–30%** (wet/icy)
- **Hydraulic modulator pressure range:** **0 – 20 MPa** (typical passenger car)
- **Wheel speed sensor output frequency:** **1 Hz per km/h** (varies with tone ring; e.g., 48-pole ring → 1.33 Hz per km/h)
- **Standard vehicle deceleration threshold for EBD activation:** **a > 0.3 g** (≈ 2.94 m/s²)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment integrates *sensor signal conditioning*, *ECU logic implementation*, *hydraulic actuator driving*, and *closed-loop test-bench validation* — testing all four COs of PCAUL607 (CO1: Identify components; CO2: Interface sensors; CO3: Design driver circuits; CO4: Validate via test rig).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Architecture of an Integrated ABS / EBD / TCS ECU

The three systems share **one ECU and one hydraulic modulator** (Bosch, Continental, Aisin call this an "Integrated Chassis Control" module). The functional decomposition is:

### A. Input Signal Acquisition Stage
1. **4 × Wheel Speed Sensors (WSS)** — variable reluctance (VR) or Hall-effect (active)
2. **Brake Pedal Position Sensor** — typically dual-redundant Hall-effect (angle + force)
3. **Steering Angle Sensor (SAS)** — for ABS/TCS coordination with ESC
4. **Yaw Rate / Lateral Acceleration Sensor** — integrated MEMS (e.g., ADXRS652)
5. **Master Cylinder Pressure Sensor** — strain-gauge, 0–25 MPa range
6. **Engine ECU CAN bus** — for engine torque request (TCS) and torque verification

### B. ECU Signal Processing Stage
- **Wheel speed calculation:** $f_{WSS} \rightarrow V_{wheel} = \pi \cdot D \cdot f_{WSS} / N$
- **Reference vehicle speed estimation:** $V_{ref}$ via max-select logic + longitudinal acceleration
- **Slip calculation per wheel:** $\lambda_i = (V_{ref} - V_{wheel,i}) / V_{ref}$
- **Decision logic:** State machine — *Build → Hold → Release → Re-apply*
- **PID controller:** Generates PWM duty cycle for each inlet/outlet solenoid

### C. Hydraulic Modulator (HECU) Output Stage
Each wheel channel has **2 solenoid valves**:
- **Inlet valve (Normally Open):** Closes to *Hold* or *Release* pressure
- **Outlet valve (Normally Closed):** Opens to vent pressure to accumulator during *Release*

Plus a **return pump (DC motor)** to recover fluid back to master cylinder.

### D. CAN / LIN Communication
- ABS event status broadcast on CAN at 100 ms cadence
- TCS torque reduction request: CAN message to ECM (Engine Control Module)

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Slip-Target Window

The **Magic Formula (Pacejka 1989)** describes tire force generation:

$$F = D \cdot \sin(C \cdot \arctan\{B \cdot x - E \cdot (B \cdot x - \arctan(B \cdot x))\})$$

Where $B$ = stiffness, $C$ = shape, $D$ = peak, $E$ = curvature, $x$ = slip input. The *peak* of this curve sits in the slip window of **10%–30%**, and this is precisely where ABS regulates.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---------------------|------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Wheel slip ratio | $\lambda = \dfrac{V_{ref} - V_{wheel}}{V_{ref}}$ | dimensionless (0–1) | ABS trigger, TCS trigger |
| 2 | Wheel speed from sensor frequency | $V_{wheel} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot f_{WSS}}{N}$ | m/s | WSS signal processing |
| 3 | Vehicle deceleration | $a = \dfrac{dV_{ref}}{dt} = \dfrac{V_{ref}(t_1) - V_{ref}(t_2)}{t_2 - t_1}$ | m/s² | EBD threshold |
| 4 | Static front-axle load transfer | $\Delta W = \dfrac{m \cdot a \cdot h_{cg}}{L}$ | N | EBD ideal ratio |
| 5 | Ideal EBD ratio (front/rear) | $\dfrac{F_f}{F_r} = \dfrac{b + h_{cg} \cdot a/g}{a - h_{cg} \cdot a/g}$ | dimensionless | Brake force split |
| 6 | Hydraulic pressure ΔP needed | $P_{wheel} = \dfrac{T_{brake}}{A_{piston} \cdot \mu_{pad} \cdot r_{eff}}$ | Pa | Modulator sizing |
| 7 | Solenoid current (H-bridge) | $I_{sol} = \dfrac{V_{bat} - V_{ce(sat)}}{R_{sol}}$ | A | Driver circuit design |
| 8 | PID controller output (brake pressure) | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t) dt + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | % PWM | Pressure regulation |
| 9 | TCS torque reduction request | $\Delta T_{eng} = J_{wheel} \cdot \dot{\omega}_{wheel} - T_{load}$ | N·m | Engine torque intervention |
| 10 | Wheel angular acceleration | $\dot{\omega} = \dfrac{\omega(t_2) - \omega(t_1)}{\Delta t}$ | rad/s² | Slip-derivative term |
| 11 | HECU pump motor back-EMF | $V_{b} = K_e \cdot \omega_{pump} + I \cdot R_a$ | V | Pump driver thermal calc |
| 12 | Stopping distance (kinetic) | $d = \dfrac{V_0^2}{2 \mu g}$ | m | ABS effectiveness metric |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation key:** $V_{ref}$ = estimated reference vehicle speed; $V_{wheel}$ = linear speed of wheel center; $D$ = tire outer diameter; $N$ = number of teeth on tone ring; $f_{WSS}$ = wheel speed sensor frequency; $h_{cg}$ = height of center of gravity; $L$ = wheelbase; $a$ = deceleration; $b$ = distance from CG to rear axle; $T_{brake}$ = brake torque; $A_{piston}$ = caliper piston area; $\mu_{pad}$ = pad friction coefficient; $r_{eff}$ = effective rotor radius.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Functional Safety (ISO 26262):** ABS/TCS are **ASIL-D** systems — failure causes life-threatening risk without redundancy. Circuit design must include watchdogs, dual-MCU lockstep, and fail-safe valve defaults.
- **EMC Compliance:** WSS inputs must have **common-mode chokes** and **TVS diodes** (per ISO 7637-2) to survive load-dump transients up to 100 V.
- **Test-Bench HIL:** dSPACE, National Instruments VT System, or ETAS LABCAR emulate the vehicle dynamics, while the real ECU executes — detecting algorithm bugs before road testing.
- **OEM Cost Pressure:** Modern HECUs combine ABS + EBD + ESC + TCS in a single 14-pin connector module; PCB designers must use **4-layer FR4** with dedicated GND pour and isolated analog/digital zones.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Reference Vehicle Speed ($V_{ref}$) for Slip Calculation

ABS requires the *true vehicle speed*, but only wheel speeds are directly measurable. We must reconstruct $V_{ref}$ from the four wheel signals.

**Step 1 — Selection of fastest wheel.**
During braking, the wheel that is *not* locking has the highest speed. This is the wheel with the lowest slip.

$$V_{ref}(k) = \max(V_{FL}(k),\ V_{FR}(k),\ V_{RL}(k),\ V_{RR}(k))$$

**Step 2 — Apply monotonicity constraint (no regen).**
Since the vehicle cannot accelerate during braking (ABS context), $V_{ref}$ can only stay the same or decrease:

$$V_{ref}(k) = \min(V_{ref}(k-1),\ \max(V_{wheel}))$$

**Step 3 — Apply physical deceleration limit.**
A road vehicle cannot decelerate faster than ~1 g on dry asphalt. If the calculated drop exceeds this, clamp it:

$$V_{ref}(k) \geq V_{ref}(k-1) - a_{max} \cdot \Delta t$$

where $a_{max} = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$ (1 g), and $\Delta t$ is the ECU task period (typically **5 ms** = 200 Hz loop).

**Step 4 — Resulting slip calculation per wheel.**

$$\lambda_i = \frac{V_{ref} - V_{wheel,i}}{V_{ref}}, \quad i \in \{FL, FR, RL, RR\}$$

**Conversion logic:** In this four-step derivation, we move from a *raw signal* (sensor frequency), to a *reconstructed* vehicle speed (with physical constraints), and finally to the *decision variable* (slip). Each step applies a constraint that mirrors physical reality — this is what makes the algorithm robust against sensor noise and frozen-wheel (loss of WSS) faults.

## 3.2 Derivation: EBD Front/Rear Pressure Split

Given a vehicle with mass $m$, wheelbase $L$, CG height $h_{cg}$, and CG located distance $b$ behind the front axle and $a$ in front of the rear axle (so $L = a + b$).

**Step 1 — Static weight distribution.**
At rest, front and rear axle loads are:

$$W_f = \frac{m \cdot g \cdot b}{L}, \qquad W_r = \frac{m \cdot g \cdot a}{L}$$

**Step 2 — Dynamic weight transfer under deceleration $a$.**
A pseudo-force $m \cdot a$ acts at the CG, shifting load forward:

$$\Delta W = \frac{m \cdot a \cdot h_{cg}}{L}$$

New axle loads become:

$$W_f^{dyn} = \frac{m \cdot g \cdot b}{L} + \frac{m \cdot a \cdot h_{cg}}{L} = \frac{m \cdot g \cdot b + m \cdot a \cdot h_{cg}}{L}$$

$$W_r^{dyn} = \frac{m \cdot g \cdot a}{L} - \frac{m \cdot a \cdot h_{cg}}{L} = \frac{m \cdot g \cdot a - m \cdot a \cdot h_{cg}}{L}$$

**Step 3 — Ideal brake force ratio (no premature lock).**
To prevent rear-wheel lock-up, brake force at each axle must be proportional to its dynamic vertical load:

$$\frac{F_f}{F_r} = \frac{W_f^{dyn}}{W_r^{dyn}} = \frac{g \cdot b + a \cdot h_{cg}}{g \cdot a - a \cdot h_{cg}}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $g$, and letting $a/g$ be the deceleration in g-units, $\xi = a/g$:

$$\boxed{\frac{F_f}{F_r} = \frac{b + \xi \cdot h_{cg}}{a - \xi \cdot h_{cg}}}$$

**Step 4 — Hydraulic pressure mapping.**
Since $F = P \cdot A_{caliper} \cdot \mu_{pad} \cdot 2$ (for a 2-piston caliper; general $n$-piston: $F = n \cdot P \cdot A \cdot \mu \cdot r_{eff}$), the required pressure ratio is:

$$\frac{P_f}{P_r} = \frac{F_f}{F_r} \cdot \frac{A_r \cdot r_{eff,r}}{A_f \cdot r_{eff,f}}$$

For equal calipers and rotors, $P_f / P_r = F_f / F_r$ exactly.

**Conversion logic:** This derivation shows the *mechanical origin* of why the front brakes do more work during heavy braking (load shifts forward). The ECU implements the right-hand side as a *lookup table* indexed by $a$ (or $\xi$), giving a target $P_r$ for any given $P_f$.

## 3.3 Derivation: TCS Engine Torque Reduction Request

**Step 1 — Detect driven-wheel slip.**
For RWD, driven wheels are $RL$ and $RR$. TCS active when:

$$\lambda_{drive} = \frac{V_{wheel,avg(drive)} - V_{ref}}{V_{ref}} > \lambda_{thresh} = 0.15\ (15\%)$$

**Step 2 — Calculate desired wheel deceleration.**
Target slip $\lambda^* = 0.10$. Required change in wheel speed:

$$\Delta V_{wheel} = V_{ref} \cdot (\lambda_{drive} - \lambda^*)$$

**Step 3 — Convert to torque at the wheel.**
Wheel moment of inertia $J_{wheel}$ (tire + hub + brake disc, typically 1.0–1.5 kg·m²):

$$T_{brake,req} = J_{wheel} \cdot \frac{\Delta V_{wheel}}{r_{tire} \cdot \Delta t} + F_{load} \cdot r_{tire}$$

**Step 4 — Account for drivetrain ratio to get engine torque request.**

$$T_{eng,req} = \frac{T_{shaft,req}}{i_{gear} \cdot i_{final}} = T_{eng,current} - \Delta T_{eng}$$

This $\Delta T_{eng}$ is sent to the ECM via CAN message (e.g., CAN ID 0x1A0, signal "TCS_Torque_Red" — 16-bit, 0.1 N·m/bit).

**Step 5 — Hybrid brake + engine intervention.**
If engine torque reduction alone is insufficient, apply *selective* brake pressure to the spinning wheel:

$$P_{wheel,spin} = \frac{T_{brake,req}}{A_{piston} \cdot \mu_{pad} \cdot r_{eff}}$$

**Conversion logic:** The TCS algorithm walks *up* the slip-control hierarchy — first throttle close, then spark retard, then fuel cut, finally brake — minimizing driveline shock and perceived intervention by the driver.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **Problem:** A car of mass $m = 1500$ kg, wheelbase $L = 2.6$ m, CG height $h_{cg} = 0.55$ m, $b = 1.4$ m, $a = 1.2$ m, decelerates at $a_{veh} = 0.7\,g$. Calculate the ideal front/rear brake-force ratio and the rear-axle brake force if the front brakes produce $F_f = 8000$ N.

**Step 1:** $\xi = a_{veh}/g = 0.7$

**Step 2:** Apply the derived formula:

$$\frac{F_f}{F_r} = \frac{b + \xi \cdot h_{cg}}{a - \xi \cdot h_{cg}} = \frac{1.4 + 0.7 \times 0.55}{1.2 - 0.7 \times 0.55}$$

$$= \frac{1.4 + 0.385}{1.2 - 0.385} = \frac{1.785}{0.815} = 2.190$$

**Step 3:** Rear brake force:

$$F_r = \frac{F_f}{2.190} = \frac{8000}{2.190} = 3653\ \text{N}$$

**Step 4:** Verify against dynamic loads:

$$W_f^{dyn} = \frac{1500 \times 9.81 \times 1.4 + 1500 \times 6.867 \times 0.55}{2.6} = \frac{20601 + 5665}{2.6} = 10102\ \text{N}$$

$$W_r^{dyn} = \frac{1500 \times 9.81 \times 1.2 - 1500 \times 6.867 \times 0.55}{2.6} = \frac{17658 - 5665}{2.6} = 4612\ \text{N}$$

**Step 5:** Check that $F_r \leq \mu \cdot W_r^{dyn}$ (lock prevention):

$$F_r = 3653\ \text{N}, \quad \mu \cdot W_r^{dyn} = 0.8 \times 4612 = 3690\ \text{N}$$

Since $F_r < 3690$ N, the rear wheels will **not lock** — the EBD calculation is valid.

**Conversion logic:** This worked example is precisely the kind of "calculate EBD split" sub-question that appears in KTU Part B (14-mark) papers. Memorize the formula and the two verification steps (load transfer + lock margin).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of Integrated ABS / EBD / TCS ECU

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WSSFL[WSS Front Left] --> sigcon1[Signal Conditioner\nFilter + Comparator]
    WSSFR[WSS Front Right] --> sigcon2[Signal Conditioner]
    WSSRL[WSS Rear Left] --> sigcon3[Signal Conditioner]
    WSSRR[WSS Rear Right] --> sigcon4[Signal Conditioner]
    BPS[Brake Pedal Sensor\nDual Hall] --> sigcon5[Signal Conditioner]
    YRS[Yaw Rate Sensor\nMEMS] --> sigcon5
    sigcon1 --> MCU1[Slave MCU\nDSP Core]
    sigcon2 --> MCU1
    sigcon3 --> MCU1
    sigcon4 --> MCU1
    sigcon5 --> MCU1
    MCU1 <--> SPI[SPI Bus\nRedundant]
    MCU1 <--> MCU2[Master MCU\nLockstep]
    MCU2 --> pidout[PID + State Machine\nSlip Controller]
    pidout --> drv1[Inlet Solenoid Driver\nH-Bridge x 4]
    pidout --> drv2[Outlet Solenoid Driver\nH-Bridge x 4]
    pidout --> drv3[Pump Motor Driver\nMOSFET Half-Bridge]
    drv1 --> solmod[Hydraulic Modulator\n8 Solenoids + Pump]
    drv2 --> solmod
    drv3 --> solmod
    solmod --> whlFL[Wheel FL Brake]
    solmod --> whlFR[Wheel FR Brake]
    solmod --> whlRL[Wheel RL Brake]
    solmod --> whlRR[Wheel RR Brake]
    MCU2 <--> CAN1[CAN Bus to ECM\nTorque Request]
    MCU2 <--> CAN2[CAN Bus to Cluster\nABS/TCS Lamps]
    MCU2 <--> WDG[External Watchdog\nTLE4278]
    BPS --> ECU2[ECM Torque Arbitration]
    ECU2 --> ENG[Engine\nThrottle + Spark + Fuel]
```

## 4.2 ABS Closed-Loop State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> PressureBuild: Brake pedal pressed
    PressureBuild --> PressureHold: lambda > 15 percent
    PressureHold --> PressureRelease: lambda > 30 percent
    PressureRelease --> PressureBuild: lambda < 5 percent
    PressureHold --> PressureBuild: lambda < 5 percent
    PressureRelease --> PressureHold: lambda within 10 to 20 percent
    PressureBuild --> [*]: Brake pedal released
    PressureHold --> [*]: Brake pedal released
    PressureRelease --> [*]: Brake pedal released
    note right of PressureBuild: Inlet OPEN\nOutlet CLOSED\nPump OFF
    note right of PressureHold: Inlet CLOSED\nOutlet CLOSED\nPump OFF
    note right of PressureRelease: Inlet CLOSED\nOutlet OPEN\nPump ON
```

## 4.3 WSS Input Signal Conditioning Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    tone[Tone Ring\n48 teeth] --> vr[VR Sensor\nVariable Reluctance]
    vr --> z1[Zener 5.1V\nESD Protection]
    z1 --> z2[Common Mode Choke\n100 uH]
    z2 --> c1[DC Blocking Cap\n100 nF]
    c1 --> r1[Pull-up Resistor\n10 kOhm]
    c1 --> cmp1[Comparator\nLM2903]
    cmp1 --> r2[Hysteresis Resistor\n100 kOhm]
    cmp2[Reference 2.5V] --> cmp1
    cmp1 --> t1[Schmitt Trigger\n74HC14]
    t1 --> mcu[ECU Timer Capture\nInput Pin]
    t1 --> iso[Digital Isolator\nADuM1411]
    iso --> mcu
```

## 4.4 Solenoid H-Bridge Driver Circuit (Per Wheel Channel)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pwm[PWM Input\nfrom MCU\n0 to 100 percent] --> logic[Driver Logic\nIR2104]
    logic --> hs1[High-Side MOSFET\nIRF7507]
    logic --> ls1[Low-Side MOSFET\nIRF7507]
    hs1 --> sol[Solenoid\n3.2 Ohm 1.5 mH]
    ls1 --> sol
    sol --> cs[Current Sense Resistor\n0.05 Ohm]
    cs --> gnd[GND]
    cs --> amp[Op-Amp Gain 20\nINA180]
    amp --> adc[ADC Channel\nMCU]
    bat[12V Battery] --> hs1
    bat --> tvd[TVS Diode\nP6KE36A]
    tvd --> bat
```

## 4.5 HIL Test Bench Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pcp[PC with ControlDesk] --> rt[Real-Time Simulator\ndSPACE DS1006]
    rt --> daca[DAC Channels\nWheel Speed x 4]
    rt --> daci[DAC Channels\nBrake Pressure x 4]
    daca --> hcu[Real HCU\nHydraulic Modulator]
    daci --> hcu
    hcu --> adc[ADC Feedback to RT\nPressure Sensors]
    hcu --> can1[CAN Bus]
    can1 --> rt
    pcp --> mon[Scope + Data Logger]
    adc --> mon
    hcu --> obd[OBD-II Port\nFault Codes]
    obd --> pcp
```

## 4.6 Fault Diagnosis Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Ignition ON] --> selftest[ABS Self-Test 1.5s]
    selftest --> ok1{Lamp OFF?}
    ok1 -->|Yes| run[Normal Operation]
    ok1 -->|No| readdtc[Read DTC via OBD-II]
    readdtc --> dtc1{Code Type?}
    dtc1 -->|C0040| flvss[FL WSS Open / Short]
    dtc1 -->|C0045| frvss[FR WSS Open / Short]
    dtc1 -->|C0050| rlvss[RL WSS Open / Short]
    dtc1 -->|C0055| rrvss[RR WSS Open / Short]
    dtc1 -->|C0110| pumpflt[Pump Motor Fault]
    dtc1 -->|C0131| absmaster[Master Cylinder Pressure Sensor]
    dtc1 -->|C0561| absdisable[ABS Disabled by ESC DTC]
    flvss --> check1[Check resistance 1000 to 2500 Ohm]
    check1 --> replace1[Replace WSS or harness]
    frvss --> check2[Check resistance 1000 to 2500 Ohm]
    check2 --> replace2[Replace WSS or harness]
    pumpflt --> check3[Check motor resistance 0.5 Ohm]
    check3 --> replace3[Replace HCU]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
> **Q1.** Define wheel slip ratio. What is its ideal range for ABS operation and why?

**Model Answer:**
Wheel slip ratio $\lambda$ is defined as the normalized difference between the estimated vehicle reference speed $V_{ref}$ and the actual wheel center speed $V_{wheel}$:

$$\lambda = \frac{V_{ref} - V_{wheel}}{V_{ref}}$$

It is dimensionless and ranges from 0 (free rolling) to 1 (fully locked). The ideal operating range for ABS is **10%–30%** because this is the region where the tire-road friction coefficient $\mu$ is at or near its **peak (Pacejka peak)**, providing maximum braking force while still allowing the wheel to rotate so the driver retains steering control. Below 10% the brake force is sub-optimal; above 30% the wheel is heading toward lock-up and lateral grip collapses. **3 marks** (1 mark definition, 1 mark range, 1 mark justification with peak μ reason).

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Retest]**
> **Q2.** List the three pressure-modulation states of an ABS hydraulic modulator and the corresponding valve positions for each state.

**Model Answer:**
1. **Pressure Build (Apply):** Inlet valve **OPEN** (energized), Outlet valve **CLOSED** (de-energized), Pump **OFF** or low speed. Master cylinder pressure flows to wheel cylinder → pressure rises.
2. **Pressure Hold:** Inlet valve **CLOSED** (de-energized), Outlet valve **CLOSED** (de-energized), Pump **OFF**. Wheel cylinder is isolated → pressure is constant.
3. **Pressure Release (Dump):** Inlet valve **CLOSED** (de-energized), Outlet valve **OPEN** (energized), Pump **ON** (high speed). Fluid is vented to accumulator and returned to master cylinder via the return pump → pressure falls.

**3 marks** (1 mark per state, including valve logic).

---

## Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

### Question A: 14 Marks
**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the functional architecture of an integrated ABS / EBD / Traction Control ECU. Label the sensor inputs, ECU processing block, and hydraulic actuator stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A passenger car of mass $m = 1400$ kg, wheelbase $L = 2.55$ m, $h_{cg} = 0.5$ m, $b = 1.35$ m, $a = 1.20$ m is braking at a deceleration of $0.65\,g$. Calculate (i) the ideal front-to-rear brake force ratio, and (ii) the rear axle brake force if the front brakes deliver $F_f = 7500$ N. Verify that rear-wheel lock-up is avoided for $\mu = 0.85$. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Block Diagram and Functional Description **(7 marks)**

The integrated ECU has **four** major subsystems:

1. **Sensor Input Stage (2 marks):**
   - 4 × Wheel Speed Sensors (VR or Hall) at each wheel
   - Brake Pedal Position Sensor (dual Hall, redundant)
   - Yaw-rate + lateral acceleration sensor (MEMS)
   - Master cylinder pressure sensor (strain gauge)
   - CAN bus from ECM (engine speed, torque, throttle)

2. **ECU Processing Stage (2 marks):**
   - Dual lockstep microcontroller (e.g., Infineon TC26x with Aurix)
   - Signal conditioning: WSS frequency-to-speed conversion, slip calculation $\lambda_i = (V_{ref} - V_{wheel,i})/V_{ref}$
   - State machine: Build → Hold → Release (15–25 Hz loop)
   - PID controller generating PWM duty cycle for each solenoid pair
   - EBD lookup table indexed by deceleration $a$
   - TCS torque request: $\Delta T_{eng}$ via CAN to ECM
   - Diagnostic / fault storage (OBD-II compliant, ISO 14229 UDS)

3. **Hydraulic Actuator Stage (2 marks):**
   - 8 × solenoid valves (2 per wheel: inlet + outlet)
   - H-bridge drivers with current sensing
   - Return pump (DC motor, 12 V, ~30 A peak)
   - Accumulator + low-pressure reservoir

4. **Communication & Safety (1 mark):**
   - CAN 2.0B / CAN-FD bus
   - External watchdog (TLE4278 / MAX6369)
   - ABS/TCS warning lamps driven by ECU

#### Part (b) — EBD Numerical **(7 marks)**

**Step 1: Compute the dynamic load transfer.** $\xi = a/g = 0.65$

$$W_f^{dyn} = \frac{m \cdot g \cdot b + m \cdot a \cdot h_{cg}}{L}$$

$$= \frac{1400 \times 9.81 \times 1.35 + 1400 \times 6.38 \times 0.5}{2.55}$$

$$= \frac{18540 + 4466}{2.55} = 9023\ \text{N}$$

$$W_r^{dyn} = \frac{1400 \times 9.81 \times 1.20 - 1400 \times 6.38 \times 0.5}{2.55}$$

$$= \frac{16481 - 4466}{2.55} = 4713\ \text{N}$$

**Step 2: Ideal brake-force ratio.** **(2 marks)**

$$\frac{F_f}{F_r} = \frac{b + \xi \cdot h_{cg}}{a - \xi \cdot h_{cg}} = \frac{1.35 + 0.65 \times 0.5}{1.20 - 0.65 \times 0.5} = \frac{1.675}{0.875} = 1.914$$

**Step 3: Rear brake force.** **(1 mark)**

$$F_r = \frac{F_f}{1.914} = \frac{7500}{1.914} = 3918\ \text{N}$$

**Step 4: Lock-up verification.** **(2 marks)**

Maximum available friction at rear axle: $F_{r,max} = \mu \cdot W_r^{dyn} = 0.85 \times 4713 = 4006$ N.

Since $F_r = 3918$ N $< 4006$ N, **rear wheels will not lock** (margin = 88 N = 2.2%). The EBD split is valid.

**Step 5: Conclusion.** **(1 mark)**
The EBD algorithm must request a rear pressure that is 1/1.914 = 52.2% of the front pressure (assuming equal caliper/rotor geometry). If the master cylinder is supplying $P_{master}$, then $P_r = 0.522 \cdot P_{master}$.

---

### Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative)

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the closed-loop slip control algorithm for a Traction Control System. Discuss engine torque reduction and selective brake intervention. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a wheel speed sensor (WSS) input signal-conditioning circuit for an ABS ECU. Specify the components for ESD protection, common-mode filtering, comparator, hysteresis, and digital isolation. Justify each stage. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — TCS Algorithm **(7 marks)**

**TCS Closed-Loop Block Diagram (3 marks):**
Reference vehicle speed $V_{ref}$ is compared with driven-wheel speed $V_{drive}$ (rear wheels for RWD, front for FWD, all four for AWD). The slip error:

$$e_{slip} = \lambda_{drive} - \lambda^*$$

is fed to a PI controller (no derivative, to avoid sensitivity to sensor noise on acceleration):

$$u(t) = K_p \cdot e_{slip}(t) + K_i \int e_{slip}(t)\,dt$$

**Hierarchy of Interventions (2 marks):**
1. **Throttle Closure** (first, smoothest) — Drive-by-wire throttle blade angle reduced.
2. **Spark Retard** (second) — Ignition timing retarded by 5°–15°.
3. **Fuel Cut** (third) — Cylinder fuel injection inhibited briefly.
4. **Selective Brake Apply** (last, harshest) — Hydraulic pressure applied *only* to the spinning wheel.

**TCS Engine Torque Request (1 mark):**
The torque request to the ECM via CAN is:

$$\Delta T_{eng} = T_{eng,current} - T_{eng,target}$$

ECM arbitrates this against driver pedal demand and reports back actual torque achieved.

**Lamp Activation (1 mark):**
TCS active lamp illuminates; if intervention is excessive, a "TCS Off" request allows driver to disable via switch.

#### Part (b) — WSS Circuit Design **(7 marks)**

| Stage | Component | Value / Part | Justification | Marks |
|-------|-----------|--------------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | TVS Diode | P6KE36CA | Suppresses ISO 7637-2 load-dump transients (clamp 36 V) | 1 |
| 2 | Common-Mode Choke | 100 µH, 2-line | Attenuates common-mode EMI from ignition system | 1 |
| 3 | DC-Blocking Capacitor | 100 nF X7R | Removes VR sensor DC bias; AC-coupled signal | 0.5 |
| 4 | Pull-up / Pull-down | 10 kΩ | Defines idle state; reduces input impedance | 0.5 |
| 5 | Comparator | LM2903 | Converts sine (VR) to square wave (TTL levels) | 1 |
| 6 | Reference Divider | 2.5 V, 1% resistor | Sets 50% threshold for zero-crossing detection | 0.5 |
| 7 | Hysteresis Resistor | 100 kΩ | Prevents chatter near threshold; noise immunity | 0.5 |
| 8 | Schmitt Trigger | 74HC14 | Squares up edges; clean edges to MCU timer capture | 0.5 |
| 9 | Digital Isolator | ADuM1411 (4-channel) | Galvanic isolation between sensor and MCU (2.5 kV) | 1 |
| 10 | Decoupling | 100 nF + 10 µF | Local rail stability for the comparator and isolator | 0.5 |

**Design Rationale (extra 0.5 mark for narrative):**
- VR sensor output is sinusoidal: amplitude 0.5–10 V peak, frequency 0–3 kHz proportional to wheel speed.
- Failure modes to guard against: open circuit, short to ground, short to battery, tooth-ring damage (variable air gap).
- The hysteresis resistor sets $\Delta V \approx V_{ref}/(1 + R_f/R_{in}) \approx 50$ mV, sufficient to ignore sensor noise.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the pump:** Many students describe the 3 ABS states *without* mentioning the **return pump** that recovers fluid during the Release phase. The pump is essential — without it, fluid would be lost and the master cylinder would bottom out after 3–4 cycles. **Deduct 1 mark** if pump state is not stated for each phase.
> 2. **Mixing up NC/NO:** A frequent error is stating that the *inlet* valve is normally closed. The **inlet valve is Normally Open (NO)** — meaning it is open when de-energized, so that brake pressure can build naturally. The **outlet valve is Normally Closed (NC)**. Confusing these is a **2-mark penalty** in KTU papers.
> 3. **Slip-ratio denominator:** Always $V_{ref}$ in the denominator, **never** $V_{wheel}$ (that would be the inverse slip, used in some traction control papers but **not** the KTU convention). Check your formula before writing.
> 4. **EBD ratio sign check:** If $a > b \cdot g / h_{cg}$, the denominator $a - \xi \cdot h_{cg}$ becomes negative — physically impossible. This indicates the deceleration exceeds the geometric lock-up limit and the vehicle will spin regardless. Note this in your answer if relevant.
> 5. **Forgetting units:** $h_{cg}$, $a$, $b$, $L$ must be in the **same unit system** (m, m, m, m). Mixing cm with m is a common 0.5-mark deduction.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Wheel slip** is the fundamental decision variable for ABS/TCS: $\lambda = (V_{ref} - V_{wheel})/V_{ref}$; target window is **10%–30%**.
- **ABS operates as a 3-state machine**: *Build → Hold → Release*, with the *return pump* ON only in Release.
- **Inlet valve is NO, outlet valve is NC** in the standard Bosch/Continental HCU architecture.
- **EBD formula** $\dfrac{F_f}{F_r} = \dfrac{b + \xi h_{cg}}{a - \xi h_{cg}}$ is the single most-tested equation — memorize with units check.
- **TCS slip is positive when wheel is faster than $V_{ref}$** — opposite sign convention to ABS. TCS target is typically 10% (not 15% as in ABS).
- **WSS signal conditioning chain**: VR sensor → TVS → CMC → DC-block → comparator with hysteresis → digital isolator → MCU timer capture.
- **Solenoid drivers are H-bridges** with current sense and freewheeling diodes; the back-EMF of the 1.5 mH solenoid can spike to >60 V without proper flyback.
- **Functional safety**: ABS/TCS are **ASIL-D**; design must include lockstep MCUs, external watchdog, and fail-safe (de-energized) valve defaults.
- **HIL testing** uses real-time simulators (dSPACE, NI VT System) to feed simulated WSS and pressure signals into the real ECU; this catches 70%+ of integration bugs before road testing.
- **Standard test sequence**: (1) Ignition ON self-test → all lamps ON then OFF, (2) Static sensor check via OBD-II, (3) Low-speed roll test on dynamometer (0–10 km/h), (4) High-speed ABS stop test on skid pad at 80–120 km/h, (5) Diagnostic scan for DTCs.
- **OBD-II DTC prefix**: ABS/TCS/ESC faults use **C-codes** (Chassis), e.g., C0040 = Front-Left WSS circuit malfunction.
- **Pacejka Magic Formula** defines the tire-force curve; peak $\mu$ is the design centerpoint for slip control.
- **CAN message arbitration**: TCS torque request is a low-latency (10 ms) CAN message to ECM with a 16-bit signal at 0.1 N·m resolution.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and testing of circuit for electronic triggering equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAUL607 - AUTOTRONICS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Design and testing of circuit for electronic triggering equipment ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Electronic Triggering Equipment (ETE)** refers to a class of semiconductor-based circuits used to generate precisely timed electrical pulses that initiate or synchronize critical automotive and industrial processes. In the context of the KTU Autotronics Laboratory, an **electronic triggering circuit** is a pulse-generating network — built around active switching devices such as **Bipolar Junction Transistors (BJT)**, **Silicon Controlled Rectifiers (SCR/Thyristor)**, **Unijunction Transistors (UJT)**, or the **555 Timer IC** — that converts a continuous DC supply into a sharply defined trigger pulse synchronized to a reference signal (e.g., crankshaft position, cam position, or an external pulse train). In an automotive ignition subsystem, the electronic trigger replaces the mechanical **breaker points** of the conventional Kettering ignition system, eliminating contact bounce, wear, and timing drift. The output pulse is used to fire a **thyristor** in a **Capacitor Discharge Ignition (CDI)** system or to switch a power transistor in a **Transistorized Coil Ignition (TCI)** system. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Doorbell with a Smart Watchdog:** > Imagine an old-fashioned doorbell where you must keep pressing a mechanical button to make it ring. Over time, the button wears out, the contacts corrode, and the timing becomes irregular. Now replace the mechanical button with a small electronic "watchdog" that watches a rotating shaft (via a magnetic pickup). Each time a tooth on a gear passes the sensor, the watchdog instantly sends a sharp, crisp electrical pulse — perfectly timed, never tired, and lasting only a few milliseconds. This watchdog **is** the electronic triggering circuit. The "doorbell" it triggers is the ignition coil (in a car) or a thyristor (in industrial motor control). ### 1.3 Core Design Objectives | Objective | Engineering Rationale | |---|---| | **Precise Edge Detection** | Convert slow analog sensor signals into crisp digital trigger edges | | **Sharper Trigger Pulse** | Reduce rise time (< 1 µs) so SCR turns on with minimum dV/dt loss | | **Adjustable Timing Advance** | Allow dwell-angle / advance control based on RPM and load | | **Noise Immunity** | Use Schmitt trigger or comparator with hysteresis to reject EMI | | **Temperature Stability** | Compensate drift so timing does not wander across −20 °C to +80 °C | | **Low Power Drain** | Dissipation typically **< 200 mW** in standby | ### 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (Bold Constants) > [!IMPORTANT] > Key benchmark values used in the KTU valuation key: > - **Trigger pulse width (t_p):** 50 µs – 500 µs (typical **200 µs**) > - **Trigger pulse amplitude:** **3 V – 12 V** (logic-level) or **5 V – 20 V** (SCR gate drive) > - **Rise time (t_r):** **< 1 µs** for SCR-grade triggering > - **Operating frequency range:** **0.1 Hz – 20 kHz** (covers idle to redline RPM) > - **Supply voltage:** **+12 V DC** (automotive) or **+5 V DC** (logic lab) > - **Hysteresis voltage (V_H):** **0.5 V – 2 V** for clean switching --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Blocks of an Electronic Triggering System A typical design implemented in the Autotronics Lab consists of **four functional stages**: 1. **Sensor / Pickup Stage** — Magnetic pickup (variable reluctance), Hall-effect sensor, or optical interrupter that produces a position-dependent waveform. 2. **Signal Conditioning Stage** — Comparator with hysteresis (Schmitt trigger) to clean the waveform and convert it to a clean logic-level square wave. 3. **Timing / Pulse-Forming Stage** — 555 timer monostable multivibrator (one-shot) or UJT relaxation oscillator that produces a pulse of defined width on each trigger edge. 4. **Output / Driver Stage** — Transistor or SCR driver that delivers the required gate current to the load (ignition coil, motor, solenoid). > [!NOTE] > **Why four stages and not one?** Each stage performs a *single, well-defined* job. Separation allows independent tuning (sensor gain, hysteresis, pulse width, drive strength) — exactly the modular approach KTU examiners expect you to justify in viva-voce. ### 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic - **Step 1 — Sensing:** A rotating toothed reluctor wheel passes the magnetic pickup. As the air-gap flux changes, a sinusoidal EMF is induced (Faraday's Law). This voltage is roughly **±2 V to ±5 V** peak. - **Step 2 — Comparison:** The signal is fed to a comparator (LM311 or op-amp). When the input crosses the reference threshold **V_ref**, the comparator flips state. Hysteresis prevents chatter near the zero-crossing. - **Step 3 — One-Shot Trigger:** Each comparator transition triggers a **monostable multivibrator** (e.g., 555 timer). The output pulse width is set by an external **R-C** network. - **Step 4 — Power Switching:** The monostable output drives the base of a power transistor or the gate of an SCR. The load (a 12 V lamp, relay coil, or model ignition coil) is energized for the duration of the pulse. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $V_{\text{out}} = A_{OL} \cdot (V_{+} - V_{-})$ | Op-amp comparator output swing | V | | 2 | $V_{H} = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Schmitt trigger hysteresis width (inverting) | V | | 3 | $t_p = 1.1 \cdot R \cdot C$ | 555 Monostable pulse width | s | | 4 | $f = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C}$ | 555 Astable frequency | Hz | | 5 | $T = t_p^{\text{high}} + t_p^{\text{low}}$ | Period of one trigger cycle | s | | 6 | $Q = C \cdot V$ | Capacitor charge transferred per pulse | C | | 7 | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ | Energy stored in CDI capacitor | J | | 8 | $I_{GT} = \dfrac{V_{GT}}{R_{G}}$ | SCR gate trigger current | A | | 9 | $\tau = R \cdot C$ | RC time constant | s | | 10 | $V(t) = V_{\infty} - (V_{\infty} - V_0) \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$ | Universal capacitor charging law | V | | 11 | $f_{\text{RPM}} = \dfrac{N \cdot p}{120}$ | Pulse frequency from RPM (N teeth/rev) | Hz | | 12 | $\eta = \dfrac{t_{\text{on}}}{T}$ | Duty cycle of trigger output | unitless | > [!IMPORTANT] > In KTU board answers, **always state the units** next to numerical answers. A formula without units can lose up to **2 marks** in the 14-mark questions. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive:** Electronic Ignition, Glow-plug control in diesels, Injector drivers. - **Industrial:** SCR-triggered motor soft-starters, phase-angle controllers, light dimmers. - **Robotics:** Stepper-motor pulse trains, servo positioning triggers. - **Power Electronics:** Gate-driver circuits for IGBTs and MOSFETs in inverters and UPS systems. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation — Pulse Width of 555 Monostable Trigger > [!NOTE] > **Problem Context:** Design a monostable 555 timer circuit that produces a trigger pulse of width **t_p = 220 µs** using a timing capacitor of **C = 10 nF**. Find the required timing resistor R. The 555 monostable charges the external capacitor C through resistor R from 0 V toward V_CC. The output HIGH period ends the moment the capacitor voltage reaches **2/3 V_CC** (the internal threshold). Using the universal charging law: $$ V_C(t) = V_{CC} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t / RC}\right) $$ Setting $V_C(t_p) = \frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$ and solving: $$ \frac{2}{3} V_{CC} = V_{CC} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t_p / RC}\right) $$ Divide both sides by $V_{CC}$: $$ \frac{2}{3} = 1 - e^{-t_p / RC} $$ Rearrange to isolate the exponential term: $$ e^{-t_p / RC} = 1 - \frac{2}{3} = \frac{1}{3} $$ Take the natural logarithm of both sides: $$ -\frac{t_p}{RC} = \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) = -\ln 3 $$ Cancel the negative signs: $$ \frac{t_p}{RC} = \ln 3 \approx 1.0986 $$ Therefore the canonical 555 monostable equation is: $$ t_p = \ln 3 \cdot R C \approx 1.0986 \cdot R C $$ The standard textbook approximation (and the one KTU expects) is: $$ t_p = 1.1 \cdot R \cdot C $$ **Solving for R** given $t_p = 220~\mu s$ and $C = 10~nF$: $$ R = \frac{t_p}{1.1 \cdot C} = \frac{220 \times 10^{-6}}{1.1 \times 10 \times 10^{-9}} $$ $$ R = \frac{220 \times 10^{-6}}{11 \times 10^{-9}} = 20 \times 10^{3}~\Omega = 20~k\Omega $$ $$ \boxed{R = 20~k\Omega} $$ ### 3.2 Derivation — Trigger Frequency from a Toothed Wheel > [!NOTE] > **Problem Context:** A 4-stroke single-cylinder engine running at **N = 3000 RPM** has a trigger wheel with **Z = 30 teeth**. Compute the pulse frequency delivered to the electronic trigger circuit.
