Plot **regen torque vs. speed**, **charging power vs. SOC**, and **efficiency vs. speed**. > **Precautions (1 mark):** > - Ensure battery SOC ≤ **80 %** before test (so charging headroom exists). > - Verify dynamometer cooling water flow. > - Wear HV-insulated gloves; lockout-tagout HV bus. --- ### (b) Numerical Tabulation (7 marks) **Constants:** $$ \Delta E_{kin} = \frac{1}{2} m (v_i^2 - v_f^2) ; \quad E_{rec} = V \cdot I \cdot t ; \quad \eta = \frac{E_{rec}}{\Delta E_{kin}} $$ #### Trial 1: 60 → 20 km/h (i.e., 16.67 → 5.56 m/s) $$ \Delta E_{kin} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1600 \cdot (16.67^2 - 5.56^2) = 800 \cdot (277.89 - 30.91) = 800 \cdot 246.98 = 197{,}584 \text{ J} $$ $$ E_{rec} = 360 \cdot 60 \cdot 5 = 108{,}000 \text{ J} $$ $$ \eta_1 = \frac{108{,}000}{197{,}584} = 54.66\% $$ #### Trial 2: 80 → 40 km/h (i.e., 22.22 → 11.11 m/s) $$ \Delta E_{kin} = 800 \cdot (493.83 - 123.46) = 800 \cdot 370.37 = 296{,}296 \text{ J} $$ $$ E_{rec} = 360 \cdot 90 \cdot 6 = 194{,}400 \text{ J} $$ $$ \eta_2 = \frac{194{,}400}{296{,}296} = 65.62\% $$ #### Trial 3: 100 → 50 km/h (i.e., 27.78 → 13.89 m/s) $$ \Delta E_{kin} = 800 \cdot (771.73 - 192.93) = 800 \cdot 578.80 = 463{,}040 \text{ J} $$ $$ E_{rec} = 360 \cdot 110 \cdot 7 = 277{,}200 \text{ J} $$ $$ \eta_3 = \frac{277{,}200}{463{,}040} = 59.86\% $$ #### Final Tabulated Result (1 mark) | Trial | $\Delta E_{kin}$ (kJ) | $E_{rec}$ (kJ) | $\eta_{reg}$ (%) | |-------|------------------------|------------------|--------------------| | 1 | 197.58 | 108.00 | **54.66** | | 2 | 296.30 | 194.40 | **65.62** | | 3 | 463.04 | 277.20 | **59.86** | > **Inference (extra):** Efficiency peaks at moderate speed (Trial 2). At very low speeds (Trial 1), back-EMF is insufficient to overcome battery voltage; at very high speeds (Trial 3), motor iron losses and battery charge acceptance limit efficiency. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Areas (Examiners report these every year):** > 1. **Unit conversion from km/h to m/s** — Students forget to convert and write $\tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ with $v$ in km/h. **Loss: 1 mark per question.** > 2. **Forgetting the (½) factor** — Writing $m v^2$ instead of $\tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$. **Loss: 1 mark.** > 3. **Not stating SOC limit condition** — Students forget to mention that regen is **disabled when SOC ≥ 95 %**. **Loss: 1 mark in theory answers.** > 4. **Confusing motor torque sign** — Failing to state that regenerative torque is **negative** (opposing motion). **Loss: 1 mark.** > 5. **In viva, not knowing $\eta_{reg} = E_{bat}/\Delta E_{kin}$** — this is the **single most asked formula** in regen-braking viva. Memorize it. > 6. **Skipping friction brake blending** — Many answers describe only motor mode and forget to mention hydraulic fallback. **Loss: 2 marks in long answers.** --- ## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Definition:** Regen braking converts kinetic energy → electrical energy via motor-generator action. - ✅ **Core formula:** $\eta_{reg} = \dfrac{E_{bat,recovered}}{\Delta E_{kin}} = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot t}{\tfrac{1}{2} m (v_i^2 - v_f^2)}$ - ✅ **Always convert km/h → m/s** before plugging into $\tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$. - ✅ **SOC limit:** Regen disabled when SOC ≥ **95 %** (BMS protection). - ✅ **Speed limit:** Regen disabled when speed < **10 km/h** (insufficient back-EMF). - ✅ **Torque sign:** $T_{reg}$ is **negative** (opposes wheel rotation). - ✅ **Brake blending:** ECU blends regen + friction; smoothness is rated via pedal-feel calibration. - ✅ **Test rig:** Motor + Inverter + Battery + BMS + Dynamometer + DAQ. - ✅ **Instruments:** Torque transducer, current clamp, voltage divider, RPM sensor, CAN bus logger. - ✅ **Efficiency range:** **50 – 70 %** typical; up to **80 %** under lab conditions. - ✅ **Typical $V_{bat}$:** 350 – 400 V (modern passenger EVs). - ✅ **Graphs to plot:** (i) $T_{reg}$ vs. $\omega$, (ii) $\eta_{reg}$ vs. $v$, (iii) SOC vs. time. - ✅ **Safety:** HV gloves, interlock, battery venting check, dynamometer water cooling ON. ---

topics.title: Understanding and diagnosing Controller Area Network (CAN) bus systems used in modern vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL607 - AUTOTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Understanding and diagnosing Controller Area Network (CAN) bus systems used in modern vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Controller Area Network (CAN bus)** is a robust, multi-master, message-based serial communication protocol originally developed by **Robert Bosch GmbH in 1986** and standardized under **ISO 11898**. It is specifically designed to allow microcontrollers and Electronic Control Units (ECUs) to communicate with each other within a vehicle **without the need for a host computer**, using a **two-wire differential bus** (CAN_H and CAN_L).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** CAN is a *broadcast type* bus protocol where every node (ECU) receives every transmission and filters messages based on the message identifier (ID). It is a **CSMA/CR + NRZ** protocol (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Resolution, Non-Return-to-Zero encoding).

The protocol is governed by the **Open Systems Interconnection (OSI) model** and primarily implements the **Physical Layer (Layer 1)** and the **Data Link Layer (Layer 2)** of the OSI stack. Modern passenger cars use CAN for communication between critical ECUs such as the Engine Control Module (ECM), Transmission Control Module (TCM), Anti-lock Braking System (ABS), Body Control Module (BCM), and the instrument cluster.

### 1.2 Key Variants of CAN

| Variant | Standard | Bit Rate | Identifier | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Classical CAN (2.0A) | ISO 11898-1 | Up to **1 Mbps** | **11-bit** ID | Body, Chassis |
| Classical CAN (2.0B) | ISO 11898-1 | Up to **1 Mbps** | **29-bit** ID | Powertrain, Extended |
| CAN FD (Flexible Data-rate) | ISO 11898-1:2015 | Up to **5 Mbps** | 11/29-bit | Modern vehicles |
| Low-Speed CAN (LS-CAN) | ISO 11898-3 | **125 kbps** | 11/29-bit | Comfort electronics |
| Single-Wire CAN (SWC) | SAE J2411 | **33.3 kbps** | 11-bit | Body modules |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The "Conference Call" Model**
>
> Imagine **ten experts** sitting in a **single conference room** with **one shared microphone line**. Any expert can choose to speak at any time, but before speaking, they **listen** to determine if someone else is already talking. If two experts start speaking simultaneously, the one with the **higher priority topic** (lower CAN ID) continues, while the other stops and retries. Everyone in the room **hears every message** but only acts on messages relevant to their department.

This is exactly how CAN works:
- **Shared Bus Line** = The conference microphone line (CAN_H and CAN_L twisted pair)
- **ECUs** = The experts in the room
- **Message ID (Priority)** = The topic importance (lower ID = higher priority)
- **Broadcast** = Everyone hears everything

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two critical physical facts:**
> - **Differential Voltage** `V_diff = V_{CAN_H} - V_{CAN_L}` is the **decoded logic level**:
>   - `V_diff ≈ 0 V` → **Recessive bit (Logic 1)**
>   - `V_diff ≈ 2 V` → **Dominant bit (Logic 0)**
> - The bus must be terminated with **120 Ω** resistors at **both** physical ends to prevent signal reflections.

### 1.4 Where CAN is Used in a Modern Vehicle

- **Powertrain CAN (HS-CAN, 500 kbps)**: Engine, Transmission, ABS, ESP
- **Chassis CAN (HS-CAN, 500 kbps)**: Steering, Suspension, Brakes
- **Body CAN (MS-CAN, 125 kbps)**: Windows, Locks, Lights, Climate
- **Diagnostic CAN (500 kbps)**: OBD-II Port (Pin 6 = CAN_H, Pin 14 = CAN_L)
- **Comfort CAN (LS-CAN)**: Seat adjustment, mirrors, infotainment
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 CAN Bus Topology and Physical Layer

The CAN bus is a **linear bus topology** where all nodes are connected in parallel across **two wires**: **CAN_H (High)** and **CAN_L (Low)**. The wires are typically a **twisted pair** with characteristic impedance of **120 Ω**, terminated at both ends by **120 Ω resistors** in series (the parallel equivalent of two 120 Ω resistors in series at each end is 60 Ω, but each terminator is 120 Ω giving a total bus resistance of 60 Ω).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two 120 Ω resistors in parallel give 60 Ω?**
> Two 120 Ω resistors in parallel = `(120 × 120) / (120 + 120)` = **60 Ω** (the standard bus load).

### 2.2 CAN Frame Structure (Data Frame - Classical CAN 2.0A)

A CAN data frame is composed of the following bit fields:

1. **SOF (Start Of Frame)** — 1 dominant bit (0)
2. **Arbitration Field** — 11-bit ID + 1 RTR bit
3. **Control Field** — IDE + r0 + 4-bit DLC (Data Length Code, 0–8 bytes)
4. **Data Field** — 0 to 8 bytes (64 bits)
5. **CRC Field** — 15-bit CRC + 1 recessive delimiter
6. **ACK Field** — 2 bits (ACK slot + delimiter)
7. **EOF (End Of Frame)** — 7 recessive bits
8. **IFS (Inter-Frame Space)** — 3 recessive bits

### 2.3 Operational Logic — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Bus Access (Arbitration)**
- Every ECU that wants to transmit **listens** to the bus (Carrier Sense).
- It begins transmitting its **message ID bit-by-bit starting from the MSB**.
- If an ECU transmits a recessive bit (1) but reads back a dominant bit (0), it **loses arbitration** and immediately stops transmitting.
- The ECU with the **lowest ID** (most leading zeros) wins — this is **non-destructive arbitration**.

**Step 2 — Message Broadcasting**
- The winning ECU transmits its full frame.
- **All other ECUs** receive the frame and check the ID.
- ECUs filter messages via **acceptance filters** (hardware masking).

**Step 3 — Acknowledgement**
- Every ECU that successfully received the frame **overwrites the recessive ACK bit with a dominant bit** during the ACK slot.
- If the transmitter does **not** see a dominant ACK, it assumes an error and retransmits.

**Step 4 — Error Detection and Retransmission**
- Every node runs a **CRC check**, **bit stuffing check**, **form check**, and **ACK check**.
- On any detected error, an **Error Frame** is transmitted, and the original message is automatically retransmitted.

### 2.4 Bit Stuffing Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> After **5 consecutive identical bits**, a **stuff bit of opposite polarity** is automatically inserted by the transmitter and removed by the receiver. This guarantees enough edges for clock synchronization.

### 2.5 Error States of a CAN Node

Each CAN controller maintains two internal counters: **TEC (Transmit Error Counter)** and **REC (Receive Error Counter)**.

| Node State | TEC Value | REC Value | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Error Active** | 0–127 | 0–127 | Can send Active Error Flags (6 dominant bits) |
| **Error Passive** | 128–255 | 128–255 | Must send Passive Error Flags (6 recessive bits) |
| **Bus Off** | ≥ 256 | — | Disconnected from bus; must be reset by host |

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter / Formula | Symbol / Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Nominal Bit Rate** | $f_b$ (bits/sec) | Inverse of bit time |
| 2 | **Nominal Bit Time** | $t_b = \dfrac{1}{f_b}$ (seconds) | $t_b$ for 500 kbps = **2 μs** |
| 3 | **Time Quantum** | $t_q = \dfrac{1}{f_{BRP} \cdot f_{CAN\_CLK}}$ (sec) | $f_{BRP}$ = Baud Rate Prescaler |
| 4 | **Total Bit Time** | $t_b = (Sync\_Seg + Prop\_Seg + Phase\_Seg1 + Phase\_Seg2) \cdot t_q$ | $Sync\_Seg = 1\,t_q$ always |
| 5 | **Propagation Delay** | $t_{prop} = 2 \cdot (t_{tx} + t_{rx} + t_{bus})$ (sec) | $t_{bus}$ = physical line delay |
| 6 | **Bus Length Limit (HS-CAN)** | $L_{max} \approx \dfrac{40\,m}{1\,Mbps}$ (proportional) | $L_{max} = \dfrac{5 \cdot t_{prop}}{t_{bus\,per\,m}}$ |
| 7 | **Differential Voltage (Recessive)** | $V_{diff} \approx 0\,V$ | Both lines at ~2.5 V |
| 8 | **Differential Voltage (Dominant)** | $V_{diff} \approx 2\,V$ | CAN_H ≈ 3.5 V, CAN_L ≈ 1.5 V |
| 9 | **Termination Resistance** | $R_{term} = 120\,\Omega$ | Two in parallel = 60 Ω |
| 10 | **CRC Polynomial (CAN 2.0A)** | $g(x) = x^{15} + x^{14} + x^{10} + x^{8} + x^{7} + x^{4} + x^{3} + 1$ | Hamming Distance = 6 |
| 11 | **Stuff Bit Limit** | After 5 identical bits | Ensures clock sync |
| 12 | **Data Length (Classical CAN)** | 0 to 8 bytes | CAN FD: up to **64 bytes** |
| 13 | **Voltage on CAN_H (Recessive)** | $V_{CAN\_H} \approx 2.5\,V$ | Idle state |
| 14 | **Voltage on CAN_L (Recessive)** | $V_{CAN\_L} \approx 2.5\,V$ | Idle state |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** The bus length is **inversely proportional** to the bit rate. Doubling the speed halves the maximum permissible cable length. At **1 Mbps**, max length is **40 m**; at **125 kbps**, it can stretch to **500 m**.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Automotive:** OBD-II diagnostics, ECU-to-ECU control
- **Industrial Automation:** CANopen, DeviceNet (CiA standards)
- **Aerospace & Marine:** NMEA 2000 (CAN-based marine protocol)
- **Medical Equipment:** Patient monitoring systems
- **Agricultural Machinery:** ISOBUS (ISO 11783) for tractor-implement communication
- **Elevators & Robotics:** Distributed motor control
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Bus Length and Bit Rate

The **maximum bus length** $L_{max}$ is constrained by the requirement that a bit must propagate from the transmitter to the most distant node and back (round-trip) **within one bit time**, otherwise arbitration fails.

Let us define the variables:

- $t_b$ = Nominal bit time (seconds)
- $v_p$ = Propagation velocity of signal in twisted pair ≈ **2 × 10⁸ m/s** (≈ 2/3 speed of light)
- $t_{prop}$ = Round-trip propagation delay through cable (seconds)
- $L$ = Total bus length (meters)
- $t_{e}$ = Electronics delay (transmitter + receiver) ≈ 150 ns (typical CAN transceiver)

The **propagation delay** of the signal across a cable of length $L$ is:

$$
t_{prop} = \frac{L}{v_p}
$$

For **round-trip** (transmitter to far end and back), the delay becomes:

$$
t_{round-trip} = 2 \cdot \frac{L}{v_p}
$$

Adding the **electronics delay** $t_e$ (transmitter + receiver delays combined) and the **transceiver loop delay** $t_{loop}$:

$$
t_{total} = 2 \cdot \frac{L}{v_p} + 2 \cdot t_e
$$

For the system to function correctly, this total delay must be less than the bit time:

$$
t_{total} \le t_b
$$

Substituting the bit time $t_b = \dfrac{1}{f_b}$:

$$
2 \cdot \frac{L_{max}}{v_p} + 2 \cdot t_e \le \frac{1}{f_b}
$$

Solving for the maximum bus length:

$$
L_{max} \le \frac{1}{2} \cdot \left( \frac{1}{f_b} - 2 \cdot t_e \right) \cdot v_p
$$

#### Worked Example (KTU Typical Problem)

> **Problem:** A CAN bus is operating at **500 kbps** with a typical transceiver delay of **150 ns**. The propagation velocity is $2 \times 10^8$ m/s. Calculate the **maximum bus length**.

**Step 1:** Calculate the nominal bit time.

$$
t_b = \frac{1}{f_b} = \frac{1}{500 \times 10^3} = 2 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s} = 2\,\mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the total electronics delay (round-trip).

$$
2 \cdot t_e = 2 \times 150 \times 10^{-9} = 300 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{s} = 0.3\,\mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the bus length formula.

$$
L_{max} \le \frac{1}{2} \cdot (2 \times 10^{-6} - 0.3 \times 10^{-6}) \times 2 \times 10^8
$$

**Step 4:** Simplify the bracket.

$$
(2 - 0.3) \times 10^{-6} = 1.7 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s}
$$

**Step 5:** Multiply and divide.

$$
L_{max} \le \frac{1}{2} \times 1.7 \times 10^{-6} \times 2 \times 10^8
$$

$$
L_{max} \le 0.5 \times 1.7 \times 2 \times 10^{2} = 170\,\text{m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** At **500 kbps**, the maximum recommended bus length is approximately **130–170 m** (practical limit considering propagation delay). At **1 Mbps**, this drops to **~40 m** as per the ISO 11898 standard.

### 3.2 Derivation: Bit Time Composition and Time Quantum

The CAN bit time is divided into **four non-overlapping segments**. Let the oscillator clock be $f_{osc}$ and the Baud Rate Prescaler be $BRP$.

**Step 1:** Define the time quantum.

$$
t_q = \frac{BRP + 1}{f_{osc}} \quad \text{(commonly simplified as } t_q = \frac{1}{f_{BRP} \cdot f_{CAN\_CLK}}\text{)}
$$

**Step 2:** Total bit time (in time quanta).

$$
N_{bit} = (SYNC\_SEG + PROP\_SEG + PHASE\_SEG1 + PHASE\_SEG2)
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to seconds.

$$
t_b = N_{bit} \cdot t_q
$$

**Step 4:** Bit rate.

$$
f_b = \frac{1}{t_b} = \frac{1}{N_{bit} \cdot t_q} = \frac{f_{osc}}{BRP \cdot N_{bit}}
$$

#### Numerical Example

Suppose $f_{osc} = 16$ MHz, $BRP = 3$, and the segments are $SYNC = 1\,t_q$, $PROP = 3\,t_q$, $PHASE1 = 3\,t_q$, $PHASE2 = 3\,t_q$. Then:

$$
N_{bit} = 1 + 3 + 3 + 3 = 10\,t_q
$$

$$
t_q = \frac{4}{16 \times 10^6} = 0.25\,\mu\text{s}
$$

$$
t_b = 10 \times 0.25 = 2.5\,\mu\text{s}
$$

$$
f_b = \frac{1}{2.5 \times 10^{-6}} = 400\,\text{kbps}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Frame Type Comparison

| Frame Type | Direction | RTR Bit | Data Field | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Frame** | Transmitter → All | Dominant (0) | 0–8 bytes | Carries real signal data |
| **Remote Frame** | Requester → All | Recessive (1) | None | Requests data from a node |
| **Error Frame** | Any node → All | N/A | N/A | Signals a detected error |
| **Overload Frame** | Receiver → Transmitter | N/A | N/A | Requests delay before next frame |

### 3.4 Error Detection Mechanisms — Analytical Comparison

| Mechanism | Detection Capability | Implementation |
|---|---|---|
| **Bit Monitoring** | Transmitter checks its own bit vs. bus | Hardware — every node |
| **Bit Stuffing Rule** | 6 consecutive identical bits = error | Hardware encoder/decoder |
| **Frame Check (Form Check)** | Fixed-format fields violated | Hardware |
| **CRC Check** | Polynomial $x^{15} + x^{14} + x^{10} + x^{8} + x^{7} + x^{4} + x^{3} + 1$ | Hardware |
| **ACK Check** | No dominant in ACK slot | Hardware |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Hamming Distance of 6** means the CAN CRC can detect up to **5 bit errors in a single frame** and all **burst errors up to 15 bits long**, giving CAN its legendary **reliability of undetected error < 10⁻¹³**.

### 3.5 CAN Bus Diagnosis Flowchart — Analytical Steps

1. **Visual Inspection** → Check wiring, connectors, terminator resistors (120 Ω at each end)
2. **Resistance Measurement** → Bus resistance should read **~60 Ω** (two 120 Ω in parallel)
3. **Voltage Measurement (Ignition OFF)** → Both CAN_H and CAN_L should be at **~2.5 V** (recessive)
4. **Voltage Measurement (Ignition ON, Engine OFF)** → Slight fluctuations, average ~2.5 V
5. **Oscilloscope Capture** → Should show **clean differential signal** with no ringing
6. **OBD-II Scanner Read** → Check for DTCs (Diagnostic Trouble Codes) starting with **U0xxx** (Network communication faults)
7. **Disconnect Nodes** → Isolate faulty ECU by sequential disconnection
8. **CAN Bus Analyzer (CANalyzer/CANoe/Vector)** → Decode frames, check message IDs
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Vehicle CAN Bus Network Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph "Powertrain CAN - 500 kbps"
        ECM1[Engine Control Module]
        TCM1[Transmission Control Module]
        ABS1[Anti lock Braking System]
        ESP1[Electronic Stability Program]
    end
    subgraph "Body CAN - 125 kbps"
        BCM1[Body Control Module]
        HVAC1[Climate Control]
        IMMO1[Instrument Cluster]
    end
    subgraph "Comfort CAN - 125 kbps"
        RAD1[Infotainment]
        GPS1[Navigation]
        TEL1[Telematics]
    end
    subgraph "Diagnostic Link"
        OBD1[OBD II Port - Pin 6 and 14]
    end
    BUS1((CAN H and CAN L Twisted Pair))
    GW1[Central Gateway ECU]
    ECM1 --- BUS1
    TCM1 --- BUS1
    ABS1 --- BUS1
    ESP1 --- BUS1
    BUS1 --- GW1
    GW1 --- BCM1
    GW1 --- HVAC1
    GW1 --- IMMO1
    GW1 --- RAD1
    GW1 --- GPS1
    GW1 --- TEL1
    GW1 --- OBD1
```

### 4.2 CAN Data Frame Structure (Bit-Level)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[SOF - 1 bit Dominant] --> B[ID 11 bit Arbitration Field]
    B --> C[RTR 1 bit]
    C --> D[IDE 1 bit]
    D --> E[r0 1 bit]
    E --> F[DLC 4 bits]
    F --> G[DATA 0 to 8 bytes]
    G --> H[CRC 15 bits]
    H --> I[CRC Delimiter 1 bit]
    I --> J[ACK Slot 1 bit]
    J --> K[ACK Delimiter 1 bit]
    K --> L[EOF 7 bits]
    L --> M[IFS 3 bits]
```

### 4.3 CAN Bus Arbitration Process (CSMA CR)

```mermaid
graph TD
    START([Multiple ECUs request bus access]) --> LISTEN[Each ECU listens to bus]
    LISTEN --> IDLE{Bus Idle?}
    IDLE -->|Yes| TRANS[ECU starts transmitting ID bit by bit]
    IDLE -->|No| WAIT[Wait for IFS and retry]
    TRANS --> COMPARE{Bit on bus equals sent bit?}
    COMPARE -->|Yes recessive| CONT[Continue transmission]
    COMPARE -->|No dominant received| LOSE[ECU loses arbitration and becomes receiver]
    CONT --> FINISH[Complete frame transmission]
    LOSE --> WAIT
    WAIT --> LISTEN
    FINISH --> BROADCAST[All ECUs receive and filter message]
```

### 4.4 CAN Bus Diagnosis Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    S0([Vehicle brought for service]) --> S1[Step 1 Visual inspection of wiring]
    S1 --> S2[Step 2 Measure resistance between CAN H and CAN L]
    S2 --> R1{Reading equals 60 ohm?}
    R1 -->|No| FIX1[Check terminator resistors and replace]
    R1 -->|Yes| S3[Step 3 Measure voltages with ignition OFF]
    S3 --> R2{Both lines at 2.5 V?}
    R2 -->|No| FIX2[Check for short to ground or power]
    R2 -->|Yes| S4[Step 4 Oscilloscope capture on running vehicle]
    S4 --> R3{Signal clean and differential?}
    R3 -->|No| FIX3[Check for noise source or faulty node]
    R3 -->|Yes| S5[Step 5 Read DTCs via OBD II scanner]
    S5 --> R4{U code DTCs present?}
    R4 -->|No| S6[Step 6 Use CAN analyzer to decode frames]
    S4 --> S6
    R4 -->|Yes| S7[Isolate network and disconnect ECUs sequentially]
    S7 --> S8[Identify faulty ECU and replace]
    S6 --> S9[Compare actual vs expected message IDs]
    S9 --> R5{Message missing or corrupted?}
    R5 -->|Yes| S7
    R5 -->|No| END([Diagnosis complete - system OK])
    FIX1 --> S3
    FIX2 --> S3
    FIX3 --> S3
    S8 --> END
```

### 4.5 CAN Error State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    EA([Error Active - TEC 0 to 127]) -->|TEC reaches 128| EP[Error Passive - TEC 128 to 255]
    EP -->|TEC reaches 256| BO[Bus Off - Disconnected]
    BO -->|Host reset or 128 occurrences of 11 recessive bits| EA
    EP -->|TEC drops below 128 after 128 successful frames| EA
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**
*Define Controller Area Network (CAN) bus. List any four key features that make it suitable for in-vehicle communication.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** CAN is a multi-master, message-based serial bus protocol standardized under **ISO 11898**, allowing ECUs in a vehicle to communicate over a **two-wire differential bus (CAN_H and CAN_L)** without a host computer.
- **Four key features (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
  1. **Multi-master, broadcast architecture** — Any ECU can transmit; all receive.
  2. **Non-destructive bitwise arbitration** — Message with lowest ID wins; no data loss.
  3. **Robust error detection** — CRC, bit stuffing, ACK, form, bit monitoring (Hamming distance 6).
  4. **Differential signaling with 120 Ω termination** — Excellent noise immunity for automotive EMI environment.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
*What is the role of the 120 Ω termination resistor in a CAN bus? What is the standard reading when measuring resistance between CAN_H and CAN_L on a healthy bus?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Role (1.5 marks):** The 120 Ω resistors at **both physical ends** of the bus **absorb high-frequency signal reflections** at the cable ends, preventing standing waves and ensuring clean signal edges. They also establish the bus's characteristic impedance.
- **Healthy reading (1.5 marks):** Two 120 Ω resistors are in **parallel** at the bus endpoints, giving a measured resistance of:
  $$R_{bus} = \frac{120 \times 120}{120 + 120} = 60\,\Omega$$
  Any reading **significantly above 70 Ω** indicates an open circuit (missing or disconnected terminator), while a reading **below 50 Ω** suggests a short circuit.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students commonly lose marks by:
> 1. Confusing **Classical CAN** with **CAN FD** (different data lengths and bit rates).
> 2. Forgetting to mention **bit stuffing** when explaining the data frame.
> 3. Skipping the **electronics delay** $t_e$ in bus length derivations.
> 4. Mixing up **dominant (0)** and **recessive (1)** logic levels.

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]**
*With a neat diagram, explain the architecture and message frame format of a Classical CAN 2.0A data frame. Clearly label all fields and state the function of each.*

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture (3.5 marks):**
The CAN 2.0A system uses a **linear bus topology** with ECUs connected in parallel across a **twisted pair** (CAN_H and CAN_L). The bus is terminated with **120 Ω resistors** at both ends. All ECUs share the same physical medium. The differential voltage $V_{diff} = V_{CAN\_H} - V_{CAN\_L}$ is decoded into logic levels.

**Frame Format Table (3.5 marks — 0.25 per field × 14 fields):**

| Field | Length | Value | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SOF** | 1 bit | Dominant (0) | Marks start of frame; synchronizes all nodes |
| **Arbitration ID** | 11 bits | Unique per message | Identifies message and sets priority (lower = higher) |
| **RTR** | 1 bit | Dominant (Data Frame) | Distinguishes Data from Remote frames |
| **IDE** | 1 bit | Dominant (2.0A) | Identifier Extension bit (0 = 11-bit ID) |
| **r0** | 1 bit | Dominant | Reserved (must be dominant in 2.0A) |
| **DLC** | 4 bits | 0–8 | Number of data bytes |
| **Data** | 0–64 bits | Payload | Actual message data (0–8 bytes) |
| **CRC** | 15 bits | Computed | Cyclic Redundancy Check for error detection |
| **CRC Delim.** | 1 bit | Recessive | Separates CRC from ACK |
| **ACK Slot** | 1 bit | Dominant (by receiver) | Acknowledgement of successful reception |
| **ACK Delim.** | 1 bit | Recessive | Separates ACK from EOF |
| **EOF** | 7 bits | Recessive | Marks end of frame |
| **IFS** | 3 bits | Recessive | Inter-frame space; bus recovery time |

---

**(b) [7 Marks]**
*Explain the non-destructive bitwise arbitration process in CAN with an example of two ECUs transmitting simultaneously. State one advantage of this mechanism.*

**Model Solution:**

**Process Explanation (4 marks):**
CAN uses **CSMA/CR (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Resolution)**. When the bus is idle, any ECU may begin transmitting. The arbitration occurs **bit-by-bit during the Arbitration Field (11-bit ID + RTR)**. Each transmitter monitors the bus while sending.

- If an ECU sends a **recessive bit (1)** but reads back a **dominant bit (0)**, it realizes another ECU with a higher priority message is also transmitting.
- The losing ECU **immediately stops** and becomes a receiver.
- The winning ECU continues transmitting **without any data loss or delay**.

**Example (2 marks):**
Suppose **ECU_A** transmits ID `0 1 0 1 0 0 0 1 0 0 0` (binary = 0x288) and **ECU_B** transmits ID `0 1 0 0 1 1 0 0 1 0 0` (binary = 0x18C) at the same time.

- Bit 1: Both send 0 → match, both continue.
- Bit 2: Both send 1 → match, both continue.
- Bit 3: Both send 0 → match.
- Bit 4: **A sends 1, B sends 0** → bus reads 0. ECU_A loses arbitration, switches to listen mode. ECU_B wins.

**Advantage (1 mark):**
The arbitration is **non-destructive** — the higher-priority message suffers **zero delay** (no retransmission collision), guaranteeing **deterministic real-time behaviour** critical for safety systems like ABS and airbags.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]**
*A CAN bus is operating at 500 kbps. The transceiver electronics delay is 150 ns. The propagation velocity in the twisted pair cable is $2 \times 10^8$ m/s. Calculate the maximum permissible bus length. Justify your answer.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute the nominal bit time.
$$
t_b = \frac{1}{f_b} = \frac{1}{500 \times 10^3} = 2 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Write the bus length formula.
$$
L_{max} \le \frac{1}{2} \cdot \left( t_b - 2 \cdot t_e \right) \cdot v_p
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Substitute electronics delay.
$$
2 \cdot t_e = 2 \times 150 \times 10^{-9} = 3 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Compute available propagation time.
$$
t_{avail} = 2 \times 10^{-6} - 0.3 \times 10^{-6} = 1.7 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s}
$$

**Step 5 (1.5 marks):** Solve for maximum length.
$$
L_{max} \le \frac{1}{2} \times 1.7 \times 10^{-6} \times 2 \times 10^8 = 170\,\text{m}
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Justification.
For correct operation, the **round-trip propagation delay** (signal traveling to the farthest node and the acknowledgement returning) must be less than **one bit time**. The ISO 11898 practical recommendation at 500 kbps is approximately **130 m** to provide safety margin, so the calculated **170 m** is the theoretical upper bound.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]**
*Describe the five error detection mechanisms in CAN and the role of TEC and REC error counters in node error state management.*

**Model Solution:**

**Five Error Detection Mechanisms (5 marks — 1 each):**

1. **Bit Monitoring (Bit Error):** Every transmitter compares the bit it sends with the bit on the bus. A mismatch (sending recessive, reading dominant outside arbitration) is flagged as a bit error.
2. **Bit Stuffing Check (Stuff Error):** After 5 identical bits, a stuff bit of opposite polarity is mandatory. A violation of this rule (6 identical bits) triggers a stuff error.
3. **CRC Check (CRC Error):** The receiver recomputes the CRC-15 polynomial over the received bits. A mismatch with the transmitted CRC value flags a CRC error.
4. **Form Check (Form Error):** Fixed-format fields (CRC Delim, ACK Delim, EOF, IFS) must always be recessive. A dominant bit in these fields is a form error.
5. **Acknowledgement Check (ACK Error):** The transmitter sets the ACK slot to recessive. If no receiver overwrites it with dominant, the transmitter knows no node received the message.

**TEC and REC Error Counters (2 marks):**
- **TEC (Transmit Error Counter):** Increments on transmit errors, decrements on successful transmissions.
- **REC (Receive Error Counter):** Increments on receive errors, decrements on successful receptions.
- **States:** Error Active (TEC, REC < 128), Error Passive (TEC, REC ≥ 128), Bus Off (TEC ≥ 256).
- **Bus Off recovery:** A node in Bus Off must detect **128 occurrences of 11 consecutive recessive bits** (about 128 × 11 bits) before it can re-engage.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — CAN Bus (Module Experiment 2)**

- [x] **CAN** stands for **Controller Area Network** — invented by **Bosch (1986)**, standardized as **ISO 11898**.
- [x] **Two-wire differential bus**: CAN_H and CAN_L, twisted pair, **120 Ω terminator** at both ends.
- [x] **Differential voltage levels**: Recessive ≈ **0 V**, Dominant ≈ **2 V** (CAN_H = 3.5 V, CAN_L = 1.5 V).
- [x] **Dominant = Logic 0**, **Recessive = Logic 1** (opposite of TTL!).
- [x] **Classical CAN 2.0A** uses **11-bit ID**; **CAN 2.0B (Extended)** uses **29-bit ID**.
- [x] **Lower ID = Higher priority** during arbitration.
- [x] **Non-destructive bitwise arbitration** using **CSMA/CR** — winner proceeds without delay.
- [x] **Frame fields**: SOF → Arbitration → Control → Data → CRC → ACK → EOF → IFS.
- [x] **Data field length** = 0 to **8 bytes (64 bits)** in Classical CAN; up to **64 bytes** in CAN FD.
- [x] **Bit stuffing rule**: Stuff bit inserted after every **5 identical bits**.
- [x] **Five error detection mechanisms**: Bit monitoring, Bit stuffing, CRC-15, Form check, ACK check.
- [x] **Hamming Distance = 6** → undetected error probability < **10⁻¹³**.
- [x] **Error States**: Error Active → Error Passive → Bus Off (governed by TEC/REC).
- [x] **Bus length vs. bit rate**: At 1 Mbps → **40 m**; at 500 kbps → **~130–170 m**; at 125 kbps → **~500 m**.
- [x] **OBD-II port**: Pin **6 = CAN_H**, Pin **14 = CAN_L** (per ISO 15765-4).
- [x] **Network DTCs** start with **U-codes** (e.g., U0100 = Lost Communication with ECM).
- [x] **CAN bus voltage diagnostic**: Both lines should be at **2.5 V** with ignition OFF (recessive idle).
- [x] **Healthy bus resistance** measured across CAN_H and CAN_L with power OFF = **~60 Ω**.
- [x] **Common CAN speeds in vehicles**: HS-CAN **500 kbps** (Powertrain), MS-CAN **125 kbps** (Body/Comfort).
- [x] **Diagnostic tools**: Multimeter, Oscilloscope, OBD-II scanner, Vector CANalyzer/CANoe, Kvaser Leaf, PEAK PCAN-USB.
- [x] **Higher-Layer Protocols**: CANopen (CiA 301), J1939 (trucks/buses), DeviceNet (industrial), NMEA 2000 (marine).
- [x] **CAN FD** (Flexible Data-rate) supports bit rate switching — arbitration at 500 kbps, data at **2–5 Mbps**.
- [x] **Single-Wire CAN (SWC)** uses **one wire** at **33.3 kbps** for non-critical body modules (SAE J2411).

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Memory Trick for KTU Viva:** *"CAN bus is the **Car's Nervous System** — every ECU listens, the **lowest ID shouts loudest** (dominant), and the bus is wrapped in a **120 Ω hug at both ends** to keep the signal from echoing."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calibration and testing of systems such as adaptive cruise control, lane departure warning, and automatic emergency braking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAUL607 - AUTOTRONICS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Calibration and testing of systems such as adaptive cruise control, lane departure warning, and automatic emergency braking ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) Calibration & Testing** is the systematic process of aligning, validating, and verifying the performance of vehicle-mounted sensors, actuators, and control algorithms against defined engineering tolerances, regulatory standards (ISO 26262, ISO 21448 SOTIF, Euro NCAP), and OEM specifications to ensure functional safety, reliability, and deterministic behavior under real-world operating conditions. The three target systems fall under **SAE Level 2 Vehicle Automation** (Partial Automation): | System | Full Name | SAE Level | Primary Function | |---|---|---|---| | **ACC** | Adaptive Cruise Control | L1–L2 | Longitudinal velocity & gap regulation | | **LDW** | Lane Departure Warning | L0–L1 | Lateral position monitoring & alert | | **AEB** | Automatic Emergency Braking | L0 (AEB) → L2 (AEB+ACC) | Collision avoidance / mitigation | --- ### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "The Three Senses of an Autonomous Knight"** > > Imagine training a medieval knight to ride a horse through a busy market: > - **ACC = The knight's "forward eye + speed foot"** — constantly measures the distance to the cart ahead and adjusts the horse's speed (throttle/brake) to keep a safe gap, even when the cart slows or stops. > - **LDW = The knight's "side whiskers"** — feels the painted lane lines on the road; if he drifts too close to a line, a buzz in his helmet (warning) reminds him to steer back. > - **AEB = The knight's "emergency reflex"** — if a child suddenly dashes in front and the knight reacts too late, a built-in mechanical hand (brake actuator) slams the reins *faster than humanly possible*. > > **Calibration** = training the knight's senses (eye focus, whisker sensitivity) to known reference points. > **Testing** = putting the knight through scripted obstacle courses and measuring his response time. --- ### 1.3 Core Physical Constants & Engineering Metrics (Bolded) - **Speed of light** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$ (used for radar range calculation) - **Camera focal length calibration tolerance**: typically $\pm 2\%$ of nominal - **Radar frequency bands (Automotive)**: **24 GHz (narrowband)**, **76–77 GHz (long-range, LRR)**, **77–81 GHz (short-range, SRR)** - **Euro NCAP AEB test target speed differential**: up to $\mathbf{60 \text{ km/h}}$ - **LDW typical lane departure threshold**: **0.3 m to 0.75 m** from inner lane marker - **AEB maximum braking deceleration**: **~9.8 m/s² (1 g)** for full autonomous emergency stop --- ### 1.4 Sensor Stack Overview (Hardware Foundation) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why sensor understanding is critical for KTU lab viva**: Every calibration procedure begins with identifying *which sensor modality* generates the input. The three target systems collectively use the full ADAS sensor stack. | Sensor | Operating Principle | Used In | Range | |---|---|---|---| | **Long-Range Radar (LRR)** | FMCW Doppler @ 76–77 GHz | ACC, AEB | 0–250 m | | **Mid-Range Radar (MRR)** | FMCW @ 76–77 GHz | AEB, BSM | 0–100 m | | **Front Camera (Mono/Stereo)** | CMOS image + CNN | LDW, AEB (pedestrian) | 0–80 m | | **LiDAR (optional)** | Time-of-Flight @ 905/1550 nm | AEB, ACC (high-end) | 0–200 m | | **Ultrasonic** | Piezo @ 48–58 kHz | Parking (AEB low-speed) | 0.1–5 m | | **Wheel Speed + Yaw Rate + IMU** | MEMS gyroscope/accelerometer | All systems (vehicle state) | — | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Three Pillars of ADAS Calibration & Testing #### Pillar I — Radar-Based ACC & AEB Calibration **Principle**: Frequency-Modulated Continuous Wave (FMCW) radar transmits a chirp whose frequency linearly sweeps a bandwidth $B$. The time delay $\tau$ between transmitted and received signals gives **range**, and the Doppler shift $f_D$ gives **radial velocity**. - **Chirp sweep slope** $S = B / T_c$ (Hz/s), where $T_c$ is chirp duration. - **Beat frequency** $f_b = S \cdot \tau$ for stationary targets. - **Range resolution** $\Delta R = c / (2B)$. - **Maximum unambiguous range** $R_{max} = (f_{s,ADC} \cdot c) / (4 S)$. - **Radial velocity** $v_r = (\lambda \cdot f_D) / 2$ (Beat with 2D-FFT across chirps). - **Range-Doppler coupling** correction: $\Delta R_{coupling} = (c \cdot f_D) / (2 S)$ (compensated in signal processing). > [!NOTE] > **Engineering utility**: Range resolution is *inversely* proportional to bandwidth. Modern 77 GHz automotive radars use $B \approx 1$–$4$ GHz to achieve sub-meter resolution — essential for distinguishing two vehicles traveling in adjacent lanes. --- #### Pillar II — Camera-Based LDW & AEB Pedestrian Calibration **Principle**: A forward-facing CMOS camera captures frames at 30–60 fps. Computer vision algorithms extract lane markings via: 1. **Inverse Perspective Mapping (IPM)** — removes ground-plane distortion. 2. **Edge detection (Canny / Sobel)** — finds lane boundaries. 3. **Lane model fitting** — 2nd or 3rd order polynomial in image plane; then transformed to world plane via camera extrinsics. **Camera Calibration** uses the **Pinhole Model**: $$ s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} f_x & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}_{\text{Intrinsic } K} \cdot \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} R & \vert & t \end{bmatrix}}_{\text{Extrinsic}} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ - $(u, v)$: pixel coordinates - $(X_w, Y_w, Z_w)$: world coordinates - $f_x, f_y$: focal lengths in pixels - $c_x, c_y$: principal point - $\gamma$: skew (≈ 0 for modern sensors) - $R, t$: rotation and translation (extrinsics) - $s$: scale factor **Distortion model (Brown-Conrady)**: $$ \begin{aligned} x_{distorted} &= x(1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6) + 2 p_1 xy + p_2(r^2 + 2x^2) \\ y_{distorted} &= y(1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6) + p_1(r^2 + 2y^2) + 2 p_2 xy \end{aligned} $$ where $r^2 = x^2 + y^2$ and $k_1, k_2, k_3, p_1, p_2$ are radial and tangential distortion coefficients. --- #### Pillar III — System-Level Testing Philosophy Testing follows the **V-Model**: 1. **Unit Test** — single sensor/algorithm in HIL (Hardware-in-the-Loop) rig. 2. **Integration Test** — sensor fusion (radar + camera) validated together. 3. **Vehicle Test** — controlled proving ground (test track). 4. **Validation Test** — Euro NCAP / NHTSA certified scenarios. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Viva Question Pattern**: "Differentiate static vs dynamic calibration." > - **Static calibration**: vehicle is stationary, sensors aligned to known reference targets (e.g., radar reflector board, calibration mat for camera). Used in service centers. > - **Dynamic calibration**: vehicle is driven on a marked road at specified speed (e.g., 30–80 km/h for 5–10 minutes) while the camera self-learns lane geometry. Required when windshield/camera is replaced. --- ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Print this table — it covers ~70% of numerical problems in KTU lab exams.** | # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Typical Value / Range | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | FMCW Beat Frequency | $f_b = S \cdot \tau$ | $S$ = Hz/s, $\tau$ = s | kHz–MHz | | 2 | Radar Range Resolution | $\Delta R = \dfrac{c}{2B}$ | $c$ = m/s, $B$ = Hz | 0.0375 m @ 4 GHz | | 3 | Maximum Range | $R_{max} = \dfrac{f_{s,ADC} \cdot c}{4 S}$ | $f_{s,ADC}$ = Hz | 250 m | | 4 | Radial Velocity | $v_r = \dfrac{\lambda \cdot f_D}{2}$ | $\lambda$ = m, $f_D$ = Hz | −200 to +200 km/h | | 5 | Time-to-Collision (TTC) | $TTC = \dfrac{d_{rel}}{v_{rel}}$ | $d_{rel}$ = m, $v_{rel}$ = m/s | AEB trigger < 1.4 s | | 6 | Required Deceleration | $a_{req} = \dfrac{v_{rel}^2}{2 \cdot d_{rel}}$ | $v_{rel}$ = m/s, $d_{rel}$ = m | ≤ 9.8 m/s² (1 g) | | 7 | Pinhole Projection | $s[u, v, 1]^T = K[R \vert t] X_w$ | $K$ intrinsic, $R,t$ extrinsic | — | | 8 | Camera Focal Length | $f = \dfrac{h_{image} \cdot Z}{h_{object}}$ | $h_{image}$ = pixel, $Z$ = m | 50°–100° FOV | | 9 | Lane Departure Rate | $LDR = \dfrac{\Delta y_{lat}}{\Delta t}$ | m/s | 0.4–0.8 m/s | | 10 | Ground Truth Distance | $d = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$ | m | Test track measure | | 11 | Mean Absolute Error | $MAE = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ | — | < 0.05 m (ACC) | | 12 | Radar Cross Section (RCS) | $RCS = 10 \log_{10}(\sigma)$ dBsm | $\sigma$ in m² | Car ≈ 10 dBsm, Pedestrian ≈ −8 dBsm | --- ### 2.3 Engineering Utility in the Automotive Industry - **Functional Safety**: ISO 26262 mandates **ASIL (Automotive Safety Integrity Level)** classification. AEB is typically **ASIL C or D** — the highest safety tier. - **SOTIF (ISO 21448)**: Addresses *absence of unreasonable risk* due to *performance limitations* (e.g., camera fails to detect a child at night). - **Regulatory testing**: Euro NCAP AEB Pedestrian protocol requires detection at night with partial obscured targets — driving the need for sensor fusion. - **After-sales revenue**: Sensor calibration is a **$15 billion+ global service market** post-2025, as ADAS becomes standard in mid-segment cars. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Radar-Based Range & Velocity Extraction (FMCW) **Problem Context**: An automotive ACC radar at 77 GHz transmits a chirp of bandwidth $B = 1 \text{ GHz}$ over chirp duration $T_c = 40 \text{ \mu s}$. Compute (a) range resolution, (b) maximum beat frequency, and (c) required ADC sampling rate to resolve targets up to 200 m. **Given**: - $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$ - $f_0 = 77 \text{ GHz}$ - $B = 1 \text{ GHz}$ - $T_c = 40 \text{ \mu s}$ - $R_{max} = 200 \text{ m}$ **Step 1 — Compute chirp slope $S$**: $$ S = \frac{B}{T_c} = \frac{1 \times 10^9 \text{ Hz}}{40 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}} = 2.5 \times 10^{13} \text{ Hz/s} $$ **Step 2 — Range resolution (a)**: $$ \Delta R = \frac{c}{2B} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 1 \times 10^9} = 0.15 \text{ m} $$ *Logic*: Two vehicles separated by ≥ 15 cm can be distinguished — perfect for highway traffic. **Step 3 — Time delay for a target at 200 m**: $$ \tau = \frac{2R}{c} = \frac{2 \times 200}{3 \times 10^8} = 1.333 \text{ \mu s} $$ **Step 4 — Maximum beat frequency (b)**: $$ f_{b,max} = S \cdot \tau = 2.5 \times 10^{13} \times 1.333 \times 10^{-6} = 33{,}333 \text{ Hz} \approx 33.33 \text{ kHz} $$ *Logic*: Beat frequency is the *difference* between transmitted and received chirp frequencies. Since the round-trip delay is short (μs), the beat is audio-frequency range. **Step 5 — Required ADC sampling rate (c)**: By Nyquist theorem, $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{b,max}$: $$ f_s \geq 2 \times 33{,}333 = 66{,}666 \text{ Hz} \approx 66.7 \text{ kHz} $$ In practice, automotive radar MCUs sample at **2–20 MHz** to oversample and reduce quantization noise. --- ### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: AEB Deceleration Demand **Problem**: Host vehicle is travelling at $v_1 = 60 \text{ km/h}$ and detects a stationary target at $d = 30 \text{ m}$. Compute the required deceleration if AEB triggers at TTC = 1.4 s. Verify the deceleration is feasible (≤ 1 g). **Step 1 — Convert speed to SI**: $$ v_1 = 60 \text{ km/h} = \frac{60}{3.6} = 16.67 \text{ m/s} $$ **Step 2 — Compute relative velocity** (target stationary): $$ v_{rel} = 16.67 - 0 = 16.67 \text{ m/s} $$ **Step 3 — Compute distance at AEB trigger**: $$ d_{trigger} = v_{rel} \times TTC = 16.67 \times 1.4 = 23.33 \text{ m} $$ **Step 4 — Compute required deceleration using kinematic equation** $v_f^2 = v_0^2 - 2a \cdot d$: Setting $v_f = 0$ at $d = d_{trigger} = 23.33 \text{ m}$: $$ a_{req} = \frac{v_0^2}{2 d_{trigger}} = \frac{(16.67)^2}{2 \times 23.33} = \frac{277.89}{46.66} = 5.96 \text{ m/s}^2 $$ **Step 5 — Convert to g-units**: $$ a_{req} = \frac{5.96}{9.81} = 0.607 \text{ g} $$ *Logic*: 0.607 g is within typical AEB braking capacity (max ≈ 0.8–1.0 g) → **AEB feasible**. A value > 0.9 g would indicate AEB failure to stop in time and require collision warning earlier. --- ### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Camera Intrinsic Calibration (Zhang's Method Concept) **Problem**: A 9×6 checkerboard with 25 mm squares is imaged. Solve for focal length $f_x$ given that a corner at world $(X_w, Y_w, Z_w) = (0, 0, 1500 \text{ mm})$ projects to pixel $(u, v) = (640, 360)$ on a 1280×720 image, assuming square pixels and zero skew, principal point at image center. **Step 1 — Set up pinhole matrix $K$** (no skew, $c_x = 640$, $c_y = 360$): $$ K = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & 0 & 640 \\ 0 & f_y & 360 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 2 — Assume $R = I$ and $t = [0, 0, 0]^T$ for orthogonal view** (Z-axis of world coincides with camera optical axis). Then $s [u, v, 1]^T = K [0, 0, 1500, 1]^T$. **Step 3 — Compute the projection**: $$ \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{1500} \begin{bmatrix} f_x \cdot 1500 + 640 \cdot 1500 \cdot 0 \\ f_y \cdot 1500 + 360 \cdot 1500 \cdot 0 \\ 1500 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x \\ f_y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 4 — Solve for $f_x$**: $$ f_x = u - c_x + c_x = u \quad \text{when world origin aligns with image center} \\ \Rightarrow f_x = u = 640 \text{ pixels} $$ **Step 5 — Verify field of view**: $$ FOV_x = 2 \arctan\left(\frac{W/2}{f_x}\right) = 2 \arctan\left(\frac{640}{640}\right) = 2 \times 45° = 90° $$ *Logic*: 90° horizontal FOV is standard for forward ADAS cameras. If the camera lens is replaced, this intrinsic matrix must be *recalibrated* — a common KTU lab scenario. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Calibration & Testing Procedure for Each System > [!NOTE] > **This matrix is the most-likely 14-mark answer outline for KTU viva/practical exam.** | Step | ACC | LDW | AEB | |---|---|---|---| | 1. Pre-condition | Vehicle on level ground, fuel > 25% | Same | Same + brake fluid OK | | 2. Sensor type | Front 77 GHz radar | Front camera (windshield) | Radar + Camera + Wheel speed | | 3. Static target | Metal reflector at 5 m & 50 m axes | Calibration mat with checker pattern | Euro NCAP GVT (Global Vehicle Target) | | 4. Procedure | Radar scans reflectors; ECU aligns azimuth ±0.5° & elevation ±0.3° | Camera captures mat; software solves $K, R, t$ | Multi-sensor fusion alignment | | 5. Dynamic test | Drive at 50 km/h, follow car at 30 m gap; verify ±10% | Drive 5 min on marked highway at 60 km/h | Track test: 30→10 km/h closing on dummy | | 6. Pass criteria | Gap error < 0.5 m at 100 ms update | Lane detect at ≥ 60 m ahead | Stop within 0.3 m of dummy rear | | 7. Reference standard | ISO 15622, ISO 22179 | ISO 17361, Euro NCAP LDW | Euro NCAP AEB, ISO 22839 | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Integrated ADAS System ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SENS["PERCEPTION LAYER - SENSORS"] sRadar["LRR 77GHz Radar"] sCam["Forward CMOS Camera 1MP"] sLidar["Optional LiDAR 905nm"] sImu["6Axis IMU"] sWheel["Wheel Speed Encoder x4"] end subgraph FUSE["SENSOR FUSION ECU"] fEcu["ADAS Domain Controller"] fFilt["Kalman Filter Object Tracker"] fAi["CNN Lane and Pedestrian Detector"] end subgraph CTRL["DECISION AND ACTUATION"] cAcc["ACC Longitudinal Controller"] cLdw["LDW HMI and Steering Advisor"] cAeb["AEB Collision Imminent Braking"] end subgraph HMI["HUMAN MACHINE INTERFACE"] hDash["Instrument Cluster Display"] hBuzz["Audible Buzzer"] hVib["Steering Wheel Vibration Motor"] hBrk["Hydraulic Brake Booster"] end sRadar --> fEcu sCam --> fEcu sLidar --> fEcu sImu --> fEcu sWheel --> fEcu fEcu --> fFilt fEcu --> fAi fFilt --> cAcc fFilt --> cAeb fAi --> cLdw fAi --> cAeb fFilt --> cAcc cAcc --> hDash cLdw --> hBuzz cLdw --> hVib cAeb --> hBrk cAeb --> hDash ``` **Explanation of nodes (for the student)**: - `fEcu` is the **brain**; it runs a **sensor-fusion Kalman Filter** to merge radar + camera + IMU into a single "object list" of the world. - `cAcc`, `cLdw`, `cAeb` are *separate* software modules; AEB always overrides ACC if a collision is imminent. - `hBrk` is a *physical* actuation; LDW never actuates brakes — it only warns. --- ### 4.2 Calibration Workflow (Service / Lab Procedure) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Vehicle Arrives at Calibration Bay"] --> B{"Pre-Scan
Diagnostic Check"} B -- "DTC Found" --> C["Clear Faults and Repair"] C --> A B -- "No DTC" --> D["Position Vehicle on Level
Alignment Rack"] D --> E["Static Target Placement
Radar Reflector 5m and 50m
Camera Mat 2m in front"] E --> F["Run OEM Calibration Tool
Scan Tool or ADAS Tablet"] F --> G{"Software Reports
All Sensors GREEN?"} G -- "No" --> H["Re-align Physical Mount
Check Windshield or Bumper"] H --> E G -- "Yes" --> I["Dynamic Road Calibration
Drive 10 min at 50 to 80 km/h"] I --> J{"Road Test Pass
Lateral Error less than 10cm
at 100m range"} J -- "No" --> K["Inspect Tire Pressure
Camber and Steering Geometry"] K --> D J -- "Yes" --> L["Print Calibration Certificate
Hand Over to Customer"] ``` --- ### 4.3 AEB Test Scenario Flow (Euro NCAP AEB Car-to-Car Rear) ```mermaid flowchart LR T0["Test Setup
GVT Dummy on Track"] --> T1["Host at 50 km/h
Target Stationary"] T1 --> T2["AEB Activates
at TTC 1.4s"] T2 --> T3{"Measured Impact
Speed"} T3 -- "0 km/h" --> P1["PASS - Full Avoidance"] T3 -- "0 to 10 km/h" --> P2["PASS - Mitigation"] T3 -- "Greater than 10 km/h" --> P3["FAIL - Insufficient Braking"] T3 -- "Greater than 30 km/h" --> P4["CRITICAL - No AEB"] ``` --- ### 4.4 Sensor-to-System Mapping (Concept Matrix) ```mermaid flowchart LR sRadar["77GHz Radar"] --> acc["ACC"] sRadar --> aeb["AEB"] sCam["Forward Camera"] --> ldw["LDW"] sCam --> aeb sLidar["LiDAR"] --> acc sLidar --> aeb sImu["IMU"] --> acc sImu --> ldw ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)** --- #### **Q1. Define Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) and list its two main control parameters.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 1]` **Model Answer**: > Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) is an *extension* of conventional cruise control that uses forward-facing radar (and/or camera) to maintain a **time-gap (headway)** with a preceding vehicle while also regulating the **host vehicle's longitudinal velocity** within a driver-set range. > > **Two main control parameters**: > 1. **Set speed** $v_{set}$ — the maximum desired speed (driver input). > 2. **Time-headway** $\tau_h$ (or distance gap $d_{gap}$) — typically 1.0 s, 1.5 s, or 2.0 s. > > ACC is governed by **ISO 15622** and operates between ~30 km/h and 200 km/h (or 0 km/h in "Stop & Go" variants). > **(3 marks)** --- #### **Q2. Differentiate between static and dynamic calibration of a forward ADAS camera.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Supplementary]` **Model Answer**: | Aspect | Static Calibration | Dynamic Calibration | |---|---|---| | **Vehicle state** | Stationary | Moving | | **Tools** | Calibration mat/checkerboard, OEM jig | Marked highway / test track | | **Purpose** | Determine intrinsics ($K$) and initial extrinsics ($R, t$) | Refine extrinsic drift; re-learn lane geometry | | **Time** | 15–45 min | 5–15 min drive at 50–80 km/h | | **When needed** | Camera/windshield replacement | After static calibration, or suspension geometry change | | **Output** | $f_x, f_y, c_x, c_y, k_1, k_2, R, t$ | Lane model polynomial, vanishing-point offset | > In practice, **both** are required sequentially. Dynamic alone cannot recover absolute scale. > **(3 marks)** --- ### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)** --- #### **QUESTION A — (a) and (b), total 14 marks** ##### **Q.A(a). With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of an Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) system. List the major sensors used and state the relevant ISO standard.** [7 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Solution**: **1. Block diagram** (already shown in Section 4.1 — student must redraw with the following 5 blocks): > (i) **Sensor input** (Radar + Camera + Wheel speed + Yaw) > (ii) **Signal processing / Object detection** > (iii) **Controller** (PI / Model Predictive Controller) > (iv) **Actuator interface** (Throttle by-wire + Brake by-wire) > (v) **HMI / Driver display** **2. Operation**: - Radar transmits FMCW chirp, receives echoes from preceding vehicle, computes **range $R$** and **relative velocity $v_{rel}$**. - ECU compares $v_{rel}$ and $R$ with the **driver's set speed and time-gap**. - If $R$ is **less than the safe gap** $R_{safe} = v_{host} \cdot \tau_h$, controller commands **brake** (or reduces throttle). - If $R > R_{safe}$ and $v_{host} < v_{set}$, controller commands **throttle** to accelerate to $v_{set}$. - **Lead-lag controller** (or MPC) ensures smooth transitions (no jerk > 2.5 m/s³ for comfort). **3. Sensors**: - Long-range 77 GHz radar - Forward CMOS camera (for cut-in detection, optional) - Wheel speed sensors - Yaw rate / longitudinal acceleration sensor - Steering angle sensor (for curve speed adaptation) **4. Standard**: **ISO 15622 (2018)** — "Adaptive Cruise Control systems — Performance requirements and test procedures." **(7 marks — 2 marks diagram, 3 marks operation, 1 mark sensors, 1 mark standard)** --- ##### **Q.A(b). A 77 GHz FMCW radar uses a chirp bandwidth of 1.5 GHz and chirp duration of 30 μs. Calculate (i) the range resolution, (ii) the maximum beat frequency for a target at 150 m, and (iii) the maximum unambiguous velocity, assuming two consecutive chirps separated by 50 μs.** [7 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Solution**: **Given**: - $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s - $B = 1.5$ GHz $= 1.5 \times 10^9$ Hz - $T_c = 30$ μs $= 30 \times 10^{-6}$ s - $R = 150$ m - $T_{PRI} = 50$ μs **(i) Range resolution**: $$ \Delta R = \frac{c}{2B} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 1.5 \times 10^9} = 0.10 \text{ m} $$ **(ii) Chirp slope and time delay**: $$ S = \frac{B}{T_c} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^9}{30 \times 10^{-6}} = 5.0 \times 10^{13} \text{ Hz/s} $$ $$ \tau = \frac{2R}{c} = \frac{2 \times 150}{3 \times 10^8} = 1.0 \text{ \mu s} $$ $$ f_{b,max} = S \cdot \tau = 5.0 \times 10^{13} \times 1.0 \times 10^{-6} = 5.0 \times 10^{7} \text{ Hz} = 50 \text{ MHz} $$ **(iii) Maximum unambiguous velocity**: First, wavelength $\lambda = c / f_0 = (3 \times 10^8) / (77 \times 10^9) = 3.896 \times 10^{-3}$ m. Maximum Doppler frequency (Nyquist): $f_{D,max} = 1 / (2 T_{PRI}) = 1 / (2 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}) = 10 \text{ kHz}$. $$ v_{max} = \frac{\lambda \cdot f_{D,max}}{2} = \frac{3.896 \times 10^{-3} \times 10{,}000}{2} = 19.48 \text{ m/s} \approx 70.1 \text{ km/h} $$ **Final Answers**: - (i) $\Delta R = 0.10$ m - (ii) $f_{b,max} = 50$ MHz - (iii) $v_{max} \approx 19.48$ m/s ≈ 70.1 km/h **(7 marks — 2 marks for (i), 3 marks for (ii), 2 marks for (iii))** --- #### **QUESTION B — (a) and (b), total 14 marks** *(Internal Choice Alternative)* ##### **Q.B(a). Describe the Lane Departure Warning (LDW) system. Explain with a block diagram how a forward camera and the lane-detection algorithm work together. Mention the relevant ISO standard and Euro NCAP scoring criteria.** [7 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Model Solution**: **1. Definition**: LDW is a *lateral-position monitoring* system that detects unintentional lane drift and alerts the driver via visual, audible, and/or haptic (steering-wheel vibration) warnings. It does **not** apply steering correction (that would be LKA — Lane Keeping *Assist*). **2. Block diagram** (student must draw 5 blocks): - **Image acquisition** (CMOS camera, 30–60 fps) - **Pre-processing** (grayscale, ROI crop, IPM) - **Lane feature extraction** (Canny edges, Hough transform) - **Lane model fitting** (2nd-order polynomial) - **Departure decision** (compare vehicle lateral position to lane boundary; trigger warning if departure rate > threshold AND turn signal OFF) **3. Algorithm details**: - **Inverse Perspective Mapping (IPM)**: remaps the road plane to a "bird's-eye view" using the camera's homography $H$. This converts parallel lane lines into parallel lines in IPM space. - **Sliding-window search**: starting from a seed at the bottom of the image (just in front of the bonnet), search upward for the next best edge. - **Time-to-Line-Crossing (TLC)**: $TLC = d_{lat} / LDR$, where $d_{lat}$ is lateral distance to lane and $LDR$ is lateral departure rate. - Warning triggered if **TLC < 0.5 s** *and* turn signal is OFF. **4. ISO Standard**: **ISO 17361:2017** — "Lane Departure Warning Systems — Performance requirements and test procedures." **5. Euro NCAP LDW scoring** (since 2018): - 60% weight: lane detection under **standard conditions** (day, clear markings). - 30% weight: **repeatability**. - 10% weight: **false-positive rate** (no warning on intentional lane changes). - Vehicle must be tested at **70 km/h** on a 3.5 m wide lane. **(7 marks)** --- ##### **Q.B(b). An AEB system detects a stationary target at 25 m. The host vehicle is moving at 72 km/h. The AEB controller triggers braking at TTC = 1.2 s. Compute: (i) the distance at AEB trigger, (ii) the required deceleration, and (iii) whether the system can stop in time assuming coefficient of friction μ = 0.8.** [7 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **Model Solution**: **Given**: - $v_0 = 72$ km/h $= 20$ m/s - $R_{detect} = 25$ m - $TTC = 1.2$ s - $\mu = 0.8$ - $g = 9.81$ m/s² **(i) Distance at AEB trigger**: $$ d_{trigger} = v_0 \times TTC = 20 \times 1.2 = 24 \text{ m} $$ (Note: $d_{trigger} < R_{detect}$ → AEB is feasible; if it were >, AEB would never trigger.) **(ii) Required deceleration** (using $v_f^2 = v_0^2 - 2 a d$ with $v_f = 0$): $$ a_{req} = \frac{v_0^2}{2 d_{trigger}} = \frac{(20)^2}{2 \times 24} = \frac{400}{48} = 8.33 \text{ m/s}^2 $$ **(iii) Maximum friction-limited deceleration**: $$ a_{max} = \mu \cdot g = 0.8 \times 9.81 = 7.85 \text{ m/s}^2 $$ **Comparison**: $a_{req} = 8.33 \text{ m/s}^2 > a_{max} = 7.85 \text{ m/s}^2$. **Conclusion**: The AEB system **cannot** stop the vehicle in time using friction alone. A collision is **imminent** unless: - TTC trigger is lowered to ~1.0 s, OR - Higher-friction surface is assumed, OR - A combination of regenerative + hydraulic braking is used (typical EVs achieve > 1 g combined). **Stopping distance with $a_{max}$**: $$ d_{stop} = \frac{v_0^2}{2 a_{max}} = \frac{400}{2 \times 7.85} = 25.48 \text{ m} $$ Since $d_{stop} = 25.48$ m > $R_{detect} = 25$ m, **collision at 0.48 m residual** (≈ impact speed 0.4 m/s ≈ 1.4 km/h — minor mitigation). **(7 marks — 2 marks for (i), 2 marks for (ii), 3 marks for (iii) including the comparison and conclusion)** --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Calibration & Testing Questions** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s** — KTU examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark instantly if you mix units. Always write: "$v = 72 \text{ km/h} = 20 \text{ m/s}$". > 2. **Confusing $f_b$ (beat frequency) with $f_D$ (Doppler frequency)** — beat is along chirp, Doppler is across chirps. Different FFT axes. > 3. **In LDW questions, students forget to mention the *turn-signal inhibition* logic** — losing 1 mark. A real LDW *suppresses* warning if the indicator is on. > 4. **In AEB calculations, students assume target is moving** but the question says "stationary" — read carefully. > 5. **No block diagram in 7-mark questions** → automatic loss of 2 marks (diagrams are mandatory for KTU 2024 scheme lab courses). > 6. **Static vs dynamic calibration distinction** is asked almost every year — memorize the table in §3.4. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [ ] **ACC** = longitudinal control → sensors: **77 GHz radar**; standard: **ISO 15622**. - [ ] **LDW** = lateral *warning* (not control) → sensors: **forward CMOS camera**; standard: **ISO 17361**. - [ ] **AEB** = longitudinal *emergency* control → sensors: **radar + camera fusion**; standard: **ISO 22839 / Euro NCAP AEB**. - [ ] **FMCW Radar formula** $f_b = S \cdot \tau = (B/T_c) \cdot (2R/c)$. - [ ] **Range resolution** $\Delta R = c / (2B)$ — *improve by increasing bandwidth*. - [ ] **Velocity** $v_r = \lambda f_D / 2$ — improve by lower wavelength (higher $f_0$). - [ ] **Time-to-Collision** $TTC = d_{rel} / v_{rel}$ — AEB triggers at **TTC ≈ 1.2–1.5 s**. - [ ] **Required deceleration** $a_{req} = v_{rel}^2 / (2 d_{rel})$ — must be $\leq \mu g$ for stopping. - [ ] **Pinhole camera model**: $s [u,v,1]^T = K [R \mid t] X_w$ with $K = [f_x, 0, c_x; 0, f_y, c_y; 0, 0, 1]$. - [ ] **Static calibration** = stationary + mat; **Dynamic** = driving on marked road. - [ ] **Lateral departure rate (LDR)** typical threshold: **0.4–0.8 m/s**. - [ ] **Euro NCAP test speeds**: AEB Car-to-Car 30→10 km/h, AEB Pedestrian 60→20 km/h, LDW 70 km/h. - [ ] **Sensor calibration tolerance**: azimuth **±0.5°**, elevation **±0.3°** for automotive radar. - [ ] **GVT (Global Vehicle Target)** is the soft-body dummy used in Euro NCAP AEB tests — *memorize the acronym*. - [ ] **ASAM standards** (e.g., ASAM ODS) are used for *recording* ADAS test data — likely viva question. - [ ] **Functional safety**: AEB = **ASIL C/D**, LDW = **ASIL A/B**, ACC = **ASIL B**.

topics.title: Testing and validation of electric vehicle powertrains including motors, controllers, and power electronics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAUL607 - AUTOTRONICS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Testing and validation of electric vehicle powertrains including motors, controllers, and power electronics # Module Experiment 2: Testing and Validation of EV Powertrains ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCAUL607 - Autotronics Lab):** > *"Testing and validation of electric vehicle powertrains"* refers to the systematic, standards-based experimental evaluation of the three functional blocks of an EV drivetrain — the **Traction Motor**, the **Motor Controller (VFD / Inverter)**, and the **Power Electronics Stage (DC-DC Converter, On-board Charger, BMS)** — using calibrated instrumentation on a dynamometer test bench to verify that the system meets its design specifications under steady-state, transient, and fault conditions. ### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition) Imagine you have bought a brand-new *Honda Activa* electric scooter. The marketing brochure claims it can climb a steep flyover bridge, cruise at 60 km/h, and still travel 100 km on a single charge. But how does the company *prove* those numbers? They cannot just guess — they must put the vehicle on a test rig (a giant treadmill for vehicles, called a **dynamometer**), load the motor electrically and mechanically, and measure whether the actual torque, speed, current, voltage, and temperature meet the datasheet. **In simple words:** Just like a doctor runs a blood test, ECG, and X-ray before declaring you "fit," an EV powertrain must undergo electrical, mechanical, and thermal tests before it is declared "roadworthy." ### 1.2 The Three Functional Blocks of an EV Powertrain | Block | Component | Test Focus | |---|---|---| | **Traction Motor** | PMSM / BLDC / Induction / SRM | Torque-speed map, efficiency, thermal endurance | | **Motor Controller** | 3-Phase VSI Inverter (IGBT/SiC/MOSFET) | PWM fidelity, switching loss, current ripple | | **Power Electronics** | DC-DC Converter, OBC, BMS, PDU | Voltage regulation, SOC accuracy, fault response | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The validation is not a single test but a **family of tests** classified by SAE J1772, ISO 8714, AIS-156, and IEC 61851. You must be able to name at least **three test categories** and the **metrics** they produce. ### 1.3 Standard Test Metrics (Engineering Vocabulary) * **Efficiency ($\eta$):** Ratio of mechanical output power to electrical input power. Expressed in **%**, typically 85–97% for modern traction drives. * **Torque ($T$):** Rotational force, measured in **N·m**. * **Speed ($N$):** Rotational velocity, measured in **rpm** or **rad/s**. * **Power ($P$):** Time-rate of doing work, measured in **W** or **kW**. * **Specific Energy:** Energy stored per unit mass, measured in **Wh/kg** (key battery metric). * **Back-EMF Constant ($K_e$):** V/(rad/s) — defines the motor's voltage generation behaviour. * **Torque Constant ($K_t$):** N·m/A — links current to mechanical torque output. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The EV Powertrain Signal and Energy Flow An EV powertrain operates on a single principle: **convert stored electrical energy into controlled mechanical energy at the wheels, with minimum loss.** The flow is: $$\text{Battery Pack (DC)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Inverter (DC} \to \text{AC)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Traction Motor} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Transmission} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Wheels}$$ Each arrow in this chain represents a **loss mechanism** that the testing process must quantify. ## 2.2 Why Do We Test? (The "Why" Behind the Bench) * **Verification of Design Specifications** — confirm the motor delivers the claimed torque curve. * **Regulatory Compliance** — AIS-156 (India), ECE R85 (UN), FMVSS (US). * **Reliability Estimation** — MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) under thermal cycling. * **Calibration of the Vehicle Control Unit (VCU)** — torque maps, regen limits. * **Safety Assurance** — over-current, over-voltage, and short-circuit behaviour. ## 2.3 The "How" — Step-by-Step Test Philosophy 1. **Bench Setup** — Mount the motor on a **dynamometer** (dyno). The dyno acts as a controllable mechanical load. 2. **Instrumentation** — Install calibrated current sensors (LEM/Fluxgate), voltage dividers, thermocouples, and a torque transducer. 3. **Steady-State Test** — Apply a fixed load and speed, record all parameters after thermal stabilization. 4. **Transient Test** — Apply step changes in torque (load) and speed (RPM) to evaluate dynamic response. 5. **Efficiency Map Generation** — Sweep across the torque-speed envelope (e.g., 25 points × 25 points) and plot the iso-efficiency contours. 6. **Thermal Endurance Test** — Run the system at rated load for hours; record winding temperature rise. 7. **Regenerative Braking Test** — Drive the motor as a generator; quantify recovered energy. 8. **Fault Injection** — Simulate phase-to-phase short, over-temperature, encoder loss; record controller response time. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table — every formula here has appeared (or will appear) in KTU ESE papers for EV-related courses.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Mechanical Power Output | $P_{out} = T \cdot \omega$ | W | $T$ in N·m, $\omega$ in rad/s | | 2 | Electrical Power Input | $P_{in} = V_{dc} \cdot I_{dc} = \sqrt{3}\,V_{ll}\,I_{ph}\cos\phi$ | W | $\phi$ is power factor angle | | 3 | Angular Velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | rad/s | $N$ in rpm | | 4 | Motor Efficiency | $\eta_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{T \cdot \omega}{V_{dc} \cdot I_{dc}}$ | — (0 to 1) | Expressed as % | | 5 | Drive Cycle Efficiency | $\eta_{cycle} = \dfrac{\int P_{out}\,dt}{\int P_{in}\,dt}$ | — | Energy-weighted over drive cycle | | 6 | Specific Energy | $E_{sp} = \dfrac{E_{stored}}{m_{pack}}$ | Wh/kg | Battery metric | | 7 | Energy Density | $E_{vol} = \dfrac{E_{stored}}{V_{pack}}$ | Wh/L | Volumetric metric | | 8 | C-rate | $C = \dfrac{I_{discharge}}{Q_{rated}}$ | — | e.g., 1C = 1-hour discharge | | 9 | Back-EMF | $E = K_e \cdot \omega$ | V | Sinusoidal PMSM | | 10 | Motor Torque | $T = K_t \cdot I_{ph}$ | N·m | With $K_t$ in N·m/A | | 11 | $K_e$ vs $K_t$ (SI units) | $K_e = K_t$ | — | Numerically equal in SI | | 12 | Switching Loss (Inverter) | $P_{sw} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,V_{dc}\,I \cdot (t_{on}+t_{off}) \cdot f_{sw}$ | W | $f_{sw}$ in Hz | | 13 | Conduction Loss (IGBT) | $P_{cond} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_{avg} + r_d \cdot I_{rms}^{2}$ | W | Per switch | | 14 | DC-DC Efficiency | $\eta_{DC} = \dfrac{V_o I_o}{V_i I_i}$ | — | Buck/Boost topology | | 15 | RMS Phase Current | $I_{rms} = \sqrt{I_d^{2} + I_q^{2}}$ | A | FOC d-q frame | | 16 | State of Charge (Coulomb) | $SOC(t) = SOC_0 - \dfrac{1}{Q}\int_{0}^{t} i(\tau)\,d\tau$ | — | 0 to 1 | | 17 | Thermal Resistance | $\Delta T = P_{loss} \cdot R_{th}$ | °C or K | Winding-to-ambient | | 18 | Slip (Induction Motor) | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | — | Critical for IM testing | | 19 | Power Factor | $\cos\phi = \dfrac{P_{in}}{V_{rms} I_{rms}}$ | — | 0 to 1 | | 20 | Range Estimation | $R = \dfrac{E_{usable}}{\int P_{load}\,dt / d}$ | km | Wh/km metric | ### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering * These formulas feed **OEM calibration maps** that the VCU reads in real time. * Efficiency maps are pasted into vehicle simulators (MATLAB/Simulink, AVL Cruise, CarMaker) to predict range *before* building a prototype. * AIS-156 / IEC 62660 testing of batteries uses the C-rate formula to standardize abuse tests. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Motor Efficiency from First Principles **Given:** A 3-phase PMSM traction motor is being tested on a dyno. The DC bus voltage is $V_{dc} = 400$ V, the DC bus current is $I_{dc} = 50$ A, the measured torque is $T = 80$ N·m, and the measured speed is $N = 3000$ rpm. **Step 1 — Compute the electrical input power.** The input to the inverter is DC; the inverter then converts to AC. So the *electrical power drawn from the battery* is the product of DC bus voltage and DC bus current. $$\begin{aligned} P_{in} &= V_{dc} \cdot I_{dc} \\ &= 400 \text{ V} \times 50 \text{ A} \\ &= 20{,}000 \text{ W} \\ &= 20 \text{ kW} \end{aligned}$$ *Logic:* The DC bus is the only point where the battery connects to the rest of the powertrain, so multiplying its voltage and current gives total electrical input. (Note: this includes inverter losses.) **Step 2 — Convert rotational speed from rpm to rad/s.** $$\begin{aligned} \omega &= \frac{2\pi N}{60} \\ &= \frac{2 \times \pi \times 3000}{60} \\ &= \frac{6000\pi}{60} \\ &= 100\pi \text{ rad/s} \\ &\approx 314.159 \text{ rad/s} \end{aligned}$$ *Logic:* The mechanical power formula $P = T\omega$ requires angular velocity in **rad/s**, not rpm. The conversion factor is $\frac{2\pi}{60} \approx 0.10472$. **Step 3 — Compute the mechanical output power at the motor shaft.** $$\begin{aligned} P_{out} &= T \cdot \omega \\ &= 80 \text{ N·m} \times 314.159 \text{ rad/s} \\ &= 25{,}132.7 \text{ W} \\ &\approx 25.13 \text{ kW} \end{aligned}$$ *Logic:* Torque (N·m) is a moment; angular velocity (rad/s) is the rate of rotation. Their product is power because Power = Force × velocity, and a moment converts tangential force at a radius of 1 m. **Step 4 — Calculate the overall powertrain efficiency.** $$\begin{aligned} \eta_{pt} &= \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \\ &= \frac{25{,}132.7}{20{,}000} \\ &= 1.2566 \end{aligned}$$ **Wait — efficiency > 1?!** This indicates a problem. The mechanical power measured at the motor shaft is **larger** than the DC bus input power. This is physically impossible for a passive load, but it is **legitimate** when the motor is in **regenerative braking mode** (acting as a generator and feeding power back to the bus). For a 100% motoring test, this would suggest measurement error. We will assume **motoring** and recompute the input. > [!NOTE] > **Realistic correction:** For motoring, $P_{in} > P_{out}$. A healthy PMSM drive should achieve $\eta \approx 0.92$ to $0.97$. So the correct motoring scenario would have $P_{in} \approx 26$ kW, $P_{out} \approx 25$ kW, giving $\eta \approx 96.15\%$. **Re-computed Motoring Case:** $$\begin{aligned} \eta_{motor} &= \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{25.13}{26.10} \\ &= 0.9628 \\ &= 96.28\% \end{aligned}$$ This is a realistic number for a well-designed PMSM traction drive. ## 3.2 Derivation: Energy Consumption over a Standard Drive Cycle **Given:** A test cycle (e.g., **Modified Indian Driving Cycle — MIDC**) has 12 operating points. The mean input power is 12 kW for 90 seconds, and 6 kW for 30 seconds, followed by 30 seconds of regenerative braking recovering 1 kW. **Step 1 — Energy drawn in the first 90 s (high-load motoring).** $$\begin{aligned} E_1 &= P_1 \cdot t_1 \\ &= 12{,}000 \text{ W} \times 90 \text{ s} \\ &= 1{,}080{,}000 \text{ J} \\ &= 1.08 \text{ MJ} \end{aligned}$$ *Logic:* Energy is power integrated over time. In a piecewise-constant signal, it becomes a rectangle: $E = P \times t$. **Step 2 — Energy drawn in the next 30 s (low-load motoring).** $$\begin{aligned} E_2 &= P_2 \cdot t_2 \\ &= 6{,}000 \text{ W} \times 30 \text{ s} \\ &= 180{,}000 \text{ J} \\ &= 0.18 \text{ MJ} \end{aligned}$$ *Logic:* Same rectangle principle; just smaller. **Step 3 — Energy recovered during 30 s of regen.** $$\begin{aligned} E_{regen} &= P_{regen} \cdot t_{regen} \\ &= 1{,}000 \text{ W} \times 30 \text{ s} \\ &= 30{,}000 \text{ J} = 0.03 \text{ MJ} \end{aligned}$$ *Logic:* Regenerative power is conventionally negative (power flowing **into** the battery). The magnitude is 1 kW for 30 s. **Step 4 — Net energy consumed per cycle.** $$\begin{aligned} E_{net} &= E_1 + E_2 - E_{regen} \\ &= 1.08 + 0.18 - 0.03 \\ &= 1.23 \text{ MJ} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Convert to kWh (the standard unit on dashboards).** $$\begin{aligned} E_{net,\,kWh} &= \frac{E_{net}}{3.6 \times 10^{6}} \\ &= \frac{1.23 \times 10^{6}}{3.6 \times 10^{6}} \\ &= 0.3417 \text{ kWh} \end{aligned}$$ *Logic:* $1 \text{ kWh} = 1000 \text{ W} \times 3600 \text{ s} = 3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ J}$. Always divide Joules by $3.6 \times 10^6$ to get kWh. ## 3.3 Derivation: State of Charge (SOC) using Coulomb Counting **Given:** A 60 Ah battery starts at $SOC_0 = 1.0$ (fully charged). The vehicle draws a constant current of 30 A for 1 hour. **Step 1 — Identify the C-rate.** $$\begin{aligned} C &= \frac{I}{Q} = \frac{30 \text{ A}}{60 \text{ Ah}} = 0.5 \text{ C} \end{aligned}$$ *Logic:* C-rate is dimensionless. A 0.5 C discharge of a 60 Ah pack means 30 A. This will empty the pack in exactly 2 hours. **Step 2 — Integrate the current over the time interval.** $$\begin{aligned} \int_0^{t} i(\tau)\,d\tau &= 30 \text{ A} \times 3600 \text{ s} \\ &= 108{,}000 \text{ As} = 30 \text{ Ah} \end{aligned}$$ *Logic:* 1 hour = 3600 seconds. Ampere-seconds is energy-equivalent charge; divide by 3600 to get Ampere-hours. **Step 3 — Update the SOC.** $$\begin{aligned} SOC(t) &= SOC_0 - \frac{1}{Q}\int_0^{t} i(\tau)\,d\tau \\ &= 1.0 - \frac{30 \text{ Ah}}{60 \text{ Ah}} \\ &= 1.0 - 0.5 \\ &= 0.5 \end{aligned}$$ *Logic:* After 1 hour at 0.5 C, exactly half the capacity is gone. SOC = 50%. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Test Procedure Decision Table | Test Type | Stimulus | Measurement | Pass Criterion | |---|---|---|---| | **No-load Back-EMF** | Drive motor at rated speed with inverter OFF | Line-to-line voltage | Within $\pm 5\%$ of $K_e \cdot \omega$ | | **Locked-Rotor Torque** | Hold rotor stationary, apply max current | Stall torque | $\geq 1.5 \times$ rated torque | | **Load Test (Rated)** | Apply rated torque at rated speed | $T, N, V_{dc}, I_{dc}, T_{winding}$ | $\eta \geq 90\%$, $\Delta T \leq$ Class limit | | **Efficiency Map** | Sweep $T, N$ grid (e.g., 25×25) | Power in/out | Peak $\eta \geq 94\%$ | | **Regen Test** | Motor as generator, apply negative torque | $V_{bus}, I_{bus}$ | $\eta_{regen} \geq 60\%$ | | **Thermal Endurance** | Rated load, ambient $T_a = 40\,°$C, run 4 h | Winding temp | $T_{winding} \leq 180\,°$C (Class H) | | **Over-speed** | Drive at $1.2 \times$ max speed for 2 min | Vibration, balance | No mechanical failure | | **Insulation (Hi-Pot)** | Apply 1500 V AC for 1 min across insulation | Leakage current | $I_{leak} \leq 1$ mA | | **EMC (Conducted)** | Inject noise into DC bus | Conducted emission | Below CISPR 25 Class 3 limit | | **BMS Communication** | Trigger fault (over-voltage) | CAN message latency | Response $< 100$ ms | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — EV Powertrain Test Bench Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Grid / Battery
Simulator DC Source] --> B[DC Link
Capacitor Bank] B --> C[3-Phase Inverter
IGBT or SiC VSI] C --> D[Current Sensors
LEM LA 305-S] D --> E[Traction Motor
PMSM 50 kW] E --> F[Torque Transducer
HBM T12] F --> G[Dynamometer
AC Induction Load] G --> H[Speed Encoder
Incremental 1024 ppr] H --> I[Data Acquisition
NI cDAQ / Hioki PW3390] I --> J[Host PC
LabVIEW / CANape] B --> K[DC-DC Converter
Aux 12V] K --> L[12V Battery
Aux Loads] C --> M[Controller Board
TI TMS320F28379D] M --> N[CAN Bus
500 kbps] N --> J style A fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style E fill:#80CFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style M fill:#FFB3B3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style J fill:#C2F0C2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` > **Reading guide:** Power flows from the **DC source** (left) through the **inverter** to the **motor**, then mechanically to the **dynamometer**. Telemetry from sensors travels via the **DAQ** to the **PC**. The **controller board** commands the inverter through a real-time link. ## 4.2 Flowchart — Test Sequence for Efficiency Map Generation ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Start: Mount Motor on Dyno] --> S2[Connect Sensors
Torque, Current, Voltage, Thermocouple] S2 --> S3[Initialize Inverter
Spin Motor at No-Load] S3 --> S4[Verify Direction
