and Encoder Polarity] S4 --> S5{For each speed point N_i} S5 --> S6[Hold N_i constant via dyno] S6 --> S7{For each torque point T_j} S7 --> S8[Apply T_j load via dyno] S8 --> S9[Wait for thermal steady-state 60s] S9 --> S10[Acquire V_dc, I_dc, T, N, T_winding] S10 --> S11[Compute eta_ij = T x omega / V x I] S11 --> S7 S7 --> S5 S5 --> S12[Generate eta Map
25 x 25 grid] S12 --> S13[Plot Iso-Efficiency Contours] S13 --> S14[Report: Peak eta, Rated eta, Operating Envelope] S14 --> End[End of Test] style S1 fill:#E0F0FF,stroke:#333 style S14 fill:#FFE0E0,stroke:#333 style End fill:#D0FFD0,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Power Electronics Validation Subsystem ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Inverter Module] --> P2[Gate Driver
Board] P2 --> P3[PWM Generation
DSP / FPGA] P3 --> P4[Current & Voltage
Feedback Sensors] P4 --> P5[Protection Logic
OCP OVP OTP] P5 --> P6[Fault Relay
and Shutdown] P7[DC-DC Buck-Boost
Converter] --> P8[Inductor &
Capacitor Bank] P8 --> P9[Output Filter
LC Stage] P9 --> P10[Aux 12V Bus
Loads] P11[BMS Slave
AFE IC] --> P12[Cell Voltage
Monitor] P12 --> P13[Temperature
Sensors NTC] P13 --> P14[CAN / SPI
Communication] P14 --> P15[VCU Master
Controller] style P1 fill:#FFCC99,stroke:#333 style P7 fill:#FFCC99,stroke:#333 style P11 fill:#CCFFCC,stroke:#333 style P15 fill:#FFB3B3,stroke:#333 ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** > Define the term *"Back-EMF constant"* ($K_e$) of a PMSM and state its SI unit. Why is it numerically equal to the torque constant $K_t$ in SI units? **Model Answer (3 marks):** The back-EMF constant $K_e$ of a permanent magnet synchronous motor is defined as the RMS line-to-line voltage induced per unit mechanical angular velocity when the motor is driven externally as a generator at open circuit. $$E_{LL,\,rms} = K_e \cdot \omega$$ Its SI unit is **V·s/rad** (volt-second per radian), which is dimensionally equivalent to **N·m/A** — exactly the unit of the torque constant $K_t$. In SI units, the numerical values of $K_e$ (in V·s/rad) and $K_t$ (in N·m/A) are equal because both are manifestations of the same stored magnetic co-energy in the air gap, and the SI system is internally consistent (Watt = Volt × Amp = N·m × rad/s). This equality stems directly from the principle of energy conservation in electromechanical energy conversion. **(3 marks)** --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** > List any **three** standard tests performed on a traction motor and the corresponding parameter each test validates. **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **No-load Back-EMF Test** — validates the **magnetic flux linkage** and integrity of permanent magnets; compares measured $E$ against the analytical value $K_e \omega$. **(1 mark)** 2. **Locked-Rotor (Stall) Test** — validates the **maximum torque capability** of the motor and the thermal limit of the winding; ensures $T_{stall} \geq 1.5 \times T_{rated}$. **(1 mark)** 3. **Thermal Endurance Test** — validates the **insulation class** and continuous power rating; ensures winding temperature rise is within the limit (e.g., 125 K for Class F). **(1 mark)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** > **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an EV powertrain test bench used for validating a 3-phase PMSM traction motor. Clearly identify the role of each subsystem. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A PMSM traction motor is tested on a dynamometer. The measured DC link voltage is 360 V, the DC link current is 80 A, the motor torque is 110 N·m, and the speed is 3500 rpm. Calculate (i) the mechanical output power, (ii) the electrical input power, and (iii) the overall powertrain efficiency. Comment on whether the result is realistic for a motoring operation. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks) The EV powertrain test bench consists of the following subsystems (refer to Section 4.1 diagram): * **Programmable DC Source / Battery Simulator:** Provides a stable, controllable DC bus voltage and current. Modern testers (e.g., Chroma 17020, EA PSI 9000) can simulate battery SOC and internal resistance. **(1 mark)** * **DC Link Capacitor Bank:** Smooths the high-frequency ripple drawn by the inverter and provides instantaneous reactive current. **(0.5 mark)** * **3-Phase Voltage Source Inverter (VSI):** Converts DC to variable-frequency AC using SVPWM. It houses the IGBT/SiC modules and gate drivers. **(1 mark)** * **Traction Motor (PMSM):** The Device Under Test (DUT). **(0.5 mark)** * **Torque Transducer + Speed Encoder:** Measure the mechanical output precisely. Common models: HBM T12 (torque), Hengstler RI 58 (encoder). **(1 mark)** * **Dynamometer (Dyno):** Acts as a programmable mechanical load. AC induction machines driving four-quadrant regenerative converters are standard. **(1 mark)** * **Data Acquisition System (DAQ):** Synchronously samples all electrical and mechanical signals; sample rate $\geq 100$ kS/s for inverter tests. **(1 mark)** * **Thermal Sensors (Thermocouples / RTDs):** Embedded in the motor windings, end-windings, and bearings to monitor hot-spots. **(0.5 mark)** * **Controller / DAQ Software:** Runs the test script, commands the inverter via CAN or analog, and stores results. **(0.5 mark)** #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks) **Given:** $V_{dc} = 360$ V, $I_{dc} = 80$ A, $T = 110$ N·m, $N = 3500$ rpm. **(i) Mechanical output power (3 marks):** $$\begin{aligned} \omega &= \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 3500}{60} = \frac{7000\pi}{60} = \frac{350\pi}{3} \text{ rad/s} \approx 366.52 \text{ rad/s} \\ P_{out} &= T \cdot \omega = 110 \times 366.52 \approx 40{,}317 \text{ W} \approx 40.32 \text{ kW} \end{aligned}$$ **(ii) Electrical input power (2 marks):** $$\begin{aligned} P_{in} &= V_{dc} \cdot I_{dc} = 360 \times 80 = 28{,}800 \text{ W} = 28.8 \text{ kW} \end{aligned}$$ **(iii) Powertrain efficiency (2 marks):** $$\begin{aligned} \eta_{pt} &= \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{40.32}{28.8} \approx 1.40 \;(140\%) \end{aligned}$$ **Comment:** The result $P_{out} > P_{in}$ is **physically impossible for motoring** because it violates energy conservation. For a passive motoring load, efficiency must satisfy $\eta \leq 1$. This means the motor is in **regenerative mode** — acting as a generator and pumping power back into the DC bus. To make it a valid motoring test, either the load torque should be increased (raising $P_{in}$) or the speed should be reduced. A realistic PMSM drive in motoring should report $\eta$ in the range 90%–97%. **(Bonus: 0–1 mark for the comment, included in the 2 marks above.)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative to Question A* > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** > **(a)** Describe the **switching loss** and **conduction loss** mechanisms in a 3-phase VSI inverter used in an EV. Give the formulas and explain how they affect the inverter efficiency. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** An EV traction battery has a usable capacity of 40 kWh. The vehicle is driven on a cycle where the average power consumption from the battery is 8 kW, and the average regen power returned to the battery is 1.5 kW for 25% of the trip time. If the trip lasts 2 hours, calculate (i) the net energy consumed, (ii) the percentage SOC drop, and (iii) the expected range if the average speed is 50 km/h. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks) A 3-phase VSI inverter uses six semiconductor switches (typically IGBTs with antiparallel diodes) arranged in a bridge. It has two principal loss mechanisms: **1. Switching Loss (3 marks):** Switching loss occurs during the brief interval when a switch transitions between OFF and ON (or vice versa). During this overlap, both voltage and current are present simultaneously, dissipating energy. $$\begin{aligned} P_{sw} &= \frac{1}{2} V_{dc} I \cdot (t_{on} + t_{off}) \cdot f_{sw} \end{aligned}$$ Here $t_{on}$ and $t_{off}$ are the turn-on and turn-off times, $f_{sw}$ is the switching frequency (typically 5–20 kHz for IGBT, up to 100 kHz for SiC MOSFET), $V_{dc}$ is the DC link voltage, and $I$ is the current being switched. For a 3-phase inverter, total switching loss is approximately **6 × (single switch loss)** since all six switches operate once per PWM period. **Effect on efficiency:** Higher $f_{sw}$ reduces current ripple and improves torque smoothness, but increases $P_{sw}$ linearly, lowering efficiency and raising junction temperature. **2. Conduction Loss (3 marks):** Conduction loss is the $I^2R$-like dissipation while the switch is fully ON. For an IGBT, the on-state voltage drop is modelled as a constant $V_{CE(sat)}$ plus a dynamic resistance $r_d$: $$\begin{aligned} P_{cond} &= V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_{avg} + r_d \cdot I_{rms}^{2} \end{aligned}$$ For a MOSFET, $V_{CE(sat)}$ is replaced by $R_{DS(on)} \cdot I^2$. Total inverter conduction loss is again summed over six devices. **Effect on efficiency:** Conduction loss scales with the square of current, so it dominates at high torque (high current) operation. **Combined Inverter Efficiency (1 mark):** $$\begin{aligned} \eta_{inv} = \frac{P_{out,AC}}{P_{in,DC}} = \frac{P_{out,AC}}{P_{out,AC} + P_{sw} + P_{cond} + P_{aux}} \end{aligned}$$ A well-designed EV inverter achieves $\eta_{inv} \approx 96\%$–$99\%$. #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks) **Given:** $E_{usable} = 40$ kWh, $P_{load} = 8$ kW (motoring), $P_{regen} = 1.5$ kW, $t_{regen} = 25\% \times 2 \text{ h} = 0.5 \text{ h}$, total $t_{trip} = 2$ h, $v_{avg} = 50$ km/h. **(i) Net energy consumed (3 marks):** Motoring time: $t_{mot} = 2 - 0.5 = 1.5$ h. $$\begin{aligned} E_{in} &= P_{load} \cdot t_{mot} = 8 \times 1.5 = 12 \text{ kWh} \\ E_{out} &= P_{regen} \cdot t_{regen} = 1.5 \times 0.5 = 0.75 \text{ kWh} \\ E_{net} &= E_{in} - E_{out} = 12 - 0.75 = 11.25 \text{ kWh} \end{aligned}$$ **(ii) Percentage SOC drop (2 marks):** $$\begin{aligned} \Delta SOC (\%) &= \frac{E_{net}}{E_{usable}} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{11.25}{40} \times 100 \\ &= 28.125\% \end{aligned}$$ **(iii) Expected range (2 marks):** $$\begin{aligned} d &= v_{avg} \cdot t_{trip} = 50 \times 2 = 100 \text{ km} \end{aligned}$$ (Alternatively, energy economy $= E_{net} / d = 11.25/100 = 0.1125$ kWh/km, and full range $= 40/0.1125 \approx 355.5$ km — but the question asked for the *trip* range, which is simply $100$ km.) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Mixing units:** Writing $\omega$ in rpm while multiplying by $T$ to get $P$ — this is the **single most common** error in EV numerical problems. Always convert $N$ to $\omega$ in rad/s. > 2. **Forgetting regen polarity:** Energy recovered via regen is *subtracted* from $E_{in}$, not added. A sign error here costs 2–3 marks in one shot. > 3. **Not stating the kWh conversion:** $1$ kWh $= 3.6 \times 10^6$ J. Examiners *expect* you to show this constant explicitly. > 4. **Vague block diagrams:** A "neat block diagram" must show the *direction* of power flow and *signal* lines separately. Use arrows and labels. > 5. **Omitting the assumption:** Always state *"Assuming steady-state, no-load losses neglected, sinusoidal back-EMF…"* in derivations. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — read this 5 minutes before the exam.** * ✅ EV powertrain = **Battery → Inverter → Motor → Transmission → Wheels**. * ✅ Test bench pillars: **DC source, Inverter (DUT or drive), Motor (DUT), Dyno, DAQ, Controller, Thermal sensors.** * ✅ Three core test types: **Steady-state, Transient, Endurance / Thermal.** * ✅ Master the **efficiency formula** $\eta = T\omega / (V_{dc} I_{dc})$ — and the **rpm → rad/s** conversion. * ✅ Know the two inverter losses: **Switching loss** (∝ $f_{sw}$) and **Conduction loss** (∝ $I^2$). * ✅ Regenerative braking makes $P_{out} > P_{in}$ possible — it is a *sign convention*, not a violation of physics. * ✅ **C-rate** = current / rated capacity (unitless). 1C = full discharge in 1 hour. * ✅ **SOC** uses Coulomb counting: $SOC(t) = SOC_0 - \frac{1}{Q}\int i\,dt$. * ✅ Standards to memorize: **AIS-156** (India, batteries), **AIS-039** (electric power train), **ISO 8714** (electric road vehicles — reference energy consumption), **IEC 61851** (conductive charging), **SAE J1772** (charging coupler). * ✅ Always state **assumptions** in derivations (steady-state, sinusoidal, ideal switches, no-load losses neglected). * ✅ For PMSM: $K_e$ (V·s/rad) **=** $K_t$ (N·m/A) numerically in SI units. * ✅ Drive cycle efficiency is energy-weighted: integrate first, then divide. * ✅ Peak efficiency of modern EV traction drives: **94%–97%** (PMSM + SiC inverter). * ✅ In exam diagrams, label both **power flow** (thick arrows) and **signal flow** (thin arrows). * ✅ Common numerical traps: mixing kW/W, missing $\sqrt{3}$ in 3-phase formulas, forgetting $2\pi/60$ conversion. ---

topics.title: Understanding and testing vehicle telematics systems, including GPS, infotainment, and vehicle-to-everything (V2X) communication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL607 - AUTOTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Understanding and testing vehicle telematics systems, including GPS, infotainment, and vehicle-to-everything (V2X) communication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Vehicle Telematics?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Automotive Telematics):**
> *Vehicle Telematics* is an interdisciplinary field that integrates **telecommunications**, **informatics**, **sensing**, and **on-board vehicular electronics** to enable bidirectional data exchange between a vehicle and its external environment, with the primary objectives of real-time navigation, remote diagnostics, fleet management, safety enhancement, and infotainment delivery.

In simpler terms, telematics = **Telecommunications + Informatics**. It is the digital nervous system of a modern car.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Under the NEP 2020 outcome-based framework, this experiment is mapped to **CO2: *Demonstrate the working of automotive sensors, actuators, and communication subsystems through experimentation*** and **CO5: *Apply modern automotive standards (CAN, LIN, V2X, GPS) to diagnose and validate integrated vehicle systems***.

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Vehicle Telematics

| # | Subsystem | One-Line Purpose | Real-World Analogy |
|---|-----------|-----------------|--------------------|
| 1 | **GNSS / GPS** | "Where is the vehicle?" | A satellite-based compass with atomic-clock precision. |
| 2 | **Infotainment** | "What does the driver/passenger see, hear, and control?" | The car's smart-tablet dashboard, just like a smartphone on wheels. |
| 3 | **V2X Communication** | "How does the car talk to the world around it?" | Walkie-talkies that let cars politely warn each other before a crash. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you are blindfolded inside an unfamiliar car. To know **where** you are, you listen to four satellites shouting their exact time. By comparing how long their shouts took to reach you, you can triangulate your position (this is **GPS**). To stay **entertained and informed**, you have a tablet on the dashboard that plays music, shows maps, and reads text messages aloud (this is **Infotainment**). Finally, to **avoid accidents**, your car constantly whispers warnings to nearby cars and traffic lights: *"Hey, I'm braking hard at the next junction!"* — this is **V2X**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics to Remember (Board-Exam Favorites):**
> - GPS uses the **L1 (1575.42 MHz)** and **L2 (1227.60 MHz)** carrier bands.
> - The speed of light used for GPS ranging: **c = 299,792,458 m/s** (exact by SI definition).
> - WGS-84 is the standard geodetic datum for civilian GPS with the Earth's semi-major axis **a = 6378.137 km** and flattening **1/f ≈ 298.257223563**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Subsystem 1 — Global Positioning System (GPS)

### 2.1.1 Operational Concept

The GPS is a **constellation of at least 24 Medium Earth Orbit (MEO) satellites** orbiting at an altitude of approximately **20,200 km** with an orbital period of **~11 hours 58 minutes** (half a sidereal day). Each satellite continuously broadcasts a *navigation message* containing:

1. Its precise **ephemeris** (orbital parameters),
2. **Almanac data** for the entire constellation,
3. A **time-stamp** referenced to the GPS Time (GPST) atomic clock ensemble.

### 2.1.2 Why Four Satellites Are Needed

A 2D fix requires **3 satellites** (solving x, y, t). A 3D fix requires **4 satellites** because the receiver clock is not synchronized with the atomic clocks on the satellites. The four unknowns are **(x, y, z, t)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The pseudo-range equation uses the receiver's *unsynchronized* clock, hence the term **pseudo**-range. Once four pseudo-ranges are measured, a closed-form solution for (x, y, z) and clock bias **b** is obtained.

### 2.1.3 The KTU Formula Sheet (Tested Topics)

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Pseudo-range** | $\rho_i = c \, (t_{r} - t_{t,i})$ | $\rho_i$: pseudo-range to satellite $i$ (m), $c$: speed of light (m/s), $t_r$: reception time, $t_{t,i}$: transmission time | Core of GPS positioning |
| 2 | **Geometric Range** | $R_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x)^2 + (y_i - y)^2 + (z_i - z)^2}$ | $R_i$: true range to satellite $i$ (m), $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$: satellite coordinates, $(x, y, z)$: receiver coordinates | Trilateration geometry |
| 3 | **Corrected Pseudo-range** | $\rho_i = R_i + c \, b + I_i + T_i + \varepsilon_i$ | $b$: receiver clock bias (s), $I_i$: ionospheric delay (m), $T_i$: tropospheric delay (m), $\varepsilon_i$: noise (m) | Full error-budget model |
| 4 | **GDOP** | $\text{GDOP} = \sqrt{\text{trace}(H^{-1})}$ | $H$: geometry matrix from satellite positions | Indicates fix quality; GDOP $\le 4$ is good |
| 5 | **Position Error** | $\sigma_P = \text{PDOP} \times \sigma_{UERE}$ | $\sigma_{UERE}$: User Equivalent Range Error (m), typically 2–5 m for civilian code | Predicts horizontal accuracy |
| 6 | **Free-Space Path Loss (V2X)** | $\text{FSPL} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | $d$: distance (km), $f$: frequency (MHz) | Predicts V2X link budget |
| 7 | **Maximum Range from Latency** | $d_{\max} = c \times \frac{\tau_{\text{latency}}}{2}$ | $\tau_{\text{latency}}$: round-trip latency budget (s) | Used in DSRC safety messaging |
| 8 | **Data Rate Limit (Shannon)** | $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | $B$: bandwidth (Hz), SNR: signal-to-noise ratio (linear) | Infotainment throughput planning |
| 9 | **Carrier Frequency Wavelength** | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | $f$: frequency (Hz) | Antenna design for V2X (5.9 GHz) |
| 10 | **Maximum Refresh Rate** | $f_{\text{BASIC CAM}} = 10 \text{ Hz}$ (ETSI standard) | Cooperative Awareness Message rate | ETSI EN 302 637-2 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why GDOP Matters:** Even with a perfect receiver, a "bad sky" (satellites clustered together) inflates position error. The Dilution of Precision (DOP) family includes **GDOP** (overall), **PDOP** (position), **HDOP** (horizontal), **VDOP** (vertical), and **TDOP** (time).

### 2.2 Subsystem 2 — Automotive Infotainment

### 2.2.1 Definition and Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> **Infotainment = Information + Entertainment.** It is the in-vehicle cluster of head-unit, display, HMI, audio amplifiers, and connected apps that delivers navigation, media, telephony, climate, and ADAS visualizations to occupants.

The modern infotainment system is built on a **System-on-Chip (SoC)** that runs an **OS** — typically:
- **QNX Neutrino** (Blackberry QNX) — the safety-certified industry leader.
- **Android Automotive OS (AAOS)** — used by Volvo, Polestar, GM.
- **Linux-based (Automotive Grade Linux / AGL)** — open-source consortium.
- **Apple CarPlay / Android Auto** — *projection* modes, not full OS replacements.

### 2.2.2 Internal Bus Hierarchy

| Bus | Speed | Use Case |
|-----|-------|----------|
| **CAN** | 500 kbps – 1 Mbps | Powertrain, body, chassis |
| **LIN** | 19.2 kbps | Low-cost actuators (mirrors, seats) |
| **MOST** | 25 – 150 Mbps | Legacy infotainment backbone (fiber) |
| **Automotive Ethernet (100BASE-T1 / 1000BASE-T1)** | 100 Mbps – 1 Gbps | Modern ADAS, IVI, backbone |
| **A2B (Analog Devices)** | 50 Mbps | Audio daisy-chain |
| **USB, Bluetooth, Wi-Fi** | varies | Phone-tethering, screen-mirroring |

### 2.3 Subsystem 3 — V2X Communication

### 2.3.1 The V2X Family

| Acronym | Meaning | Typical Range | Latency Target |
|---------|---------|---------------|----------------|
| **V2V** | Vehicle-to-Vehicle | 300 m | < 20 ms |
| **V2I** | Vehicle-to-Infrastructure (RSU) | 1 km | < 100 ms |
| **V2P** | Vehicle-to-Pedestrian | 50 m | < 100 ms |
| **V2N** | Vehicle-to-Network (cellular) | wide-area | < 1 s (tolerated) |
| **V2G** | Vehicle-to-Grid | backhaul | non-safety critical |
| **V2X** | Umbrella term | – | – |

### 2.3.2 The Two Competing V2X Standards

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DSRC (Dedicated Short-Range Communications)** — based on **IEEE 802.11p** Wi-Fi variant, uses the 5.85–5.925 GHz band (US) / 5.855–5.905 GHz (EU). Operates without cellular coverage. Latency ~ 4 ms.
> **C-V2X (Cellular V2X)** — defined by **3GPP Release 14** onward. Two modes: **PC5** (sidelink, direct, like DSRC) and **Uu** (via cellular base station). 5G-V2X (Rel. 16+) targets **URLLC** with < 5 ms latency and < 99.999% reliability.

### 2.3.3 Engineering Trade-Offs (Board-Exam Favorite)

| Parameter | DSRC (802.11p) | C-V2X (LTE) | 5G-V2X |
|-----------|----------------|-------------|--------|
| Spectrum | 75 MHz at 5.9 GHz | Cellular bands + ITS 5.9 GHz (PC5) | 5G NR (FR1) |
| Range | ~ 300 m | ~ 1 km | ~ 1 km |
| Latency | 4 – 50 ms | 20 – 100 ms | < 5 ms (URLLC) |
| Mobility support | moderate | excellent | excellent |
| Deployment cost | low (no SIM) | requires cellular | higher |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — GPS Pseudo-Range Trilateration (3D Fix)

We derive the canonical four-equation system used inside a GPS receiver to compute position $(x, y, z)$ and clock bias $b$ (in metres, $b = c \cdot \Delta t$).

### Step 1 — Measure the pseudo-range

The receiver detects the time of arrival $t_r$ of a signal transmitted by satellite $i$ at time $t_{t,i}$. Since the receiver's clock has an unknown bias $\Delta t$, the *measured* pseudo-range is:

$$\rho_i = c \, (t_r - t_{t,i}) = c \, (t_r^{\text{true}} + \Delta t - t_{t,i})$$

### Step 2 — Express the true geometric range

The Euclidean distance between the receiver at $(x, y, z)$ and satellite $i$ at $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$ in WGS-84 ECEF coordinates is:

$$R_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x)^2 + (y_i - y)^2 + (z_i - z)^2}$$

### Step 3 — Combine the two expressions

For the ideal noise-free case, $\rho_i = R_i + c \, \Delta t$. The full measurement equation becomes:

$$\rho_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x)^2 + (y_i - y)^2 + (z_i - z)^2} + c \, \Delta t$$

### Step 4 — Write the system for $N$ satellites (here $N=4$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_1 &= \sqrt{(x_1 - x)^2 + (y_1 - y)^2 + (z_1 - z)^2} + c \, b \\
\rho_2 &= \sqrt{(x_2 - x)^2 + (y_2 - y)^2 + (z_2 - z)^2} + c \, b \\
\rho_3 &= \sqrt{(x_3 - x)^2 + (y_3 - y)^2 + (z_3 - z)^2} + c \, b \\
\rho_4 &= \sqrt{(x_4 - x)^2 + (y_4 - y)^2 + (z_4 - z)^2} + c \, b
\end{aligned}
$$

where the clock bias in metres is $b = c \, \Delta t$.

### Step 5 — Linearise with a Taylor expansion about an initial guess $(x_0, y_0, z_0, b_0)$

Let $R_i^0 = \sqrt{(x_i - x_0)^2 + (y_i - y_0)^2 + (z_i - z_0)^2}$. Differentiating w.r.t. $(x, y, z, b)$ gives the geometry matrix $H$:

$$H = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{-(x_1 - x_0)}{R_1^0} & \frac{-(y_1 - y_0)}{R_1^0} & \frac{-(z_1 - z_0)}{R_1^0} & 1 \\ \frac{-(x_2 - x_0)}{R_2^0} & \frac{-(y_2 - y_0)}{R_2^0} & \frac{-(z_2 - z_0)}{R_2^0} & 1 \\ \frac{-(x_3 - x_0)}{R_3^0} & \frac{-(y_3 - y_0)}{R_3^0} & \frac{-(z_3 - z_0)}{R_3^0} & 1 \\ \frac{-(x_4 - x_0)}{R_4^0} & \frac{-(y_4 - y_0)}{R_4^0} & \frac{-(z_4 - z_0)}{R_4^0} & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 6 — Solve iteratively using the weighted least-squares (WLS) update

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta \rho_i &= \rho_i^{\text{measured}} - R_i^0 - c \, b_0 \\
\Delta \mathbf{x} &= (H^T H)^{-1} H^T \, \Delta \boldsymbol{\rho} \\
\mathbf{x}_{\text{new}} &= \mathbf{x}_{\text{old}} + \Delta \mathbf{x}
\end{aligned}
$$

The iterations continue until $\vert \Delta \mathbf{x} \vert < \varepsilon$ (typically 1 cm). This is the standard "navigation solution" executed in real GPS receivers at **1 Hz to 50 Hz** depending on application.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) for a V2X Link

We derive the link-budget expression used in the lab to predict whether a 5.9 GHz DSRC packet will reach a receiver at distance $d$.

### Step 1 — Start from the Friis transmission equation

$$\frac{P_r}{P_t} = G_t G_r \left( \frac{\lambda}{4 \pi d} \right)^2$$

### Step 2 — Convert to dB and substitute $\lambda = c / f$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FSPL}_{\text{dB}} &= 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_t}{P_r}\right) \bigg|_{G_t = G_r = 1} \\
&= 20 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{4 \pi d}{\lambda} \right) \\
&= 20 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{4 \pi d \, f}{c} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert $d$ to km and $f$ to MHz, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s

$$\boxed{\text{FSPL (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20 \log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}}) + 32.44}$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Example (Board-Style)

> **Problem:** A V2X transmitter broadcasts at **5.9 GHz** with **20 dBm** EIRP. The receiver sensitivity is **−90 dBm**. Antenna gains are 0 dBi each. Find the maximum communication range.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_r^{\min} &= P_t + G_t + G_r - \text{FSPL}_{\max} \\
-90 &= 20 - \text{FSPL}_{\max} \\
\text{FSPL}_{\max} &= 110 \text{ dB} \\
110 &= 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(5900) + 32.44 \\
110 &= 20 \log_{10}(d) + 75.43 + 32.44 \\
20 \log_{10}(d) &= 110 - 107.87 = 2.13 \text{ dB} \\
\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) &= 0.1065 \\
d_{\max} &\approx 1.28 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The theoretical free-space range is **~ 1.28 km**. In practice, urban clutter (buildings, foliage, vehicles) reduces this to **300 – 500 m**, which is why V2X safety messages are designed for that horizon.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Wavelength and Antenna Sizing for V2X

$$
\begin{aligned}
f &= 5.9 \text{ GHz} \\
\lambda &= \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{5.9 \times 10^9} = 5.08 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m} \approx 50.8 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

A standard quarter-wave monopole antenna used in the lab will therefore be:

$$\frac{\lambda}{4} = \frac{50.8}{4} = 12.7 \text{ mm}$$

This is a tested, easily-remembered figure for KTU viva voce questions.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How the Three Subsystems Interact

| Trigger Event | GPS Contribution | Infotainment Role | V2X Role |
|---------------|------------------|-------------------|----------|
| Sudden hard brake | Reports speed drop, position | Haptic/visual alert, optional dashcam record | CAM/DENM broadcast → nearby vehicles |
| Approaching red light | Lane-level fix (< 1.5 m) | Updated map ETA, green-wave advisory | SPaT (Signal Phase and Timing) from RSU |
| Highway pile-up ahead | None directly | "Slow traffic in 2 km" alert via TMC | IMA (Incident Messaging Application) from authority |
| EV at charging station | Precise dock position | Payment, billing via app | V2G handshake with grid operator |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]*
> **Define Vehicle Telematics. List any FOUR major applications of telematics in modern automobiles.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Definition (1 mark):** *Vehicle telematics is the integrated use of telecommunications, information technology, sensors, and on-board vehicular electronics for two-way exchange of data between a vehicle and its external ecosystem, including satellites, roadside infrastructure, other vehicles, and cloud servers.*
>
> **Four applications (2 marks — ½ mark each):**
> 1. **Real-time navigation and route guidance** using GNSS/GPS.
> 2. **Remote diagnostics and predictive maintenance** via OBD-II + cloud.
> 3. **Fleet management and stolen-vehicle recovery** using cellular TCU.
> 4. **V2X safety messaging** (CAM/DENM) to prevent collisions.
> 5. *(Bonus acceptable)* Usage-based insurance (UBI) and infotainment streaming.

---

> **Q2.** *[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]*
> **Differentiate between DSRC and C-V2X with respect to PHY/MAC layer standards, spectrum, and latency.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per row of comparison):**

| Parameter | DSRC | C-V2X |
|-----------|------|-------|
| Standard | IEEE 802.11p (WAVE stack) | 3GPP Release 14 / 15 (LTE), 16 (5G) |
| Spectrum | 5.85 – 5.925 GHz (75 MHz, dedicated ITS band) | ITS 5.9 GHz (PC5 sidelink) *and* licensed cellular bands (Uu) |
| Typical latency | ~ 4 – 50 ms | ~ 20 – 100 ms (LTE), < 5 ms (5G URLLC) |
| **Exam-tip line:** | "**Wi-Fi on wheels**" | "**Cellular on wheels**" |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

### ➤ Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an integrated **vehicle telematics system**. Clearly identify the roles of the **GNSS receiver**, **TCU**, **infotainment head unit**, and **V2X modem**. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A vehicle receives signals from four GPS satellites. Derive the four pseudo-range equations used to compute the receiver's 3D position and clock bias. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Block diagram (3 marks):** Draw the architecture shown in SECTION 4.1 of these notes. The examiner expects a minimum of these labelled blocks:
- GNSS antenna + receiver (L1/L5)
- Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)
- CAN bus from ECU
- Telematics Control Unit (TCU) with cellular modem
- Infotainment Head Unit SoC with display + HMI
- V2X modem (5.9 GHz) with antenna
- Cloud / OEM backend

**Functional explanation (4 marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **GNSS receiver:** provides absolute position, velocity, time (PVT) by trilateration against ≥ 4 satellites; refresh 1–10 Hz.
2. **TCU:** aggregates vehicle data, transmits via cellular (4G/5G) to OEM cloud; enables remote commands (lock/unlock, AC start), OTA updates, and emergency calling (eCall / ERA-GLONASS).
3. **Infotainment head unit:** runs the HMI, navigation, media, telephony, and ADAS visualization; bridges smartphone protocols (CarPlay/AA) and renders maps.
4. **V2X modem:** broadcasts/receives CAM (10 Hz) and DENM (event-driven) using 802.11p or C-V2X PC5 to enable cooperative safety and traffic efficiency.

#### Model Solution — (b)

The derivation in **SECTION 3.1** of these notes is the complete answer. To summarise, present:
- Step 1: The pseudo-range measurement equation $\rho_i = c \, (t_r - t_{t,i})$.
- Step 2: True range $R_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x)^2 + (y_i - y)^2 + (z_i - z)^2}$.
- Step 3: Combined form including receiver clock bias $b = c \Delta t$.
- Step 4: The **four-equation system** for $N = 4$ satellites (write all four lines).
- Step 5: Linearisation via Taylor expansion leading to the geometry matrix $H$.
- Step 6: WLS update rule $(H^T H)^{-1} H^T \Delta \rho$.

*(7 marks are awarded: 1 mark for setup, 2 marks for writing the system, 2 marks for the matrix, 2 marks for the WLS solution.)*

---

### ➤ Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Style]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the working of **Cooperative Awareness Messaging (CAM)** and **Decentralized Event Notification Message (DENM)** in V2X. State which ETSI standard defines each, the message frequency, and a typical safety use case. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A V2X transmitter operating at **5.9 GHz** with **EIRP = 23 dBm** must reach a receiver with **sensitivity = −92 dBm** in a line-of-sight scenario. Calculate the maximum theoretical free-space range. Use $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — (a)

| Property | CAM | DENM |
|----------|-----|------|
| Standard | **ETSI EN 302 637-2** | **ETSI EN 302 637-3** |
| Trigger | Periodic, every **100 ms (10 Hz)** | **Event-driven** (e.g., hard brake, airbag deploy) |
| Payload | Position, speed, heading, acceleration, path history | Event type, severity, position, relevance area, duration |
| Direction | One-hop broadcast | One-hop broadcast, *flooded* by forwarders |
| Use case | "I am here, doing this" — collision avoidance at intersections | "Emergency at location X" — hazardous-location notification |

**Exam-tip line:** "*CAMs are the heartbeat; DENMs are the scream.*"

(7 marks: 2 marks CAM, 2 marks DENM, 1 mark standards, 2 marks use-cases)

#### Model Solution — (b)

Use the derivation in **SECTION 3.2**, Step 4.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_t &= 23 \text{ dBm} \\
P_{r,\min} &= -92 \text{ dBm} \\
\text{Maximum allowable path loss} &= P_t - P_{r,\min} = 23 - (-92) = 115 \text{ dB} \\
115 &= 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(5900) + 32.44 \\
115 &= 20 \log_{10}(d) + 75.43 + 32.44 \\
20 \log_{10}(d) &= 115 - 107.87 = 7.13 \\
\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) &= 0.3565 \\
d_{\max} &\approx 2.27 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
$$

(7 marks: 2 marks for link-budget equation, 2 marks for substituting $f = 5900$ MHz and computing constants, 1 mark for solving $20 \log d$, 1 mark for conversion to km, 1 mark for final numerical answer with units.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout (MOST COMMON MARK-LOSS ZONES):**
> 1. **Forgetting to add the clock-bias term $b$** in the GPS equation — loses 2 marks instantly.
> 2. **Mixing up CAM and DENM** — students often swap their standards and frequencies.
> 3. **Using $f$ in GHz instead of MHz** in FSPL — the +32.44 constant depends on these units; common loss of 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Calling V2X "Bluetooth" or "Wi-Fi"** — examiners deduct for confusing 802.11p with 802.11ac.
> 5. **Writing "GPS uses 4G"** — GPS is one-way satellite reception, *not* 4G.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Memorise Bullet List — *Look at this the night before the exam*:**
> - [x] **Telematics = Telecom + Informatics**; triad is **GNSS + Infotainment + V2X**.
> - [x] GPS needs **≥ 4 satellites** for 3D fix; unknowns are $(x, y, z, b)$.
> - [x] **Speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s** is the conversion constant for *all* ranging problems.
> - [x] **FSPL constant = 32.44** *only* when $d$ is in **km** and $f$ in **MHz**.
> - [x] **V2X spectrum = 5.85 – 5.925 GHz**; $\lambda \approx 50.8$ mm at 5.9 GHz; quarter-wave $\approx$ 12.7 mm.
> - [x] **CAM = ETSI EN 302 637-2, 10 Hz, periodic; DENM = ETSI EN 302 637-3, event-driven.**
> - [x] **DSRC = IEEE 802.11p** (Wi-Fi variant); **C-V2X = 3GPP Rel. 14+** (cellular).
> - [x] Infotainment OS leaders: **QNX, Android Automotive OS, AGL, with CarPlay/AA as projection layers**.
> - [x] In-vehicle buses: **CAN, LIN, MOST, Automotive Ethernet (100/1000BASE-T1), A2B**.
> - [x] Always state **WGS-84 datum** and **L1 carrier 1575.42 MHz** for GPS questions.
> - [x] **GDOP ≤ 4** indicates a good satellite geometry; **PDOP** multiplies UERE to give position error.
> - [x] V2X safety latency target: **< 100 ms** (5G URLLC targets < 5 ms).
> - [x] Infotainment in modern cars is **NOT a single unit** — it is a SoC + HMI + cluster + TCU federation over Ethernet/CAN.
> - [x] Always mention the **OEM cloud** and **OTA update** capability when describing the TCU.
